System.out.println("\nTreeSet (Sorted by Age):");

sortedPeopleByAge.forEach(System.out::println);

// 4. Using a TreeMap (custom comparator for keys)

Map<Person, String> personMap = new TreeMap<>(nameComparator); // TreeMap uses

Comparator

personMap.put(new Person("Zara", 28), "Developer");

personMap.put(new Person("Yara", 32), "Manager");

personMap.put(new Person("Xavier", 25), "Analyst");

System.out.println("\nTreeMap (Sorted by Name):");

personMap.forEach((person, role) -> System.out.println(person + " -> " + role));

}

} The Collections Framework is vast, but focusing on these core interfaces and their most common implementations, their performance characteristics, and thread-safety concerns will make you well- prepared for interview questions. Be ready to discuss specific scenarios for when to use ArrayList vs. LinkedList, HashMap vs. TreeMap, and HashMap vs. ConcurrentHashMap. Got it. Let's delve into "4. Multithreading and Concurrency" in detail. This is a crucial topic for a 3- year experienced Java developer, as it directly relates to building high-performance, responsive, and scalable applications. Interviewers will often ask about thread creation, synchronization, common concurrency problems, and the java.util.concurrent package.

4
Chapter 4

Multithreading and Concurrency: Building Concurrent Applications

Multithreading allows a program to execute multiple parts of its code concurrently within a single process. Each independent part of execution is called a thread. Concurrency is the ability of different parts of a program or system to execute independently or in parallel.

4.1.Thread Basics

  • What is a Thread?
    • The smallest unit of execution within a process.1
    • A single process can have multiple threads.2
    • Threads share the same memory space (heap) of the process, but each has its own call stack and PC (Program Counter) register.3
    • Lightweight compared to processes (faster context switching).4
  • Advantages of Multithreading:
    • Responsiveness: UI applications remain responsive while long-running tasks execute in the background.
    • Resource Utilization: Efficiently utilizes CPU (especially multi-core processors) by allowing tasks to run in parallel.5
    • Faster Execution: Completes tasks quicker by dividing them among multiple threads.6
    • Better Resource Sharing: Threads within the same process can easily share data.7
  • Disadvantages of Multithreading:
    • Complexity: Harder to design, debug, and test concurrent applications due to race conditions, deadlocks, etc.
    • Overhead: Context switching between threads incurs some overhead.
    • Resource Contention: Multiple threads accessing shared resources can lead to data corruption or inconsistent states.8
  • Creating Threads: In Java, there are two primary ways to create and run threads:9

1. Extending the Thread Class:

  • Create a class that extends java.lang.Thread.
  • Override the run() method with the code that the thread will execute.
  • Create an instance of your class and call its start() method (which in turn calls run() in a new thread).
  • Limitation: Java does not support multiple inheritance, so your class cannot extend any other class if you choose this approach.
Java
class MyThread extends Thread {
  @Override
  public void run() {
    System.out.println("Thread extending Thread class is running: " +
Thread.currentThread().getName());
  }
}
public class ThreadCreationDemo {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    MyThread thread1 = new MyThread();
    thread1.start(); // Starts a new thread, calls run()
  }
}
          2. Implementing the Runnable Interface: (Recommended approach)
  • Create a class that implements java.lang.Runnable.
  • Override the run() method.
  • Create an instance of your Runnable class.
  • Create a Thread object, passing your Runnable instance to its constructor.
  • Call the start() method on the Thread object.
  • Advantage: Your class can still extend another class while implementing Runnable, offering more flexibility. This separates the task (what to run) from the thread (how to run it).
Java
class MyRunnable implements Runnable {
  @Override
  public void run() {
    System.out.println("Thread implementing Runnable is running: " +
Thread.currentThread().getName());
  }
}
public class RunnableCreationDemo {
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    MyRunnable myRunnable = new MyRunnable();
    Thread thread2 = new Thread(myRunnable, "WorkerThread-1"); // Give thread a name
    thread2.start();
  }
}
          3. Implementing Callable and Future (Java 5+):
  • Purpose: Runnable's run() method cannot return a value or throw checked exceptions. Callable solves this.
  • Callable's call() method returns a result and can throw exceptions.10
  • Future represents the result of an asynchronous computation.11 It provides methods to check if the computation is complete, wait for its completion, and retrieve the result.12
  • Requires an ExecutorService to execute Callable tasks.13
Java
import java.util.concurrent.*;
class MyCallable implements Callable<Integer> {
  private int number;
  public MyCallable(int number) {
    this.number = number;
  }
  @Override
  public Integer call() throws Exception {
    System.out.println("Callable task " + number + " running in thread: " +
Thread.currentThread().getName());
    Thread.sleep(1000); // Simulate work
    return number * number;
  }
}
public class CallableFutureDemo {
  public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException, ExecutionException {
    ExecutorService executor = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(2); // Create a thread pool
    Future<Integer> future1 = executor.submit(new MyCallable(5));
    Future<Integer> future2 = executor.submit(new MyCallable(10));
    System.out.println("Result of task 1: " + future1.get()); // get() blocks until result is available
    System.out.println("Result of task 2: " + future2.get());
    executor.shutdown(); // Shut down the executor
  }
}
  • Thread Lifecycle: A thread goes through various states from its creation to its termination.
    • NEW: A thread that has been created but not yet started (i.e., start() method not called).
    • RUNNABLE: A thread that is executing or ready to execute. It's waiting for its turn on the CPU. It includes both "Running" and "Ready" states.
    • BLOCKED: A thread that is waiting for a monitor lock (e.g., waiting to enter a synchronized block/method) to access a shared resource.14
    • WAITING: A thread that is indefinitely waiting for another thread to perform a particular action (e.g., calling Object.wait(), Thread.join(), LockSupport.park()). It will not return to the runnable state until another thread explicitly wakes it up.
    • TIMED_WAITING: A thread that is waiting for another thread to perform an action for a specified waiting time (e.g., Thread.sleep(long millis), Object.wait(long millis), Thread.join(long millis), LockSupport.parkNanos(), LockSupport.parkUntil()). It will return to the runnable state after the time expires or if woken up.
    • TERMINATED: A thread that has finished its execution (its run() method has completed or thrown an uncaught exception).

4.2.Synchronization

The process of controlling access to shared resources by multiple threads to prevent race conditions and ensure data consistency.15

  • Race Condition: Occurs when multiple threads try to access and modify a shared resource simultaneously, and the final outcome depends on the unpredictable order of execution of these threads.16
  • How to achieve Synchronization:

1. synchronized Keyword:

  • synchronized method:

▪ When applied to an instance method, it acquires the lock on the

object itself (i.e., this object) before executing the method. Only one

synchronized method of an object can be executed at a time by any

thread.

▪ When applied to a static method, it acquires the lock on the class

object (ClassName.class). Only one static synchronized method of a

class can be executed at a time by any thread.

▪ Java

▪ class Counter {

▪ private int count = 0;

▪ public synchronized void increment() { // Synchronized method

▪ count++;

▪ }

▪ public synchronized int getCount() {

▪ return count;

▪ }

▪ }

  • synchronized block:

▪ Acquires a lock on a specified object (the monitor).

▪ Allows for more fine-grained control than synchronized methods.

You can synchronize only the critical section of code.

▪ Java

▪ class SharedResource {

▪ private int data = 0;

▪ private final Object lock = new Object(); // A dedicated lock

object

▪

▪ public void increment() {

▪ synchronized (lock) { // Synchronized block on 'lock' object

▪ data++;

▪ }

▪ }

▪ public void updateData(int newValue) {

▪ synchronized (this) { // Synchronized on 'this' object

▪ this.data = newValue;

▪ }

▪ }

▪ }

  • Key points about synchronized:

▪ Re-entrant: A thread that has acquired a lock can re-enter any

synchronized block or method that is guarded by the same lock.

▪ Intrinsic Lock (Monitor Lock): Every Java object has an intrinsic lock

associated with it.

2. volatile Keyword:

  • Purpose: Ensures visibility of changes to variables across threads. It prevents compilers/CPUs from caching variables locally and ensures that any write to a volatile variable happens before any subsequent read of that variable by another thread.
  • Does NOT guarantee atomicity: volatile only ensures visibility, not that operations like i++ (which are read-modify-write) are atomic. For atomicity, you need synchronization or Atomic classes.
  • Use Case: When one thread writes to a variable and other threads only read that variable, and you need to ensure readers see the latest value. (e.g., a flag to stop a thread).
class VolatileExample {
    private volatile boolean running = true;

    public void start() {
        new Thread(() -> {
            while (running) {
                // Do work
            }
            System.out.println("Thread stopped.");
        }).start();
    }

    public void stop() {
        running = false; // Change will be immediately visible to other thread
    }
}

3. wait(), notify(), notifyAll() Methods:

  • From Object class: These methods are used for inter-thread communication and must be called from within a synchronized block/method, on the object whose intrinsic lock is held.
  • wait(): Causes the current thread to release the lock on the object and go into a WAITING or TIMED_WAITING state until another thread invokes notify() or notifyAll() on the same object, or the specified timeout expires.
  • notify(): Wakes up a single thread that is waiting on this object's monitor.17 Which thread gets woken up is non-deterministic.
  • notifyAll(): Wakes up all threads that are waiting on this object's monitor.
  • Typical Use Case: Producer-Consumer problem.
Java
class Message {
  private String msg;
  private boolean isEmpty = true;
  public synchronized String take() { // Consumer method
    while (isEmpty) {
      try {
        wait(); // Wait if no message
      } catch (InterruptedException e) {
        Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
      }
    }
    isEmpty = true;
    notifyAll(); // Notify producer that it can put another message
    return msg;
  }
  public synchronized void put(String msg) { // Producer method
    while (!isEmpty) {
      try {
        wait(); // Wait if message is not consumed
      } catch (InterruptedException e) {
        Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
      }
    }
    this.msg = msg;
    isEmpty = false;
    notifyAll(); // Notify consumer that message is available
  }
}

4.3.Thread Pools (ExecutorService)

  • Problem: Creating a new thread for every task is expensive in terms of resource consumption (memory, CPU cycles for context switching).
  • Solution: Thread pools manage a pool of worker threads. Tasks are submitted to the pool, and the pool's threads execute them.
  • ExecutorService (Interface): The main interface for executing tasks asynchronously.18
  • Executors (Utility Class): Provides static factory methods to create common ExecutorService configurations.19
    • newFixedThreadPool(int nThreads): Creates a thread pool with a fixed number of threads.20 If more tasks are submitted than threads, they wait in a queue.
    • newCachedThreadPool(): Creates a thread pool that creates new threads as needed, but reuses existing ones when they become available.21 Good for applications with many short-lived tasks.
    • newSingleThreadExecutor(): Creates a single-threaded executor.22 Ensures tasks are executed sequentially.
    • newScheduledThreadPool(int corePoolSize): Creates a thread pool that can schedule commands to run after a given delay, or to execute periodically.23
  • Lifecycle: ExecutorService needs to be explicitly shut down using shutdown() (waits for current tasks to complete) or shutdownNow() (attempts to stop all running tasks immediately).24
  • Benefits:
    • Reduced Overhead: Reuses threads, avoiding the overhead of creating/destroying threads.25
    • Resource Management: Limits the number of concurrent threads, preventing resource exhaustion.
    • Improved Responsiveness: Tasks are picked up faster from the queue.
    • Separation of Concerns: Decouples task submission from thread management.
  • Example (See CallableFutureDemo above for basic usage).
Multithreading

Thread Lifecycle

4.4.Concurrency Utilities (java.util.concurrent)

This package introduced powerful, high-level concurrency constructs that are generally preferred over low-level wait()/notify() or synchronized for complex scenarios, due to their ease of use, robustness, and performance.26

  • Lock Interface (e.g., ReentrantLock):
    • Problem with synchronized: Limited flexibility (must release lock in the same block, cannot interrupt a waiting thread, cannot try to acquire a lock without blocking, cannot acquire multiple locks in specific order).
    • ReentrantLock: An explicit lock implementation that provides more flexibility than synchronized.
      • lock(): Acquires the lock. Blocks if the lock is not available.
      • unlock(): Releases the lock. Must be called in a finally block to ensure release even if exceptions occur.
      • tryLock(): Attempts to acquire the lock without blocking.27
      • tryLock(long timeout, TimeUnit unit): Attempts to acquire the lock within a specified timeout.28
      • lockInterruptibly(): Acquires the lock unless the current thread is interrupted.29
      • Re-entrant: A thread can acquire the same lock multiple times.30
    • Condition Interface: Can be obtained from a Lock (lock.newCondition()).31 It's an alternative to wait()/notify() that provides more granular control over thread waiting and notification (e.g., multiple wait sets for a single lock).
    • ReentrantReadWriteLock: Allows multiple readers to access a resource concurrently, but only one writer at a time.32 Ideal for read-heavy workloads.
  • CountDownLatch:
    • Purpose: A synchronization aid that allows one or more threads to wait until a set of operations being performed in other threads completes.
    • Mechanism: Initialized with a count. Threads call countDown() when their task is complete.33 A thread waiting on the latch calls await() and blocks until the count reaches zero.34
    • Use Case: Start multiple threads, do some concurrent work, and then wait for all of them to finish before proceeding.
  • CyclicBarrier:
    • Purpose: A synchronization aid that allows a set of threads to wait for each other to reach a common barrier point.35
    • Mechanism: Initialized with a number of parties. Threads call await() when they reach the barrier. All threads block until all parties have called await().
    • Reusability: The barrier can be reused once all threads have passed it.36
    • Use Case: Useful for scenarios where a group of threads needs to perform a series of operations in stages, with all threads needing to complete a stage before moving to the next.
  • Semaphore:
    • Purpose: Controls access to a limited number of resources.
    • Mechanism: Initialized with a number of permits. Threads acquire a permit (acquire()) to access the resource and release it (release()) when done.37 If no permits are available, threads block.
    • Use Case: Limiting the number of concurrent database connections, limiting concurrent requests to a specific service.
  • BlockingQueue (e.g., ArrayBlockingQueue, LinkedBlockingQueue):
    • Purpose: Queues that automatically handle synchronization when adding or removing elements.
    • Blocking Operations:
      • put(E e): Waits if the queue is full.
      • take(): Waits if the queue is empty.38
    • Non-blocking variants: offer(), poll().
    • Use Case: The quintessential solution for the Producer-Consumer problem. Producers add items, consumers take items.
  • Atomic Variables (e.g., AtomicInteger, AtomicLong, AtomicBoolean, AtomicReference):
    • Purpose: Provide atomic operations on single variables without using explicit locks.
    • Mechanism: Use low-level, hardware-supported compare-and-swap (CAS) operations.
    • Benefits: More performant and scalable than using synchronized for simple variable updates.
    • Use Case: Atomic counters, flags, or references that need to be updated safely by multiple threads.

4.5.Deadlock

  • What it is: A situation where two or more threads are blocked indefinitely, each waiting for the other to release a resource that it needs.39
  • Conditions for Deadlock (Coffman Conditions - all four must be met):

1. Mutual Exclusion: At least one resource must be held in a non-sharable mode (e.g.,

a lock).

2. Hold and Wait: A thread holding at least one resource is waiting to acquire

additional resources held by other threads.40

3. No Preemption: Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a thread; they must be

released voluntarily.41

4. Circular Wait: A set of threads T1, T2, ..., Tn exists such that T1 is waiting for a

resource held by T2, T2 is waiting for a resource held by T3, and so on, with Tn

waiting for a resource held by T1.

  • Example (Classic Deadlock):
Java
public class DeadlockExample {
  private static Object lock1 = new Object();
  private static Object lock2 = new Object();
  public static void main(String[] args) {
    // Thread 1: Tries to acquire lock1, then lock2
    new Thread(() -> {
      synchronized (lock1) {
        System.out.println("Thread 1: Acquired lock1");
        try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        System.out.println("Thread 1: Waiting for lock2...");
        synchronized (lock2) { // Tries to acquire lock2 while holding lock1
          System.out.println("Thread 1: Acquired lock2");
        }
      }
    }, "Thread-1").start();
    // Thread 2: Tries to acquire lock2, then lock1
    new Thread(() -> {
      synchronized (lock2) {
        System.out.println("Thread 2: Acquired lock2");
        try { Thread.sleep(100); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        System.out.println("Thread 2: Waiting for lock1...");
        synchronized (lock1) { // Tries to acquire lock1 while holding lock2
          System.out.println("Thread 2: Acquired lock1");
        }
      }
    }, "Thread-2").start();
  }
}
  • How to Prevent Deadlock:
    • Break Circular Wait: The most common strategy. Establish a consistent ordering of resource acquisition. If Thread 1 acquires Lock A then Lock B, Thread 2 should also acquire Lock A then Lock B, never Lock B then Lock A.
    • Avoid Hold and Wait: Acquire all necessary resources at once, or release all held resources if additional ones cannot be acquired.
    • Allow Preemption: If a thread cannot acquire a resource, it releases the ones it holds and retries.
    • Avoid Mutual Exclusion (if possible): Use concurrent data structures that don't require explicit locking (e.g., ConcurrentHashMap).
  • How to Detect Deadlock:
    • Thread Dumps: Analyze thread dumps (jstack <pid>). They will show threads in BLOCKED state, often with waiting for monitor entry on specific locks and holding lock on others.
    • Monitoring Tools: Profilers or APM (Application Performance Monitoring) tools can detect deadlocks.42
  • How to Resolve Deadlock: Usually involves fixing the code to prevent one of the four conditions from being met, most commonly by ensuring a consistent lock acquisition order.

Multithreading and Concurrency are vast and complex topics. For a 3-year experienced developer, the expectation is to not just know the basics, but also to understand the trade-offs of different synchronization mechanisms, when to use the java.util.concurrent utilities, and how to identify and prevent common concurrency issues like deadlocks. Practice writing concurrent code and debugging it to solidify your understanding. Got it! Let's dive deep into "5. Spring Boot & Microservices". This is a crucial area for a Java developer with 3 years of experience, as Spring Boot is the de-facto standard for building modern Java applications, especially in the context of microservices.

5
Chapter 5

Spring Framework, Spring Boot, and Maven

This chapter covers Spring Framework, Spring Boot, and Maven. It includes 71 interview questions and scenarios.

5.1.Key Concepts

  • What is the Spring Framework?

    Spring is a lightweight Java framework for building enterprise applications. It gives you a container that manages objects called beans and connects them through dependency injection. In a banking system, Spring can manage your AccountService, TransactionRepository, and PaymentGateway objects without you manually creating them with new.
  • What are the advantages of the Spring Framework?

    Spring reduces boilerplate code, supports transactions, integrates with databases and messaging, and makes unit testing easy through dependency injection. For example, in a supply chain application, you can swap a real ShippingClient with a mock during tests because Spring injects the dependency from outside.
  • What are the main modules of the Spring Framework?

    Core modules include Spring Core (beans and DI), Spring AOP (cross-cutting concerns), Spring Data Access (JDBC, JPA), Spring MVC/Web (REST and web apps), Spring Security (authentication and authorization), and Spring Test. A banking ledger app might use Core, Data Access, and Security together.
  • Why is Spring called a lightweight framework?

    Spring does not force you to use heavy application servers or inherit from specific classes. You use plain Java objects called POJOs and configure them with annotations or XML. This makes a payment microservice small and fast to start.
  • What is Inversion of Control (IoC) in Spring?

    Inversion of Control means the Spring container controls object creation and wiring instead of your code doing it. You declare what you need, and Spring provides it. In a supply chain order service, instead of the service creating a WarehouseClient, Spring injects a ready-made one.
  • What are the ways to create a Spring Boot project?

    You can use Spring Initializr (start.spring.io) in a browser, the Spring Boot CLI, IDE plugins in IntelliJ IDEA or Eclipse, or manually create a Maven/Gradle project. In a banking team, developers usually use Spring Initializr to generate a project with spring-boot-starter-web and spring-boot-starter-data-jpa.
  • What is Spring Initializr?

    Spring Initializr is a web tool that generates a ready-to-import Spring Boot project. You pick the language, build tool, Spring Boot version, and dependencies, and it creates the project structure, pom.xml, and main class. It saves time when starting a new payment API service.
  • What is the typical structure of a Spring Boot project?

    A typical project has src/main/java for code, src/main/resources for config and static files, src/test/java for tests, a main class with @SpringBootApplication, and application.properties or application.yml. In a supply chain app, packages are often controller, service, repository, model, and config.
  • What is the role of the main class in a Spring Boot application?

    The main class contains the main method and is annotated with @SpringBootApplication. Calling SpringApplication.run() starts the Spring context, triggers auto-configuration, begins component scanning, and starts the embedded server. It is the single entry point for a banking microservice.
  • What is the default embedded server in Spring Boot?

    The default embedded server is Tomcat. Spring Boot also supports Jetty and Undertow. You can switch by excluding Tomcat and adding the Jetty starter. This is useful in high-throughput supply chain APIs where Jetty's threading model may perform better.
  • What is a Spring Bean?

    A Spring Bean is any Java object that is created, configured, and managed by the Spring IoC container. Beans are usually annotated with @Component, @Service, @Repository, or @Controller. In a banking app, a FundTransferService is a bean that Spring injects wherever it is needed.
  • What are Spring bean scopes?

    The common scopes are singleton (one instance per context), prototype (new instance on every request), request (one per HTTP request), and session (one per HTTP session). Singleton is the default. A supply chain CurrencyConverter might be singleton, while a per-request OrderBuilder could be prototype.
  • What is the default bean scope in Spring?

    The default scope is singleton. Spring creates exactly one instance of the bean and shares it across the entire application context. This is efficient for stateless services, such as a banking InterestCalculationService.
  • Are singleton Spring beans thread-safe?

    No, singleton beans are not automatically thread-safe just because Spring creates one instance. If the bean holds mutable shared state, multiple threads can corrupt it. You should make beans stateless or use synchronization. A banking AccountBalanceCache must use thread-safe collections or locks.
  • What is the Spring bean lifecycle?

    Spring creates the bean, injects dependencies, runs initialization callbacks like @PostConstruct, makes it available for use, and finally calls destruction callbacks like @PreDestroy when the context closes. In a supply chain app, you might use @PostConstruct to load region codes into memory on startup.
  • What is the difference between @Component, @Service, @Repository, and @Controller?

    All four create Spring beans, but they signal different layers. @Component is generic. @Service marks business logic. @Repository marks data access and enables exception translation. @Controller marks web controllers. A banking app uses @Service for PaymentService and @Repository for TransactionRepository.
  • What is the difference between @Bean and @Component?

    @Component is used on classes that Spring scans automatically. @Bean is used on methods inside a @Configuration class to create beans manually, often for third-party objects you cannot annotate. For example, you might define a @Bean for a banking SDK client that is not under your control.
  • What is Dependency Injection (DI)?

    Dependency Injection means an object receives its dependencies from outside instead of creating them itself. Spring provides the dependencies at runtime. In a supply chain app, OrderService does not create InventoryClient; Spring injects it, so the service is easier to test and change.
  • What are the types of dependency injection in Spring?

    Spring supports constructor injection, setter injection, and field injection. Constructor injection is recommended because it makes dependencies required and immutable. Field injection is easy but hides dependencies. In a banking LoanApprovalService, constructor injection ensures all required services are present at startup.
  • Why is constructor injection preferred in Spring?

    Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit, allows immutable objects, makes unit testing easy, and prevents a bean from being created with missing dependencies. It also avoids the circular dependency problems that constructor injection itself detects at startup.
  • What is @Autowired?

    @Autowired tells Spring to inject a matching bean automatically by type. It can be placed on a constructor, setter, or field. If multiple beans match, you can resolve the conflict with @Qualifier or @Primary. In a banking app, @Autowired injects a NotificationSender into a PaymentService.
  • What is the difference between @Qualifier and @Primary?

    @Primary marks one bean as the default choice when multiple beans of the same type exist. @Qualifier is used at the injection point to pick a specific bean by name. If you have both EmailNotificationSender and SmsNotificationSender, you can mark one @Primary or use @Qualifier("email").
  • What is a circular dependency in Spring, and how do you resolve it?

    A circular dependency happens when Bean A depends on Bean B and Bean B depends on Bean A. Spring fails to start with constructor injection. You can fix it by redesigning the classes, using setter or field injection, or using @Lazy. In a banking app, separating a PaymentService and AuditService via events can break the cycle.
  • What is the difference between IoC and DI?

    Inversion of Control is the principle where the framework manages object creation and flow. Dependency Injection is one way to implement IoC by supplying dependencies from outside. So DI is the mechanism, and IoC is the broader design goal behind it.
  • What is the difference between Spring and Spring Boot?

    Spring is the core framework that provides IoC, transactions, data access, and web support. Spring Boot is built on top of Spring and removes boilerplate by providing auto- configuration, embedded servers, and starter dependencies. In simple words, Spring gives you the engine, and Spring Boot gives you a ready-to-drive car.
  • Why do we use Spring Boot over Spring Framework?

    Spring Boot reduces setup time with auto-configuration, starter POMs, embedded servers, and production-ready monitoring through Actuator. It lets a banking team create a working REST API in minutes instead of spending hours on XML configuration.
  • What are Spring Boot starter dependencies?

    Starters are convenient dependency bundles. spring-boot-starter-web includes Spring MVC, Tomcat, and Jackson. spring-boot-starter-data-jpa includes Hibernate, Spring Data JPA, and a connection pool. For a supply chain app, adding one starter pulls in everything needed for a layer.
  • Does Spring Boot replace Spring Framework?

    No, Spring Boot does not replace Spring. It makes Spring easier to use by providing defaults and conventions. Underneath, Spring Boot still uses Spring Core, Spring MVC, Spring Data, and other Spring modules.
  • When would you choose plain Spring over Spring Boot?

    You might choose plain Spring when you need full control over every configuration detail, when you deploy to an external application server as a WAR, or when integrating with a legacy codebase that already has custom Spring setup. Most modern banking microservices, however, use Spring Boot.
  • What is Spring Boot auto-configuration?

    Auto-configuration automatically sets up Spring beans based on the dependencies present in your classpath. If spring-boot-starter-data-jpa is present, Spring Boot configures a DataSource and EntityManagerFactory for you. You can override defaults in application.properties. In a banking app, this means the database connection is ready without manual bean definitions.
  • How does Spring Boot auto-configuration work internally?

    Spring Boot reads META-INF/spring.factories or META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfigu ration.imports to find auto-configuration classes. Each class is decorated with @Conditional annotations, such as @ConditionalOnClass and @ConditionalOnMissingBean, so beans are only created when appropriate. If your supply chain app adds Redis, the Redis auto-configuration kicks in.
  • What is @SpringBootApplication?

    @SpringBootApplication is a convenience annotation that combines @Configuration, @EnableAutoConfiguration, and @ComponentScan. It marks the main class, enables auto-configuration, and tells Spring where to look for components. Without it, you would need three separate annotations.
  • What is the ApplicationContext in Spring?

    The ApplicationContext is the Spring IoC container. It holds bean definitions, creates beans, wires dependencies, and provides services like event publishing and internationalization. When a banking application starts, SpringApplication.run() creates the ApplicationContext.
  • What is the difference between ApplicationContext and BeanFactory?

    BeanFactory is the basic container with lazy initialization and minimal features. ApplicationContext extends it with eager initialization, event propagation, AOP support, and easier integration. Modern Spring Boot apps always use ApplicationContext.
  • What happens when you run SpringApplication.run()?

    Spring Boot creates the application context, loads property sources, runs ApplicationRunner and CommandLineRunner beans, starts the embedded web server, and enables auto-configuration. By the end, your banking payment API is listening on its configured port.
  • What is component scanning?

    Component scanning is how Spring automatically discovers beans in specified packages. @ComponentScan tells Spring to look for @Component, @Service, @Repository, and @Controller annotations. In a supply chain app, Spring scans com.company.supplychain.* and registers all discovered beans.
  • How do you disable a specific auto-configuration class?

    You can use @SpringBootApplication(exclude = DataSourceAutoConfiguration.class) or set spring.autoconfigure.exclude in application.properties. This is useful when a banking app needs to turn off default datasource setup because it uses a custom connection factory.
  • What are @Conditional annotations in Spring Boot?

    Conditional annotations control when beans are created. @ConditionalOnClass creates a bean only if a class is on the classpath. @ConditionalOnProperty creates it only if a property matches. @ConditionalOnMissingBean creates it only if the bean is not already defined. These make auto-configuration smart and safe.
  • How can you view all beans loaded by Spring Boot?

    Inject the ApplicationContext and call getBeanDefinitionNames(). Spring Boot Actuator also provides the /beans endpoint. This helps during debugging when a banking app fails to start due to a missing bean.
  • What is Maven and what problem does it solve?

    Maven is a build automation and dependency management tool for Java. It standardizes project structure, downloads required libraries from repositories, and provides lifecycle phases like compile, test, package, and deploy. In a banking project, Maven ensures every developer uses the same Spring Boot version.
  • What is a pom.xml file?

    pom.xml is the Project Object Model file. It contains project metadata, dependencies, plugins, build settings, and parent POM references. It is the recipe Maven follows to build the project. A supply chain microservice pom.xml lists starters like spring-boot-starter-web and spring-boot-starter-test.
  • What are the Maven lifecycle phases?

    The default lifecycle includes validate, compile, test, package, verify, install, and deploy. The clean lifecycle removes generated files. Running mvn clean install cleans old output, compiles code, runs tests, packages a JAR, and installs it in the local repository.
  • What is the difference between mvn clean and mvn install?

    mvn clean deletes the target directory from previous builds. mvn install compiles, tests, packages, and copies the artifact to your local Maven repository. In a banking CI pipeline, you usually run mvn clean install to get a fresh, tested artifact.
  • What is the difference between compile-scope and test-scope dependencies?

    Compile-scope dependencies are needed to compile and run the application, such as spring-boot-starter-web. Test-scope dependencies are only needed during testing, such as JUnit and Mockito. They are not included in the final production artifact.
  • What is the Maven local repository?

    The local repository is a folder on your machine, usually ~/.m2/repository, where Maven stores downloaded jars. When a teammate in a banking team adds a new dependency, Maven first checks this local cache before downloading from the internet or a corporate Nexus.
  • What is a Maven repository manager like Nexus or Artifactory?

    A repository manager is a private server that stores internal artifacts and proxies public repositories. Banks use Nexus or Artifactory to share private libraries across teams, control versions, and improve build speed by caching external dependencies.
  • What is dependencyManagement in Maven?

    dependencyManagement declares dependency versions centrally without adding them to the project directly. Child modules or dependencies can then omit the version. Spring Boot's parent POM uses this to align all starter versions automatically.
  • How do you exclude a transitive dependency in Maven?

    You add an block inside the tag. For example, if a banking library pulls in an old version of Jackson that conflicts with Spring Boot, you exclude it so Spring Boot's managed version is used.
  • How do Maven profiles help in a Spring Boot project?

    Profiles let you define environment-specific configuration within the same POM. You can have dev, test, and prod profiles that activate different databases or logging levels. A supply chain team might activate a profile for local H2 testing versus production Oracle.
  • What is spring-boot-starter-parent?

    It is a parent POM that provides default Java version, default Spring Boot dependency versions, plugin configurations, and resource filtering. Inheriting from it simplifies your pom.xml because you do not need to specify versions for every starter.
  • How do you run a Spring Boot application using Maven?

    You can use mvn spring-boot:run to start the application directly from Maven, or build with mvn clean package and then run the generated JAR with java -jar target/app.jar. In a banking deployment pipeline, the JAR is built once and deployed to multiple environments. Scenario-Based Questions
  • Your team needs to build a new real-time fund-transfer microservice. How would you set it up using Spring Boot?

    I would use Spring Initializr with Java, Maven, and dependencies spring-boot- starter-web, spring-boot-starter-data-jpa, spring-boot-starter- security, and spring-boot-starter-validation. I would structure packages into controller, service, repository, model, and config. The main class would use @SpringBootApplication to enable auto-configuration and component scanning. This gives a working REST service with database and security support in minutes.
  • The fund-transfer service needs a TransactionService and a TransactionRepository. How do you wire them using Spring DI?

    I annotate TransactionService with @Service and TransactionRepository with @Repository. I inject the repository into the service using constructor injection and @Autowired. Spring creates both beans and wires them automatically. This keeps TransactionService testable because I can replace the real repository with a Mockito mock during unit tests.
  • A developer accidentally used field injection everywhere. What risk does this create, and how do you fix it?

    Field injection hides dependencies, making them hard to unit test without reflection, and it can mask circular dependencies until runtime. The fix is to move dependencies to a constructor and use constructor injection. Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit, supports immutable fields, and fails fast at startup if a dependency is missing.
  • During load testing, the TransactionService singleton shows race conditions. What could be wrong?

    Singleton beans are not thread-safe by default. If TransactionService holds mutable shared state, such as a counter or a non-thread-safe map, concurrent fund-transfer requests can corrupt it. The fix is to keep the bean stateless or use thread-safe data structures like ConcurrentHashMap. Better still, move per-request data into method parameters or use the prototype scope if the bean must hold request state.
  • The service must support multiple environments: dev, UAT, and prod. How do you manage this in Spring Boot?

    I use Spring profiles. I create application-dev.yml, application-uat.yml, and application-prod.yml. Each profile defines its own database URL, logging level, and feature flags. I activate the profile using spring.profiles.active in the environment or as a JVM argument. In prod, I also secure Actuator endpoints and disable dev tools.
  • A warehouse order API receives a request, calls inventory to reserve stock, then calls payment to charge the customer. How do you design this in Spring Boot?

    I create an OrderController annotated with @RestController, an OrderService annotated with @Service, and InventoryClient and PaymentClient beans. The controller maps the incoming request, the service orchestrates the steps, and the clients handle external calls. I inject all clients into the service via constructor injection so the flow is clear and testable.
  • The order service sometimes fails because the inventory and payment clients are coupled. How do you break the circular dependency?

    If InventoryClient depends on PaymentClient and vice versa, Spring fails at startup with constructor injection. I redesign the code so both clients are independent and the OrderService coordinates them. Alternatively, I can use @Lazy or setter injection as a short- term fix, but the real solution is better separation of concerns.
  • The team adds both Tomcat and Jetty starters by mistake. How does Spring Boot decide which server to use?

    Spring Boot checks classpath order and defaults to the first embedded server it finds, usually Tomcat if it appears first. To switch to Jetty intentionally, I would exclude the Tomcat starter from spring-boot-starter-web and add the Jetty starter explicitly. This controls which server runs the supply-chain API.
  • You need to expose order health status to the operations team. What Spring Boot feature helps?

    Spring Boot Actuator provides production-ready endpoints such as /actuator/health. I add the spring-boot-starter-actuator dependency and configure security so only authorized users can access sensitive endpoints. I can also create a custom health indicator that checks connectivity to the warehouse database and external payment gateway.
  • The application does not start, and the error points to a missing bean. How do you investigate?

    I check that the missing bean class has a Spring stereotype annotation such as @Component, @Service, or @Repository, and that its package is under the component scan path defined by @SpringBootApplication. I also verify that any third-party bean is declared with @Bean in a @Configuration class. If needed, I list all loaded beans using ApplicationContext.getBeanDefinitionNames() or the Actuator /beans endpoint.
  • A banking build passes locally but fails in the CI pipeline with dependency conflicts. How do you fix this with Maven?

    I run mvn dependency:tree to inspect transitive dependencies and identify the conflicting versions. I then use the dependencyManagement section or the tag to force the correct version. If the team uses a repository manager like Nexus or Artifactory, I verify that the CI environment points to the same repository as local machines.
  • The CI pipeline needs to build a JAR once and deploy it to dev, UAT, and prod. How do you configure Maven for this?

    I run mvn clean package to produce a single JAR. Environment-specific configuration is externalized into profile-specific property files or environment variables, not baked into the JAR. Maven profiles can help run environment-specific tests, but the same artifact is promoted across environments to guarantee consistency.
  • A developer checked in a change that breaks the Maven build for everyone. What Maven command helps isolate the problem?

    I use mvn clean install -X to run the build in debug mode and capture detailed logs. I also use mvn dependency:tree to spot new conflicts and mvn test to identify failing tests. Once I find the problematic commit, I either revert it or work with the developer to fix the POM and dependency versions.
  • During peak season, the order API must handle ten times the normal traffic. Which Spring Boot settings matter?

    I tune the embedded server thread pool, configure database connection pool limits in application.yml, add caching with Redis or Caffeine, and consider scaling horizontally behind a load balancer. I also monitor Actuator metrics for response time and error rate. If the bottleneck is database connections, I increase the HikariCP pool size within safe limits.
  • The order API must switch from an in-memory database to Oracle in production without code changes. How is this possible?

    I externalize the datasource configuration into application.yml using placeholders or Spring profiles. In dev, the profile uses H2; in prod, the profile uses Oracle URL, username, password, and driver. Auto-configuration detects the database driver on the classpath and creates the datasource. No code changes are needed because the same JPA code works against both databases.
  • A new starter was added, but Spring Boot now auto-configures a bean the team does not want. How do you disable it?

    I disable the unwanted auto-configuration class using @SpringBootApplication(exclude = {UnwantedAutoConfiguration.class}) or by listing it in spring.autoconfigure.exclude in application.yml. This is useful when a starter pulls in a feature, such as a default datasource or security filter, that conflicts with custom configuration.
  • You have three classes — PaymentController, PaymentService, and PaymentRepository. Annotate them.

    PaymentController gets @RestController because it exposes REST endpoints. PaymentService gets @Service because it holds business logic. PaymentRepository gets @Repository because it accesses the database. I inject PaymentService into the controller and PaymentRepository into the service, all via constructor injection, so Spring manages the object graph.
  • Two beans of type NotificationSender exist — one for email, one for SMS. How do you inject the correct one?

    I use @Qualifier("emailNotificationSender") at the injection point to pick the email bean, or @Qualifier("smsNotificationSender") for SMS. Alternatively, I mark one bean @Primary if it should be the default. This avoids ambiguity when Spring finds multiple candidates of the same type.
  • A bean must load exchange rates once at startup. Which lifecycle callback do you use?

    I use @PostConstruct on a method inside the bean. Spring calls this method after dependency injection is complete but before the bean is used. I can call an external FX-rate service or load rates from a file. The bean stays a singleton and uses the loaded rates for all subsequent payment calculations.
  • A build artifact is 200 MB because it includes unnecessary dependencies. What do you check?

    I run mvn dependency:analyze and mvn dependency:tree to find unused or oversized transitive dependencies. I remove unused direct dependencies and add for large transitive libraries that the project does not need. I also verify that test-scoped dependencies are not accidentally marked as compile scope.
6
Chapter 6

Spring Boot Web MVC, JPA, Validation, Exception Handling, Lambda, and Streams

This chapter covers Spring Boot Web MVC, JPA, Validation, Exception Handling, Lambda, and Streams. It includes 76 interview questions and scenarios.

6.1.Key Concepts

  • What is Spring MVC?

    Spring MVC is a web framework within Spring that follows the Model-View-Controller pattern. The Model holds data, the View renders the user interface, and the Controller handles requests and business flow. The core idea is separation of concerns: controllers deal with HTTP, services with logic, and views with presentation. In a banking web portal, the controller receives a login request, the service validates credentials, the model carries account details, and the view renders the dashboard. For REST APIs, the "view" is usually JSON produced by message converters.
  • What is the role of DispatcherServlet in Spring MVC?

    DispatcherServlet is the front controller that receives every HTTP request and acts as the single entry point. Its job is to delegate: it uses handler mappings to find the right controller, handler adapters to invoke it, and view resolvers or message converters to produce the response. It also applies interceptors and centralizes exception handling. In a supply-chain order system, every request to /orders/* first hits the DispatcherServlet, which then routes it to OrderController. Without it, each controller would need its own routing and common concern logic.
  • Describe the lifecycle of a Spring MVC request.

    The lifecycle begins when the client sends an HTTP request, which the embedded Tomcat receives and forwards to DispatcherServlet. The servlet consults HandlerMapping to find the controller method matching the URL and HTTP method. It then invokes the method through a HandlerAdapter. The controller may call services, repositories, or external clients. It returns either a ModelAndView or a response body. For REST, HttpMessageConverters serialize the return object to JSON. For traditional MVC, a ViewResolver maps the logical view name to a JSP or Thymeleaf template. Finally, DispatcherServlet sends the rendered response back to the client. Interceptors run before and after the controller if configured.
  • What is the difference between Spring MVC and Spring Boot Web MVC?

    Spring MVC is the web framework that handles HTTP requests, controllers, view resolution, and interceptors. It requires manual configuration such as registering DispatcherServlet in web.xml or a Java initializer, configuring view resolvers, and setting up component scanning. Spring Boot Web MVC is Spring MVC with opinionated auto- configuration. When you add spring-boot-starter-web, Spring Boot registers DispatcherServlet, configures default JSON message converters, starts embedded Tomcat, and enables component scanning automatically. In a traditional banking project using plain Spring MVC, you write more XML or Java config. In a modern Spring Boot banking microservice, you write the controller and the framework wires the rest.
  • What is Tomcat's role in a Spring Boot application?

    Tomcat is the embedded servlet container bundled with Spring Boot Web. It listens for HTTP requests and passes them to DispatcherServlet. In production, a banking API runs as a JAR, and the embedded Tomcat starts automatically when the application launches.
  • What is the WebApplicationContext?

    WebApplicationContext is a Spring container specialized for web applications. It holds web-specific beans such as controllers, view resolvers, and interceptors. DispatcherServlet creates or inherits this context to resolve and invoke controller beans when requests arrive.
  • What is a DTO, and why do you use it?

    A DTO, or Data Transfer Object, is a plain Java object used to carry data between application layers or between the backend and external clients. Its purpose is to decouple the internal domain model from the API contract. In a banking app, instead of exposing a BankAccount JPA entity with database IDs, audit fields, and internal flags, you expose an AccountDto with only account number, balance, and holder name. DTOs prevent over-fetching of data, protect sensitive fields, reduce payload size, and make versioning easier because you can change the entity schema without changing what clients receive.
  • What is the difference between an entity and a DTO?

    An entity maps to a database table and includes persistence annotations. A DTO is a plain object used for API requests and responses. For example, a supply-chain ShipmentEntity might have database IDs and audit fields, while a ShipmentDto contains only tracking number, origin, and destination.
  • What is the role of a controller in Spring Web MVC?

    A controller receives HTTP requests, extracts input data, delegates processing to services, and returns a response or view name. In a banking transfer API, a TransferController maps a POST request, validates the DTO, calls TransferService, and returns the transaction reference.
  • What is the difference between @Controller and @RestController?

    @Controller is a Spring stereotype for web controllers that return view names by default. If you want to return raw data like JSON from a @Controller method, you must add @ResponseBody to that method. @RestController is a convenience annotation that combines @Controller and @ResponseBody at the class level, so every method automatically writes its return value to the response body. For example, a banking AccountController using @RestController can return an AccountDto object, and Jackson automatically converts it to JSON without adding @ResponseBody on every method.
  • What are @RequestMapping, @GetMapping, @PostMapping, @PutMapping, @PatchMapping, and @DeleteMapping?

    These annotations map URLs to controller methods. @RequestMapping is generic and can specify any HTTP method. The others are specialized shortcuts: @GetMapping for read, @PostMapping for create, @PutMapping for full update, @PatchMapping for partial update, and @DeleteMapping for delete.
  • What is the difference between @PathVariable and @RequestParam?

    @PathVariable captures values from the URL path, like /accounts/{id}. @RequestParam reads query parameters or form data, like /accounts?type=savings. In a supply-chain API, /shipments/123 uses @PathVariable for the ID, while /shipments? status=DELIVERED uses @RequestParam.
  • What is @RequestBody used for?

    @RequestBody tells Spring to read the HTTP request body and deserialize it into a Java object. The conversion is done by HttpMessageConverters, usually Jackson for JSON. For example, in a banking payment API, a POST body { "fromAccount": "12345", "toAccount": "67890", "amount": 1000 } is automatically mapped to a PaymentRequestDto object. The DTO can then be validated and passed to the service. If the JSON is malformed, Spring responds with a 400 Bad Request before the controller method executes.
  • What is @ResponseBody and how does it work?

    @ResponseBody tells Spring to write the controller method's return value directly into the HTTP response body rather than interpreting it as a view name. Spring uses HttpMessageConverters, such as Jackson, to serialize the object to JSON or XML. For example, a method returning an AccountDto object becomes a JSON response like { "accountNumber": "...", "balance": 5000 }. When you use @RestController, every method inherits @ResponseBody automatically, which is why REST controllers do not need it on each method.
  • What are the PUT, PATCH, and DELETE HTTP methods used for?

    PUT replaces an entire resource, so the client must send the full updated object. PATCH applies a partial update, so only the fields that change are sent. DELETE removes a resource or performs a logical delete. In a banking API, PUT might fully replace a customer profile, PATCH might update only the phone number or address, and DELETE might deactivate an account while keeping the record in the database. Using the right method keeps the API predictable and RESTful.
  • When should you use PUT versus PATCH?

    Use PUT when the client is responsible for sending the complete resource, even for fields that do not change. Use PATCH when only a subset of fields needs to change, making the payload smaller and the operation more focused. For a supply-chain product catalog, PUT might replace the entire product object including name, category, price, and stock. PATCH might send only { "price": 29.99, "stock": 150 } to update just those two fields. PATCH reduces network overhead and avoids accidental overwrites of unchanged data.
  • What is the role of the service layer in a Spring application?

    The service layer sits between controllers and repositories and owns the business logic of the application. It coordinates multiple repositories, external APIs, and domain rules, and it defines transaction boundaries. A PaymentService in a banking app validates transfer limits, calls a fraud-check service, debits the source account, credits the destination account, and logs the transaction. By placing these steps in one service method annotated with @Transactional, you ensure that all database changes succeed or fail together. Keeping business logic in the service layer also makes controllers thin, repositories focused on data access, and the application easier to test and maintain.
  • How do you keep controllers thin and services fat?

    Controllers should only handle HTTP mapping, input validation, and response building. All business rules, calculations, and data orchestration belong in the service layer. This separation makes the supply-chain order controller easy to read and the OrderService easy to unit test.
  • What is a repository in Spring Data JPA?

    A repository is an interface that Spring Data JPA implements automatically at runtime. It provides CRUD methods, query derivation, paging, sorting, and custom queries. A banking TransactionRepository extends JpaRepository gives you save, findById, and custom finder methods for free.
  • What is the difference between CrudRepository, PagingAndSortingRepository, and JpaRepository?

    CrudRepository provides basic CRUD operations. PagingAndSortingRepository adds pagination and sorting. JpaRepository extends both and adds JPA-specific methods like flush, saveAll, and batch delete. Most Spring Boot projects use JpaRepository for its richer feature set.
  • How does Spring Data JPA create query methods automatically?

    Spring Data JPA uses a query derivation mechanism. When you declare a method like findByAccountNumber or findByStatusAndCreatedDateAfter in a repository interface, it parses the method name into a JPQL query. Keywords like And, Or, GreaterThan, Like, OrderBy, and IgnoreCase control the query shape. For complex joins or queries that cannot be expressed by naming convention, you annotate the method with @Query and write JPQL or native SQL. For example, in a banking app, List findByAccountNumberAndTransactionDateBetween(String accountNumber, LocalDate start, LocalDate end) automatically generates a query that fetches transactions for an account within a date range.
  • What is the purpose of pagination in Spring Data JPA?

    Pagination breaks large result sets into smaller pages to save memory and improve response time. You pass a Pageable object with page number, size, and sort. A supply-chain shipment search returning millions of rows can return Page with only twenty records per request.
  • What is the difference between FetchType.EAGER and FetchType.LAZY?

    EAGER loads related entities immediately with the parent. LAZY loads them only when accessed. LAZY is preferred for performance. In a banking app, eager loading all transactions with every account query can cause the N+1 problem, so lazy loading is usually better.
  • What is the N+1 SELECT problem, and how do you avoid it?

    The N+1 problem happens when one query fetches N parent records, and then N additional queries are executed to load related child records. For example, if you fetch 100 supply- chain orders and then lazily access order.getItems() for each order, Hibernate performs one query for the orders plus 100 queries for the items. To avoid this, you can use JOIN FETCH in a @Query annotation to load the parent and children in a single query. Alternatively, you can use entity graphs, batch fetching through hibernate.default_batch_fetch_size, or FetchType.EAGER carefully where it makes sense. The goal is to load the required data with the minimum number of queries.
  • What is input validation in Spring Boot?

    Input validation ensures that request data meets business rules before processing. Spring Boot supports Bean Validation using annotations like @NotNull, @Size, @Min, @Max, @Email, and @Pattern. A banking fund-transfer DTO might require @NotBlank account numbers and @Positive amount.
  • What is the difference between @Valid and @Validated?

    @Valid comes from the Bean Validation standard and is used on request bodies and nested objects. @Validated is a Spring extension that supports group validation and can be used at the class or method level. For a supply-chain API, @Valid validates a posted order DTO, while @Validated can validate method parameters by group.
  • How do you create a custom validation annotation in Spring Boot?

    You define an annotation, a ConstraintValidator implementation, and link them using @Constraint. For example, a custom @AccountNumber validator checks that a banking account number matches a specific length and digit pattern. You then place the annotation on a DTO field.
  • What is @ControllerAdvice and @RestControllerAdvice?

    @ControllerAdvice is a special annotation that marks a class as a global advisor for multiple controllers. Methods inside it can use @ExceptionHandler, @InitBinder, and @ModelAttribute to apply across controllers. @RestControllerAdvice is a convenience variant that combines @ControllerAdvice and @ResponseBody, so the return values are written directly to the response body as JSON. In a banking app, a single @RestControllerAdvice class can catch MethodArgumentNotValidException for validation failures, AccountNotFoundException for missing accounts, and generic exceptions, returning a consistent error response structure for every endpoint.
  • What is @ExceptionHandler?

    @ExceptionHandler marks a method that handles a specific exception type thrown by controller methods. When that exception occurs, Spring routes control to the handler method instead of letting the exception bubble up as a 500 error. The method typically returns a ResponseEntity with an HTTP status and an error body. For example, a method annotated with @ExceptionHandler(AccountNotFoundException.class) in a @RestControllerAdvice class can return ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND).body(errorResponse). This keeps error handling centralized and user-friendly.
  • How do you handle validation errors in Spring Boot?

    When validation fails, Spring throws MethodArgumentNotValidException. A global @ExceptionHandler can catch it, read the BindingResult to extract field-level errors, and return a structured error response. The response typically contains the HTTP status, a timestamp, the request path, and a list of errors with field names and messages. For example, in a supply-chain API, the client receives {"errors": [{"field":"quantity","message":"must be at least 1"}]} with a 400 status. This avoids raw stack traces and gives clients actionable feedback.
  • How do you transform or customize API responses in Spring Web MVC?

    You can customize responses in several ways. Use ResponseEntity to set the exact HTTP status, headers, and body. Use DTOs to shape the data sent to the client. Use Jackson annotations like @JsonProperty to rename fields, @JsonIgnore to exclude fields, and @JsonFormat to format dates. Use message converters to support multiple formats like JSON and XML. For example, in a banking API, after creating an account, you return ResponseEntity.created(location).body(new AccountDto(account)) with a 201 status and a Location header pointing to the new resource.
  • What is ResponseEntity?

    ResponseEntity is a Spring class that represents the complete HTTP response, including status code, headers, and body. It lets a controller decide exactly what the client receives. For example, ResponseEntity.created(URI.create("/orders/123")).body(orderDto) returns 201 Created, adds a Location header pointing to the new resource, and includes the created order data in the body. For errors, you can return ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.BAD_REQUEST).body(errorResponse). This is much more flexible than simply returning a DTO.
  • How do you use Jackson annotations to shape JSON responses?

    Jackson annotations let you control serialization without changing the service logic. Use @JsonProperty("customer_name") to change the JSON key name. Use @JsonIgnore to prevent a field from appearing in the response, such as an internal database ID. Use @JsonFormat(pattern = "yyyy-MM-dd", shape = JsonFormat.Shape.STRING) to format dates. Use @JsonInclude(JsonInclude.Include.NON_NULL) to omit null fields and reduce payload size. In a banking API, you might hide the internal accountId and expose only accountNumber, while formatting transaction timestamps to a readable string.
  • What is a lambda expression in Java 8?

    A lambda expression is a concise way to represent an anonymous function that implements a functional interface. It has parameters, an arrow, and a body. For example, list.forEach(item -> System.out.println(item)) replaces a verbose anonymous inner class.
  • What is a functional interface?

    A functional interface has exactly one abstract method. Java 8 provides built-in ones like Predicate, Function, Consumer, and Supplier. The @FunctionalInterface annotation marks them and prevents adding extra abstract methods. A supply-chain Predicate can test if an order is urgent.
  • What are intermediate and terminal operations in the Stream API?

    Intermediate operations transform a stream into another stream and are lazy, meaning they do not execute until a terminal operation is called. Examples include filter, map, flatMap, sorted, distinct, and limit. Terminal operations consume the stream and produce a result, such as collect, forEach, reduce, count, and findFirst. For example, in a banking report, transactions.stream().filter(t -> t.getType().equals("DEBIT")).map(Transaction::getAmount).collect(C ollectors.toList()) does nothing until collect runs. Then the entire pipeline executes efficiently in one pass.
  • What is the difference between map and flatMap in streams?

    map transforms each element into exactly one new element, preserving the one-to-one relationship. flatMap transforms each element into a stream of zero or more elements, and then flattens all those nested streams into a single stream. For example, if you have List and each Order contains List, orders.stream().map(Order::getItems) gives you a stream of lists. orders.stream().flatMap(order -> order.getItems().stream()) gives you a single stream of all OrderItem objects. This is useful for nested data in supply-chain order processing.
  • How do you filter and collect data using streams?

    Use filter with a predicate to keep matching elements, then collect with a collector like Collectors.toList(). For example, transactions.stream().filter(t -> t.getAmount() > 10000).collect(Collectors.toList()) finds high-value banking transactions.
  • What is the purpose of reduce in streams?

    reduce combines stream elements into a single value using an accumulator. For example, amounts.stream().reduce(0, Integer::sum) adds all transaction amounts. It is useful for aggregations like totals, averages, and maximums.
  • How do you group or partition data with streams?

    Use Collectors.groupingBy to group elements by a key, such as grouping transactions by status. Use Collectors.partitioningBy to split into two groups based on a predicate. A supply-chain app might group shipments by destination country for a summary report.
  • What is Optional, and why is it useful?

    Optional is a wrapper introduced in Java 8 that represents a value that may or may not be present. It forces the caller to explicitly handle the absence of a value instead of risking a NullPointerException. For example, a repository method can return Optional instead of an Account that might be null. The service can then call .orElseThrow(() -> new AccountNotFoundException("Account not found")) or .ifPresent(account -> process(account)). This makes the code safer and more readable, especially in banking lookups where missing data is a real possibility.
  • What are method references, and when do you use them?

    Method references are shorthand for lambdas that simply call an existing method. For example, names.stream().map(String::toUpperCase) is shorter than names.stream().map(s -> s.toUpperCase()). Use them when a lambda only calls one method without extra logic.
  • What is lazy evaluation in streams?

    Lazy evaluation means intermediate operations are not executed until a terminal operation is called. This allows the stream to optimize by fusing operations and avoiding unnecessary work. For a large supply-chain dataset, filtering before mapping reduces the number of transformations.
  • What are peek, limit, and skip used for in streams?

    peek inspects elements for debugging without changing them. limit truncates the stream to a maximum size. skip discards the first N elements. In a banking transaction history, skip(10).limit(20) implements simple pagination.
  • How do you find duplicates in a list using streams?

    You can use Collectors.groupingBy and then filter groups with more than one element, or use a Set to track seen items. For example, list.stream().filter(i -> Collections.frequency(list, i) > 1).collect(Collectors.toSet()) finds duplicate account numbers, though it is not the most efficient for huge lists.
  • What is a HandlerInterceptor, and when do you use it?

    A HandlerInterceptor is a Spring MVC mechanism that intercepts HTTP requests within the Spring lifecycle. It has three methods: preHandle runs before the controller and can block the request by returning false; postHandle runs after the controller but before the view is rendered; afterCompletion runs after the entire request is complete and is useful for cleanup or final logging. In a banking API, you can use an interceptor to log every incoming request, add a correlation ID to the response, or check a custom security header before the controller executes. It is registered globally through WebMvcConfigurer.addInterceptors.
  • How do you serve static resources in Spring Boot Web MVC?

    Spring Boot serves static files from /static, /public, /resources, or /META- INF/resources by default. You can also register custom resource handlers with WebMvcConfigurer.addResourceHandlers. In a supply-chain portal, PDF invoices might be served from the /static/invoices folder.
  • What is content negotiation in Spring MVC?

    Content negotiation lets a controller produce different response formats, such as JSON or XML, based on the client's Accept header. Spring uses ContentNegotiatingViewResolver and message converters. A banking partner requesting XML can receive the same data as another partner requesting JSON.
  • What is the difference between a filter and an interceptor?

    A servlet filter operates at the servlet container level, before the request reaches DispatcherServlet. It is part of the Java Servlet API and can wrap any request or response, making it suitable for low-level tasks like request logging, CORS handling, compression, or security headers. An interceptor operates inside the Spring MVC lifecycle, after DispatcherServlet has received the request. It has access to the controller handler, the model, and the view, making it suitable for Spring-aware concerns like adding model attributes, authentication checks, or post-processing responses. In a banking app, a filter might add security headers to every response, while an interceptor might check a per-endpoint permission annotation.
  • How do you write a unit test for a Spring MVC controller?

    I annotate the test class with @WebMvcTest(TransferController.class) so Spring loads only the web layer components needed for that controller. I inject MockMvc to simulate HTTP requests without starting a real server. I use @MockBean to replace the service layer with a Mockito mock. In the test, I configure the mock with when(transferService.transfer(...)).thenReturn(transactionReferenc e), then call mockMvc.perform(post("/transfers").contentType(MediaType.APPLICATI ON_JSON).content(jsonBody)). I verify the HTTP status, response body, and sometimes the interaction with the mock using verify(transferService, times(1)).transfer(...). This tests the controller in isolation and runs quickly because no database or external service is started.
  • What is @WebMvcTest used for?

    @WebMvcTest is a test slice that loads only Spring MVC components for a specific controller, without starting the full application context or database. It makes controller tests fast. In a supply-chain project, you test OrderController without loading OrderRepository. Scenario-Based Questions
  • A banking portal receives an HTTP POST to /accounts/{id}/transfer. Walk me through the Spring MVC flow.

    The flow starts when the client sends the POST request with JSON body and account ID. Tomcat accepts the connection and forwards the request to DispatcherServlet. DispatcherServlet consults handler mappings to find AccountController.transferFunds. It invokes the method through a handler adapter. The @PathVariable extracts the account ID and @RequestBody deserializes the JSON into a TransferRequestDto. The controller calls TransferService, which validates balances, calls the fraud engine, and processes the transfer inside a transaction. The service returns a transaction reference. The controller builds a ResponseEntity with 201 Created and a Location header. Jackson converts the DTO to JSON, and DispatcherServlet sends the response back to the client.
  • A supply-chain client wants to update only the delivery date of a shipment. Which HTTP method and Spring annotation do you use?

    Use PATCH with @PatchMapping("/shipments/{shipmentId}"). PATCH is designed for partial updates, meaning the client sends only the fields that need to change. The controller maps the shipment ID with @PathVariable and the request body with @RequestBody. The DTO contains only deliveryDate and perhaps a version field for optimistic locking. The service loads the existing shipment, applies the change, and saves. Because PUT would require the client to send the entire shipment object, PATCH is the cleaner choice here. The response typically returns 200 OK with the updated shipment DTO.
  • A bank needs to expose account details but must hide the internal database ID and the customer's full address. How do you handle this?

    The safest approach is to introduce a dedicated AccountDto that contains only the fields the API contract allows, such as account number, account type, available balance, and a masked phone number. In the service layer, I map the Account entity to the DTO using a mapper like MapStruct or a manual mapping method. The controller returns the DTO, never the entity. This prevents internal fields like the database ID, full address, and audit metadata from leaking to clients. It also decouples the database schema from the API contract, so future schema changes do not break external consumers.
  • A supply-chain API accepts a huge CSV upload of orders. How do you validate each row before saving?

    I process the CSV in a streaming manner to avoid loading everything into memory. For each row, I map it to an OrderImportDto and run Bean Validation. Fields like product code use @NotBlank, quantity uses @Positive, and delivery date uses @Future or a custom date validator. I use a Validator instance directly or validate programmatically. I collect all failures in a list with row number, field, and message. Valid rows are grouped and processed in batches using saveAll. At the end, the API returns a summary such as total rows, successful imports, failed rows, and the detailed error list. This ensures no partial or invalid data enters the database.
  • A banking API returns 500 Internal Server Error for every invalid request. How do you improve the client experience?

    The problem is that unhandled exceptions default to 500, which hides the real cause. I add a @RestControllerAdvice class with specific @ExceptionHandler methods. For MethodArgumentNotValidException, I return 400 Bad Request with a list of field errors and messages. For AccountNotFoundException, I return 404 Not Found with the account identifier. For business rule violations, I return 409 Conflict or 422 Unprocessable Entity. For unexpected errors, I return 500 but log the stack trace internally and expose only a generic reference ID. The response body always follows the same structure: status, timestamp, path, message, and optional details. This gives clients clear, actionable feedback instead of a generic server error.
  • A supply-chain search endpoint returns 100,000 shipment records, and the UI freezes. How do you fix it?

    Implement pagination with Pageable in the repository method. Return Page with only the requested page, size, and sort. Add default limits and validate that clients cannot request an excessively large page size. This reduces memory usage and improves response time.
  • A banking fund-transfer DTO has many fields, and validation rules differ for retail versus corporate customers. How do you manage this?

    Use validation groups. Define interfaces like RetailValidation and CorporateValidation. Annotate DTO fields with @NotNull(groups = CorporateValidation.class) and use @Validated(CorporateValidation.class) on the controller method. This applies different rules based on the customer type without duplicating DTOs.
  • A supply-chain order response needs to show total amount, currency, and item count in a custom shape that does not match the entity. How do you build it?

    I create a dedicated OrderSummaryDto with fields like orderNumber, totalAmount, currency, and itemCount. In the service layer, I fetch the Order entity along with its line items, either eagerly or via a fetch join. I then compute the total by summing unitPrice * quantity across items, determine the currency from the order header, and count the number of line items. I map these computed values into the DTO. The controller returns OrderSummaryDto instead of the raw entity. This gives the client a clean, purpose-built shape without exposing internal persistence details or forcing the client to do its own calculations.
  • A banking transaction report must group transactions by type and show the sum for each type. How do you implement this with streams?

    I use transactions.stream().collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Transaction::g etType, Collectors.summarizingDouble(Transaction::getAmount))). This returns a Map where the key is the transaction type and the value contains sum, count, average, min, and max. From there, I iterate over the map entries and build a list of TransactionSummaryDto objects with fields for type, totalAmount, transactionCount, and averageAmount. The service returns this list, and the controller serializes it to JSON. This approach keeps the grouping and aggregation concise without writing explicit loops.
  • A supply-chain API needs to return the top five highest-value orders placed in the last 30 days. Write the stream pipeline.

    orders.stream().filter(o -> o.getOrderDate().isAfter(LocalDate.now().minusDays(30))).sorted(Co mparator.comparing(Order::getTotalAmount).reversed()).limit(5).col lect(Collectors.toList()). This filters by date, sorts descending by amount, limits to five, and collects the result.
