Kubernetes creates new pods, waits for readiness probes to succeed, gradually shifts traffic, and terminates old pods with a 30-second grace period. No requests are dropped because the application handles SIGTERM gracefully and Kubernetes only removes pods after the readiness endpoint marks new pods healthy.
  • A container needs to access host services such as a local database. What networking options are available?

    On Docker Desktop, host.docker.internal resolves to the host from inside a container. On Linux, this requires extrafficonfiguration or using the host network mode with --network host, which removes container network isolation. Another option is to connect the container and the host service to a common bridge network if the host service also runs in a container. For production, services should not depend on the host network. For local development, host.docker.internal is the most portable approach when supported. I prefer to run dependencies in containers and use Compose networks rather than relying on the host. Real-time example: a developer runs a local Postgres on the laptop and wants a containerized service to connect to it. On Docker Desktop, I configure the service connection string to use host.docker.internal:5432 and the application connects successfully. On a Linux CI runner, I instead add a Postgres service container to the same network as the application.
19
Chapter 19

Kubernetes for Backend and DevOps Engineers

This chapter covers Kubernetes for Backend and DevOps Engineers. It includes 44 interview questions and scenarios.

19.1.Key Concepts

  • What is Kubernetes, and why is it used?

    Kubernetes is an open-source container orchestration platform that automates the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. It provides a declarative API for de ning desired application state and continuously works to maintain that state. Kubernetes is used because it abstracts away infrastructure complexity, enables self-healing through pod rescheduling, supports rolling updates and rollbacks, and provides service discovery, load balancing, storage orchestration, and secrets management. For backend developers, it standardizes how applications run across environments. For DevOps engineers, it provides the control plane for running production workloads at scale.
  • What is a Kubernetes cluster, and what are its main components?

    A Kubernetes cluster consists of a control plane and worker nodes. The control plane includes the API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager, and optionally the cloud controller manager. The API server is the entry point for all commands and state changes. etcd is the distributed key-value store for cluster state. The scheduler assigns pods to nodes based on resources and constraints. The controller manager runs controllers that regulate the state of Deployments, ReplicaSets, Services, and other objects. Worker nodes run the kubelet, kube-proxy, and container runtime. The kubelet ensures containers run as specified in pod definitions, while kube-proxy handles network routing and load balancing for Services.
  • What is a Pod in Kubernetes?

    A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. It represents one or more containers that share the same network namespace, IP address, storage volumes, and lifecycle. Containers within a Pod can communicate via localhost. Pods are ephemeral by design; their IP addresses change when they are recreated. Higher-level controllers such as Deployments, ReplicaSets, StatefulSets, and DaemonSets manage Pods to provide scaling, self-healing, and ordered lifecycle semantics. A backend developer typically does not create Pods directly but instead defines Deployments that create them.
  • What is the difference between a Pod and a container?

    A container is a single process runtime instance with its own isolated lesystem and resources. A Pod is a Kubernetes abstraction that wraps one or more containers and provides shared networking and storage. A Pod gives Kubernetes a higher-level unit to schedule, manage, and scale. Containers inside a Pod share the same network interface and can mount the same volumes. A Pod also carries metadata, labels, and lifecycle policies that a bare container does not. In practice, most application Pods contain one main container and sometimes sidecar containers for logging, proxying, or configuration.
  • What is a ReplicaSet?

    A ReplicaSet is a Kubernetes controller that ensures a specified number of identical Pod replicas are running at any time. It uses a label selector to identify which Pods it manages and creates or deletes Pods to match the desired replica count. ReplicaSets provide self-healing by replacing failed Pods and horizontal scaling by adjusting replica count. However, users usually work with Deployments rather than ReplicaSets directly because Deployments manage ReplicaSets and provide rolling update and rollback capabilities.
  • What is a Deployment in Kubernetes?

    A Deployment is a higher-level controller that manages a ReplicaSet and provides declarative updates for Pods. It is used for stateless applications. A Deployment allows you to define the desired state, including the container image, replica count, strategy, and update parameters. It supports rolling updates that replace old Pods gradually, and it keeps a revision history so you can roll back to a previous version. Deployments are the most common workload object for backend microservices because they handle availability during updates automatically.
  • What is the difference between a Deployment and a StatefulSet?

    A Deployment manages stateless Pods that are interchangeable. It supports arbitrary scaling and does not guarantee pod identity or ordering. A StatefulSet is designed for stateful applications that require stable network identity, stable persistent storage, and ordered deployment, scaling, and termination. StatefulSet Pods have predictable names with ordinal indexes such as web-0, web-1, and web-2. Each Pod can have its own PersistentVolumeClaim via a volumeClaimTemplate. Deployments are ideal for REST services, while StatefulSets are used for databases, message queues, and distributed stateful systems.
  • What is a DaemonSet?

    A DaemonSet ensures that one Pod runs on each selected node in the cluster. It is used for node-level infrastructure services such as log collectors, monitoring agents, node exporters, and network proxies. When nodes are added, DaemonSet pods are automatically scheduled on them. When nodes are removed, the associated Pods are garbage collected. Unlike Deployments, DaemonSets are not scaled by replica count because their scale is determined by the number of nodes.
  • What is a Kubernetes Namespace?

    A Namespace is a logical partition within a Kubernetes cluster that isolates groups of resources. Namespaces allow multiple teams or applications to share a cluster without name collisions. They are often used to separate environments such as development, staging, and production, or to isolate microservices by team. Resource quotas, limits, RBAC policies, and network policies can be applied per namespace. Objects such as Pods, Services, Con gMaps, and Secrets exist within a namespace, but cluster-wide resources such as Nodes and PersistentVolumes are not namespaced.
  • What is a Kubernetes Service, and why is it needed?

    A Service exposes a set of Pods as a stable network endpoint. Because Pod IPs are ephemeral, clients cannot rely on individual Pod addresses. A Service provides a stable IP, DNS name, and load balancing across matching Pods. Services use label selectors to determine which Pods are members. The main service types are ClusterIP for internal access, NodePort for exposing on a static port on each node, LoadBalancer for cloud provider load balancers, and ExternalName for mapping to an external DNS name. Services are essential for microservices communication within a cluster.
  • How does a Kubernetes Service route traffic to Pods?

    A Service selects Pods using label selectors and maintains a list of endpoints through the Endpoints or EndpointSlice API. kube-proxy runs on every node and watches Service and EndpointSlice objects. It configures network rules, typically using iptables or IPVS, so that traffic to the Service IP is translated and load-balanced across the backend Pod IPs. ClusterDNS provides DNS records such as my-service.my-namespace.svc.cluster.local so clients can resolve Services by name.
  • What is a headless Service?

    A headless Service is a Service with clusterIP: None. It does not allocate a virtual IP or perform load balancing. Instead, DNS lookups return the individual Pod IPs directly. Headless Services are used when clients need to discover and communicate with specific Pods, such as in StatefulSets where each Pod has a stable hostname. For example, a headless Service for a StatefulSet allows DNS records like web-0.my-service.default.svc.cluster.local to resolve to the specific Pod.
  • What are the different Service types?

    The four main Service types are ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, and ExternalName. ClusterIP is the default and exposes the Service only within the cluster. NodePort exposes the Service on a static port of every node, making it reachable from outside the cluster through the node's IP. LoadBalancer provisions an external load balancer from a cloud provider and forwards traffic to NodePorts or directly to Pods. ExternalName maps the Service DNS name to an external DNS name and is used for integrating external services into cluster DNS.
  • What is a PersistentVolume and a PersistentVolumeClaim?

    A PersistentVolume is a cluster-level storage resource representing a piece of network- attached or cloud-backed storage. A PersistentVolumeClaim is a request for storage by a Pod or workload. Pods use PVCs to mount storage without knowing the underlying storage provider. Kubernetes binds a PVC to a compatible PV based on access mode, storage class, and capacity. With dynamic provisioning, a StorageClass automatically creates a PV when a PVC is submitted. PVCs are namespaced, while PVs are cluster-scoped.
  • What are StorageClasses and dynamic provisioning?

    A StorageClass defines the type of storage and the provisioner that creates it. Examples include AWS EBS, GCP Persistent Disk, Azure Disk, and NFS provisioners. When a PVC references a StorageClass, the provisioner automatically creates a matching PV and binds it to the PVC. Dynamic provisioning removes the need for administrators to manually create volumes. It also allows reclaim policies such as Delete or Retain to be defined, determining what happens to the PV when the PVC is deleted.
  • What are the access modes for PersistentVolumes?

    PersistentVolumes support three access modes. ReadWriteOnce means the volume can be mounted as read-write by a single node. ReadOnlyMany means many nodes can mount the volume read- only. ReadWriteMany means many nodes can mount the volume read-write simultaneously. The supported access modes depend on the underlying storage technology. Block storage such as EBS supports RWO, while shared lesystems such as NFS or EFS support RWX. Choosing the correct access mode is important for workloads that run on multiple nodes.
  • What is Minikube, and when is it used?

    Minikube is a tool that creates a single-node Kubernetes cluster on a local machine for development and learning. It runs the control plane and worker components on the same node, either as a virtual machine, a container, or directly on the host. Minikube is used when developers need a local Kubernetes environment to test manifests, deploy applications, and learn Kubernetes concepts without needing a cloud cluster. It supports addons for ingress, DNS, storage, and dashboard. For production workloads, a managed or self-hosted multi-node cluster is required.
  • What is a Con gMap, and how is it used?

    A Con gMap is a Kubernetes object for storing non-sensitive configuration data as key-value pairs. It decouples configuration from container images, allowing the same image to be used across environments. Con gMaps can be mounted as files in a volume, exposed as environment variables, or read by an application through the Kubernetes API. For backend developers, Con gMaps are useful for application settings such as feature ags, URLs, and logging levels. Sensitive data should not be stored in Con gMaps; use Secrets instead.
  • What is a Secret in Kubernetes?

    A Secret is a Kubernetes object for storing sensitive data such as passwords, tokens, and certificates. Secret values are base64-encoded but are not encrypted by default unless etcd encryption at rest is enabled. Secrets can be mounted as files or injected as environment variables. Best practice is to mount them as files so multiple values can be updated without recreating the Pod, and so secrets do not appear in process listings. For production, integration with external secret managers such as Vault or cloud secret stores is recommended.
  • What is a liveness probe, a readiness probe, and a startup probe?

    A liveness probe determines whether the container is running. If it fails, Kubernetes restarts the container. A readiness probe determines whether the container is ready to accept traffic. If it fails, the Pod is removed from Service endpoints. A startup probe is used for slow-starting containers; while it is running, liveness and readiness checks are disabled. Once the startup probe succeeds, the other probes begin. Probes can be HTTP GET, TCP socket, gRPC, or exec commands. Proper probe configuration is critical for availability and graceful rollouts.
  • What is a rolling update in Kubernetes?

    A rolling update is a strategy for replacing old Pods with new ones gradually without downtime. A Deployment creates a new ReplicaSet for the updated version and scales it up while scaling down the old ReplicaSet. The parameters maxSurge and maxUnavailable control how many extra Pods can exist and how many old Pods can be unavailable during the update. Kubernetes waits for readiness probes on new Pods before terminating old Pods, ensuring the Service always has healthy backends. Rolling updates are the default strategy for Deployments and are essential for zero-downtime deployments.
  • How do you roll back a Deployment in Kubernetes?

    Kubernetes keeps a revision history for Deployments. To roll back, you run kubectl rollout undo deployment/ to revert to the previous revision. You can also specify a revision number with --to-revision. The Deployment then scales down the current Pods and scales up the Pods from the selected revision. Rollbacks are useful when a new release introduces errors or performance problems. It is important to set a reasonable revisionHistoryLimit so old ReplicaSets are retained for rollback.
  • What is a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler?

    The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler automatically adjusts the number of Pod replicas in a Deployment, ReplicaSet, or StatefulSet based on observed metrics such as CPU utilization, memory utilization, or custom metrics. It queries the Metrics API and compares current usage to target thresholds. If usage is above the target, it scales up. If usage is below the target for a sustained period, it scales down. HPA improves resource efficiency and handles traffic spikes automatically. It requires the Metrics Server or a custom metrics adapter to be installed in the cluster.
  • What is RBAC in Kubernetes?

    Role-Based Access Control in Kubernetes restricts access to cluster resources based on the roles of users, groups, or service accounts. RBAC uses Roles and ClusterRoles to define permissions, and RoleBindings and ClusterRoleBindings to assign those permissions to subjects. A Role applies within a namespace, while a ClusterRole applies cluster-wide. RBAC is essential for securing multi- tenant clusters and ensuring that applications and users have only the permissions they need. For example, a backend service account should only be allowed to read Con gMaps in its own namespace, not delete nodes.
  • A Pod is stuck in CrashLoopBackOff. What is your troubleshooting work ow?

    I start with kubectl get pods -n to confirm the status and restart count. Then I run kubectl describe pod to inspect events, which often reveal the cause such as OOMKilled, liveness probe failure, or image pull errors. I read logs with kubectl logs for the current container and kubectl logs --previous for the last crashed instance. If logs are not enough, I use kubectl exec -it -- /bin/sh to inspect the running state, or temporarily add a debug init container. Common causes include missing configuration, invalid startup command, dependency not ready, or insufficient resources. Real-time example: a Spring Boot Pod restarts continuously with CrashLoopBackOff. kubectl describe shows Back-off restarting failed container. kubectl logs --previous reveals Caused by: java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: Could not resolve placeholder 'DB_HOST'. The Con gMap app-config was not mounted because of a typo in configMapRef. I correct the Con gMap name, apply the manifest, and the Pod starts.
  • A Pod is in ImagePullBackOff. How do you resolve it?

    ImagePullBackOff means Kubernetes cannot pull the container image. I check kubectl describe pod for the exact error, which may say manifest unknown, unauthorized, or pull access denied. I verify the image name and tag with docker pull from a local machine. If the registry is private, I create a docker-registry Secret and reference it in the Pod spec via imagePullSecrets, or attach it to the ServiceAccount. If the image tag is mutable such as latest, I switch to an immutable tag such as a Git commit SHA. I also check network connectivity and registry quotas. Real-time example: a CI pipeline pushes to a private registry but the production Pod fails with ImagePullBackOff. The Pod spec references imagePullSecrets: regcred, but the secret is in a different namespace. I create the same secret in the application namespace with kubectl create secret docker-registry -n prod regcred ..., and the image pulls successfully.
  • A Pod is OOMKilled. What do you do?

    I confirm the OOM status with kubectl describe pod or kubectl get pod -o yaml. I look at memory usage with kubectl top pod or Prometheus metrics. If the application legitimately needs more memory, I increase the resources.limits.memory and the corresponding JVM or runtime heap setting. If memory usage grows continuously, I investigate for a memory leak using heap dumps, pro ling, or application metrics. I always set both requests and limits, with limits higher than requests but not excessively high. Real-time example: a Java service is OOMKilled during batch processing. The container limit is 512 MiB but the JVM uses -Xmx512m, leaving no room for native memory. I change the JVM argument to -Xmx384m and raise the container limit to 768 MiB. The batch completes without restarts.
  • A Service is not reachable even though the Pod is running. How do you debug it?

    I check the Service selector against Pod labels with kubectl get svc and kubectl get pods --show-labels. I verify that the Service has endpoints with kubectl get endpoints . If endpoints are empty, the selector does not match or the Pods are not ready. I check that the Service targetPort matches the container containerPort. I test DNS resolution with nslookup ..svc.cluster.local from another Pod. I also inspect NetworkPolicies that may block traffic, and Ingress or load balancer configuration if the Service is exposed externally. Real-time example: a frontend service cannot reach the backend. kubectl get endpoints backend returns no endpoints. The backend Pod label is app: backend-api but the Service selector is app: backend. I correct the selector, the endpoints populate, and the frontend resolves the backend DNS.
  • A Deployment rolling update is stuck and old Pods are not terminating. Why?

    This usually happens because new Pods are not becoming ready, so Kubernetes cannot terminate old Pods without violating maxUnavailable. I check new Pod status and logs with kubectl get pods and kubectl logs. I look for readiness probe failures, application startup errors, or missing dependencies. If the readiness probe is misconfigured with too short an initialDelaySeconds, new Pods are marked not ready before the application nishes starting. I tune the probe or add a startup probe for slow-starting applications. I also check if maxUnavailable is set to zero and maxSurge is zero, which prevents any replacement. Real-time example: a Spring Boot service update hangs because the readiness probe hits /actuator/health immediately, but the context takes 45 seconds to start. I add a startup probe with initialDelaySeconds: 10 and periodSeconds: 10 over 10 attempts, and the rolling update completes.
  • A StatefulSet Pod loses its data after rescheduling to another node. Why?

    This happens when the StatefulSet does not use a PersistentVolumeClaim template or when the PVC is not bound to a storage class with the correct reclaim policy. In a StatefulSet, each Pod must have its own stable PVC created by volumeClaimTemplates. If the Pod uses an emptyDir volume, data is lost when the Pod is deleted or moved. I verify the StatefulSet manifest has volumeClaimTemplates, check that the PVCs exist and are bound, and ensure the StorageClass supports the required access mode. Real-time example: a MongoDB replica set uses emptyDir for data in a StatefulSet. When a node is drained, the Pod reschedules and restarts with empty data, causing replica set inconsistency. I switch to a volumeClaimTemplate backed by a gp3 StorageClass with ReadWriteOnce, and rescheduling preserves the data.
  • You need to run a log collector on every node. Which workload object do you use?

    I use a DaemonSet. A DaemonSet ensures one Pod runs on each selected node, and automatically adds Pods when nodes join the cluster. Log collectors, monitoring agents like Prometheus node-exporter, and CNI plugins are common DaemonSet workloads. I create the DaemonSet with a toleration if it needs to run on control plane or tainted nodes, and mount the host log directory as a volume. For backend observability, this ensures all application logs are collected regardless of where Pods run. Real-time example: the platform team wants Fluent Bit to ship logs from every node to Elasticsearch. I deploy Fluent Bit as a DaemonSet with a hostPath volume for /var/log/containers, tolerations for master nodes, and a resource limit to prevent it from starving workloads. Every node now ships container logs.
  • A Pod needs to wait for a database to be ready before starting. How do you handle it?

    I use an init container that checks database readiness and exits successfully only when the database accepts connections. For example, an init container running a Postgres client can run pg_isready -h db -U user in a loop. Init containers run sequentially before the main container starts. Alternatively, the application can implement retry logic with exponential backoff at startup. The cleanest Kubernetes-native approach is init containers, while application retries provide resilience outside Kubernetes. Real-time example: a Spring Boot application starts before Postgres is ready and crashes repeatedly. I add an init container with image: postgres:15 and command ['sh', '- c', 'until pg_isready -h postgres -U app; do sleep 2; done']. The main container starts only after Postgres accepts connections, eliminating CrashLoopBackOff.
  • A Namespace needs resource limits so one team cannot consume all cluster capacity. How do you enforce it?

    I create a ResourceQuota in the namespace that caps CPU, memory, number of Pods, Services, PersistentVolumeClaims, and other objects. I also create LimitRange objects to set default resource requests and limits for containers that do not specify them. ResourceQuota prevents one namespace from starving others, while LimitRange ensures predictable resource profiles. I apply these policies at namespace creation time and document them for developers. Real-time example: the staging namespace runs a load test that consumes all CPU, causing production alerts. I add a ResourceQuota limiting staging to 40 CPU cores, 80 GiB memory, and 50 Pods. I also add a LimitRange with default memory request 256 Mi and limit 512 Mi. The staging load test is throttled, and production stability is restored.
  • A backend developer wants to expose a service on a custom domain with HTTPS. What Kubernetes resources are involved?

    I use an Ingress resource to route HTTP and HTTPS traffic from the custom domain to a ClusterIP Service. The Ingress references a TLS Secret that contains the certificate and private key. An Ingress controller such as NGINX, Trae k, or AWS Load Balancer Controller must be installed in the cluster to satisfy Ingress resources. Optionally, cert-manager can automatically provision and renew certificates from Let's Encrypt. The Service must select the backend Pods, and the Pods must respond to health checks. For backend developers, the key is that the Service is ClusterIP while Ingress handles external access and TLS termination. Real-time example: a Spring Boot API needs to be available at api.shop.com with HTTPS. I create a ClusterIP Service, an Ingress with host: api.shop.com, tls referencing a Secret api-shop-tls, and install NGINX Ingress Controller with a public IP. cert- manager provisions the certificate, and traffic reaches the backend Pods.
  • A Pod cannot mount a PersistentVolumeClaim. What do you check?

    I check the PVC status with kubectl get pvc. If it is Pending, there may be no matching PV or the StorageClass provisioner failed. I read the events with kubectl describe pvc and kubectl describe pod. Common causes include no available PV for static provisioning, the StorageClass does not exist, the access mode is unsupported by the storage backend, or the requested storage exceeds available capacity. For dynamic provisioning, I check that the CSI driver or cloud provider integration is working and that credentials are correct. Real-time example: a Postgres Pod stays in ContainerCreating. kubectl describe pod shows Unable to attach or mount volumes: unmounted volumes=[data] and persistentvolumeclaim postgres- data not found. The PVC name in the Pod spec was postgres-data-claim but the actual PVC was postgres-data. I correct the volume name and the Pod mounts successfully.
  • A Kubernetes secret value needs to be updated. Will running Pods pick it up automatically?

    No, Pods do not automatically pick up Secret changes when the Secret is mounted as environment variables. Environment variables are set at Pod creation time. If the Secret is mounted as a volume, Kubernetes updates the mounted files asynchronously, usually within a few minutes, but the application may not reload them unless it watches the file. For immediate updates, I restart the Pods using kubectl rollout restart deployment/. For dynamic secret rotation, I integrate with an external secret operator or Vault agent that injects updated secrets and signals the application. Real-time example: a database password is rotated. The Secret is updated, but the application still uses the old password from environment variables. I perform a rolling restart of the Deployment, and the new Pods start with the updated Secret value.
  • A multi-tenant cluster needs network isolation between namespaces. How do you implement it?

    I create NetworkPolicy resources with a default-deny rule for ingress and egress in each namespace, then add explicit allow rules for required traffic. Default-deny prevents all Pods in the namespace from receiving or initiating traffic unless allowed. I allow ingress from specific namespaces or Pods using label selectors, and egress to DNS, dependencies, and external endpoints as needed. This ensures teams cannot accidentally access each other's services. Calico, Cilium, and other CNI plugins support NetworkPolicy enforcement. Real-time example: two teams share a cluster. I apply a default- deny ingress NetworkPolicy to each namespace and allow only the frontend namespace to reach the backend API on port 8080. Cross-namespace traffic from unrelated workloads is blocked, reducing the blast radius of a compromised service.
  • A Deployment needs zero-downtime updates with a minimum number of Pods available. How do you configure it?

    I configure the Deployment strategy with RollingUpdate, set maxUnavailable to zero or a small number, and set maxSurge to allow extra Pods during the update. I also define a PodDisruptionBudget to ensure a minimum number of replicas remain available during voluntary disruptions such as node drains and updates. Readiness probes must be accurate so Kubernetes only routes traffic to Pods that are truly ready. For example, maxUnavailable: 0 and maxSurge: 1 ensures old Pods stay running until a new Pod is ready. Real-time example: a payment service runs with three replicas. I set maxUnavailable: 0, maxSurge: 1, and a PodDisruptionBudget with minAvailable: 2. During a rollout, at least two Pods are always ready, and traffic is never served to a Pod before its readiness probe succeeds.
  • A backend application needs different configurations per environment. How do you manage it in Kubernetes?

    I use Con gMaps and Secrets for environment-specific configuration, keeping the container image identical across environments. Each namespace represents an environment such as dev, staging, or prod. I mount Con gMaps as files or inject values as environment variables. For complex configuration, I use Helm or Kustomize to generate manifests per environment. Sensitive values go into Secrets, and non-sensitive values go into Con gMaps. I avoid embedding environment differences in the image so that the same artifact can be promoted through the pipeline. Real-time example: the order service needs different database URLs for staging and production. I create a Con gMap order- service-config in each namespace with the appropriate DB_URL. The Deployment references the Con gMap as environment variables. The same Docker image is deployed to both environments, and only the Con gMap differs.
  • A Pod is stuck in Pending and never schedules. What do you check?

    I run kubectl describe pod to read the events, which usually explain why scheduling failed. Common causes include insufficient CPU or memory on nodes, node selectors that match no nodes, taints preventing scheduling, missing PersistentVolume for a PVC, or anti-a nity rules that cannot be satis ed. I check node resources with kubectl describe nodes and kubectl top nodes. If the issue is resources, I scale the cluster or reduce the Pod's resource requests. If it is scheduling constraints, I review node labels, taints, tolerations, and a nity rules. Real-time example: a new service requests cpu: 4 and memory: 16Gi, but the cluster nodes only have 2 CPU and 8 GiB each. The Pod stays Pending. I reduce the request to cpu: 500m and memory: 1Gi based on actual pro ling, and the Pod schedules immediately.
  • A Pod logs sensitive information. How do you prevent it from being collected?

    I update the application to mask or remove sensitive fields before logging. I configure the logging framework to redact known patterns such as tokens, passwords, and credit card numbers. I also restrict access to log storage and ensure log retention policies comply with security requirements. If sensitive data is already in logs, I rotate and purge old logs and investigate how it was introduced. From a DevOps perspective, I avoid mounting logs to host paths where multiple users can read them, and I use centralized logging with role-based access. Real-time example: an API logs request headers including Authorization: Bearer . I update the logging interceptor to replace the Authorization value with [REDACTED] before writing to stdout. Existing logs in Elasticsearch are purged according to retention policy, and access to log indices is restricted to the security team.
  • A Kubernetes cluster needs to scale Pods based on custom business metrics such as queue depth. How do you set it up?

    I use the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler with custom metrics. The application exposes a custom metric through a metrics endpoint or through Prometheus Adapter. I configure the HPA to scale on that metric instead of CPU. For example, an order processing service can scale based on the number of messages in a Kafka queue. The Prometheus Adapter translates Prometheus queries into the custom.metrics.k8s.io API. The HPA then adjusts replicas to keep the metric near a target value. I also set min and max replicas to control scaling bounds. Real-time example: an email notification service is backed by a Redis queue. I expose queue length as a Prometheus metric, configure Prometheus Adapter to serve it as custom.metrics.k8s.io|queues|email_queue_length, and create an HPA targeting 100 messages per replica with minReplicas: 2 and maxReplicas: 20. When a marketing campaign oods the queue, the service scales automatically.
  • A StatefulSet requires ordered scaling and stable storage. How do you design the manifest?

    I use a StatefulSet with a serviceName referencing a headless Service, volumeClaimTemplates for stable storage, and a podManagementPolicy of OrderedReady for ordered startup and shutdown. Each Pod gets a stable hostname like kafka- 0.kafka.default.svc.cluster.local. The volumeClaimTemplate creates one PVC per Pod with a stable name, so data persists across rescheduling. I set an update strategy of RollingUpdate with an appropriate partition if I need controlled rollout. For backend services that require identity such as database replicas or Kafka brokers, this design is essential. Real-time example: a Kafka cluster runs as a StatefulSet with three replicas. Each broker has a headless Service kafka, a StatefulSet kafka, and a volumeClaimTemplate using the fast-ssd StorageClass. Broker 0 always has hostname kafka-0.kafka and mounts the same PV, even after node failure. This stability is required for Kafka broker identity and persistent log storage.
  • A junior backend developer asks why they cannot directly create a Pod in production. How do you explain?

    I explain that a bare Pod is not self-healing, not scalable, and not rolling-updatable. If the node fails or the Pod crashes, Kubernetes will not recreate it automatically. A Deployment provides replicas, health monitoring, rolling updates, and rollback. A Pod also has an ephemeral IP that changes on restart, while a Service provides stable networking. In production, developers should define Deployments or StatefulSets, not raw Pods. I also mention that organizational policy may enforce this through admission controllers or RBAC. Real-time example: a developer creates a standalone Pod for a batch job. The node is drained for maintenance, the Pod is evicted, and the job never restarts. I show them how to use a Job or CronJob for batch work, and a Deployment for long-running services, so Kubernetes manages lifecycle and recovery.
20
Chapter 20

Google Kubernetes Engine, Stateful Workloads, Microservices DNS, Ingress, Con gMap, Secrets, and CI/CD

This chapter covers Google Kubernetes Engine, Stateful Workloads, Microservices DNS, Ingress, Con gMap, Secrets, and CI/CD. It includes 44 interview questions and scenarios.

20.1.Key Concepts

  • What is Google Kubernetes Engine, and why would a team choose it?

    Google Kubernetes Engine is a managed Kubernetes service provided by Google Cloud. It handles the control plane, including the API server, scheduler, etcd, and controller manager, while users manage the worker nodes and workloads. Teams choose GKE because it reduces operational burden compared to self-managed Kubernetes, integrates with Google Cloud services such as Cloud Storage, Cloud SQL, and IAM, and offers features like automatic node repair, cluster autoscaling, and managed upgrades. For backend and DevOps engineers, GKE provides a production-ready Kubernetes platform with built-in networking, security, and observability integrations.
  • What is the difference between GKE Standard and GKE Autopilot?

    GKE Standard gives you full control over node pools, machine types, scaling, and cluster configuration. You pay for the nodes and manage capacity planning. GKE Autopilot abstracts node management away; Google automatically provisions, scales, and secures nodes based on the Pods you deploy. Autopilot charges per Pod rather than per provisioned node and enforces best practices such as resource requests, restricted security settings, and allowed container images. Standard is better for workloads that need custom node configuration, GPUs, or privileged containers. Autopilot is better for teams that want to focus on applications rather than infrastructure.
  • How do you create a GKE cluster using gcloud CLI?

    First, set the project and region with gcloud config set project and gcloud config set compute/region . Then create the cluster with gcloud container clusters create --zone --num-nodes . For Autopilot, use gcloud container clusters create-auto --region . After creation, fetch credentials with gcloud container clusters get-credentials --zone so kubectl can interact with the cluster. You can also specify machine type, network, subnet, release channel, and node labels as ags.
  • What are common gcloud commands used to manage GKE clusters?

    Common commands include gcloud container clusters create to create a cluster, gcloud container clusters list to list clusters, gcloud container clusters describe to inspect details, gcloud container clusters get-credentials to configure kubectl, gcloud container clusters update to modify cluster settings, and gcloud container clusters delete to remove a cluster. For node pools, gcloud container node- pools list, create, delete, and update are used. For workloads, you usually switch to kubectl commands after fetching credentials. These commands are essential for DevOps automation and local cluster management.
  • What is Workload Identity in GKE, and why is it important?

    Workload Identity allows a Kubernetes service account to impersonate a Google Cloud IAM service account without storing long-lived credentials inside Pods. A Kubernetes service account is mapped to a Google service account through an annotation and IAM policy binding. Applications can then use Google Cloud client libraries to access services like Cloud Storage, Pub/Sub, and Secret Manager with ne-grained IAM permissions. It is important because it eliminates the need to mount JSON key files, which are a security risk if leaked. It follows the principle of least privilege and rotates tokens automatically.
  • How do you set up a database in GKE using a StatefulSet?

    A StatefulSet is used because databases require stable network identity and persistent storage. The StatefulSet defines a volumeClaimTemplate so each replica gets its own PersistentVolumeClaim backed by a Google Cloud persistent disk through a StorageClass. A headless Service provides stable DNS names for each Pod, such as db- 0.db.default.svc.cluster.local. Con gMaps can separate primary and replica configuration, and Secrets store credentials. Init containers can perform bootstrapping tasks like setting server IDs or cloning data from a peer. Readiness and liveness probes are configured to verify the database is healthy before traffic is sent or before Kubernetes restarts the Pod.
  • Why should a StatefulSet use a headless Service for a database cluster?

    A headless Service has clusterIP: None and does not load balance. Instead, it creates DNS records for each individual Pod. For a database cluster, members need to discover and communicate with specific peers by stable name. For example, a MySQL InnoDB cluster can use mysql-0.mysql, mysql-1.mysql, and mysql-2.mysql as stable hostnames. A regular ClusterIP Service would return one virtual IP and distribute traffic, which breaks protocols that require direct peer connections. Headless Services are therefore essential for protocols and clustering mechanisms that rely on per-instance identity.
  • How do Kubernetes microservices discover each other using DNS?

    Kubernetes runs an internal DNS service, usually CoreDNS, inside the cluster. Each Service receives a DNS name in the format ..svc.cluster.local. Pods can resolve this name to the Service's ClusterIP, and traffic is load-balanced across backend Pods. Headless Services return the individual Pod IPs. For cross-namespace communication, the full DNS name including the namespace is used. DNS-based discovery allows microservices to find dependencies without hard-coding IP addresses, and it works automatically as Pods are created, deleted, or rescheduled.
  • What is the difference between a ClusterIP, NodePort, and LoadBalancer Service?

    ClusterIP exposes the Service only inside the cluster on an internal IP. It is the default and is used for internal microservice communication. NodePort exposes the Service on a static port of every node, allowing external traffic to reach the Service through any node's IP. LoadBalancer provisions a cloud provider load balancer with a public IP and forwards traffic to the Service. In GKE, LoadBalancer Services create a Google Cloud network load balancer. For HTTP applications, Ingress is usually preferred over LoadBalancer because it supports path-based routing, TLS termination, and cost sharing.
  • What is a Kubernetes Ingress, and how is it used in GKE?

    An Ingress is a Kubernetes resource that exposes HTTP and HTTPS routes from outside the cluster to Services inside the cluster. In GKE, an Ingress resource is ful lled by GKE Ingress Controller, which provisions a Google Cloud HTTP load balancer. Ingress supports host-based and path-based routing, TLS termination using Kubernetes Secrets, and backend health checks. For backend developers, Ingress is the entry point for external API traffic. For DevOps engineers, it centralizes external access, SSL management, and routing rules without creating multiple LoadBalancer Services.
  • How do you reserve a static IP for a GKE Ingress?

    You create a global static IP in GCP with gcloud compute addresses create --global, then reference it in the Ingress annotation networking.gke.io/static-ip: . The IP must be in the same region as the load balancer for regional Ingress, or global for global Ingress. This ensures the external IP does not change when the Ingress is recreated. For production APIs and websites, a static IP is important because DNS records can point to it reliably.
  • What is the difference between a Con gMap and a Secret?

    Con gMaps store non-sensitive configuration data such as environment variables, command- line arguments, and configuration files. Secrets store sensitive data such as passwords, tokens, and TLS certificates. Both can be mounted as volumes or injected as environment variables. Values in Secrets are base64-encoded but not encrypted by default unless etcd encryption at rest is enabled. Secrets should be used for sensitive data, but they should still be protected with RBAC and integrated with external secret managers when possible. Con gMaps can be updated more freely and are safe to store in version control if they contain no secrets.
  • How do you manage environment-specific configuration in GKE?

    I use separate namespaces for each environment such as dev, staging, and prod. Each namespace contains its own Con gMaps and Secrets. The Deployment references these resources, so the same container image can run in any environment with the correct configuration. For managing many environments, I use Helm or Kustomize to generate manifests per environment from a common base. Sensitive values are stored in Google Secret Manager and injected by an external secrets operator or Workload Identity, rather than being committed to Git. This separation keeps images immutable and configuration environment-specific.
  • How do you securely store and use secrets in GKE?

    The best approach is to use Workload Identity to allow Pods to fetch secrets directly from Google Secret Manager at runtime. Alternatively, I use an external secrets operator such as External Secrets Operator or Sealed Secrets for GitOps work ows. Native Kubernetes Secrets can be used for simple cases, but they should be encrypted at rest in etcd, access should be restricted with RBAC, and they should be mounted as files rather than environment variables when possible. I never store secrets in container images, Con gMaps, or Git repositories in plain text. Regular rotation and audit logging are also important.
  • What is the typical CI/CD ow for deploying to GKE using GitHub Actions?

    The CI/CD ow usually has two stages. The continuous integration stage runs on every pull request or push to the main branch. It builds the application, runs tests, performs code quality checks, and builds a Docker image. The image is tagged with the Git commit SHA and pushed to Artifact Registry. The continuous delivery stage is triggered after a successful build. It updates the Kubernetes manifest, typically with the new image tag, and applies it to the cluster. In a GitOps approach, GitHub Actions only builds and pushes the image, while a tool like ArgoCD detects the manifest change and syncs the cluster. This separates image building from deployment.
  • What is GitOps, and how does it apply to GKE deployments?

    GitOps is an operational model where Git is the single source of truth for infrastructure and application configurations. In GKE, Kubernetes manifests or Helm charts are stored in a Git repository. A GitOps controller such as ArgoCD or Flux watches the repository and continuously reconciles the cluster state with the desired state in Git. When a developer merges a change, the controller applies it. GitOps provides version control, audit trails, easy rollbacks by reverting a commit, and drift detection. It is popular for GKE deployments because it makes deployment history transparent and automates complex multi-environment management.
  • What is the difference between push-based and pull-based CI/CD?

    In push-based CI/CD, a pipeline such as GitHub Actions builds the artifact and then pushes configuration changes directly to the cluster using kubectl. The pipeline controls when deployments happen. In pull-based CI/CD, the pipeline builds and pushes the image, then updates a Git repository. A controller running in the cluster such as ArgoCD pulls the desired state from Git and applies it. Pull- based deployment is more secure because the cluster does not need long-lived external credentials to be modified, and it supports automatic drift correction. Push-based deployment is simpler but can lead to configuration drift if manual cluster changes occur.
  • What are common deployment strategies for GKE?

    Common strategies include rolling updates, blue-green deployments, canary deployments, and recreates. Rolling updates replace Pods gradually using Deployment strategy settings. Blue-green runs two identical environments and switches traffic between them. Canary deploys a new version to a small subset of users and gradually increases traffic while monitoring metrics. Recreate stops old Pods before starting new ones, causing downtime and is rarely used for production services. GKE supports these through Kubernetes Deployments, Ingress traffic splitting, Istio, or Argo Rollouts.
  • How do you implement a canary deployment in GKE?

    A canary deployment can be implemented with a Service mesh such as Istio or with GKE Ingress traffic splitting. With Istio, you define a VirtualService that routes a small percentage of traffic to the canary version based on labels. With GKE Ingress, you create a BackendCon g with traffic splitting weights across two Services. You deploy the canary version as a separate Deployment with a small replica count, route 5 or 10 percent of traffic to it, monitor error rates and latency, and gradually increase the percentage if healthy. Automated rollback triggers if metrics exceed thresholds.
  • How do you monitor and log GKE workloads?

    GKE integrates with Google Cloud Logging and Cloud Monitoring, which collect container logs and metrics automatically. I configure structured logging so logs can be filtered and analyzed easily. For metrics, I use Cloud Monitoring dashboards and alert policies for CPU, memory, disk, and custom application metrics. I also deploy Prometheus and Grafana for Kubernetes-native metrics such as pod resource usage, node health, and HPA behavior. For distributed systems, I add tracing with Cloud Trace or OpenTelemetry. Logs, metrics, and traces together provide full observability.
  • What is a StorageClass in GKE, and how do you use it?

    A StorageClass defines the type of storage provisioned for PersistentVolumeClaims. In GKE, common StorageClasses include standard backed by standard persistent disks and premium-rwo backed by SSDs. When a PVC references a StorageClass, GKE automatically provisions a PersistentVolume using the Google Cloud persistent disk CSI driver. For databases, I choose SSD- backed StorageClasses for better IOPS and latency. I can also set volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer so the disk is provisioned in the same zone as the Pod that will use it, which is important for zonal persistent disks.
  • How do you handle node upgrades in GKE?

    GKE supports automatic and manual node upgrades. For manual upgrades, I use gcloud container clusters upgrade or the Google Cloud Console. GKE creates new nodes with the new version, drains old nodes by evicting Pods gracefully using PodDisruptionBudgets, and then deletes the old nodes. To minimize disruption, I use regional clusters with nodes spread across zones, configure PodDisruptionBudgets, and ensure applications handle SIGTERM gracefully. For critical databases in StatefulSets, I perform upgrades carefully, one replica at a time, and verify cluster quorum before proceeding.
  • What is GKE's Cluster Autoscaler?

    Cluster Autoscaler automatically adjusts the number of nodes in a node pool based on pending Pods. If Pods cannot schedule because of insufficient resources, the autoscaler adds nodes. If nodes are underutilized and Pods can be moved elsewhere, it removes nodes. It works with node pools and respects resource requests, taints, and a nity rules. For backend teams, this means applications scale without manual capacity planning. For DevOps engineers, it reduces cost by removing idle nodes, but it requires careful tuning to avoid thrashing and ensure scale-up happens fast enough for traffic spikes.
  • How do you implement network policies in GKE?

    Network policies are Kubernetes resources that control traffic between Pods at the IP layer. In GKE, they require a CNI plugin that supports network policy enforcement, such as Calico. I define default-deny policies for ingress and egress in each namespace, then add allow policies for legitimate traffic such as from the ingress controller to the backend, from backend to database, and DNS egress. Network policies reduce the blast radius of a compromised Pod and enforce zero-trust communication within the cluster. They are essential for multi-tenant or regulated environments.
  • You are asked to create a production-ready GKE cluster. What gcloud commands and ags do you consider?

    I start by setting the project and region with gcloud config set project and gcloud config set compute/region . I create a regional cluster for high availability using gcloud container clusters create --region --num-nodes 3 --enable-autoscaling --min-nodes 2 --max-nodes 10 --release- channel regular --enable-network-policy --enable-vertical-pod-autoscaling. I enable Workload Identity with --workload-pool=.svc.id.goog so Pods can use GCP IAM without service account keys. I choose a private cluster with authorized networks for the control plane if security requires it. I then run gcloud container clusters get-credentials --region to configure kubectl. For storage, I verify the default StorageClass and create a premium SSD class if needed. Real-time example: a ntech company needs a GKE cluster in us-central1. I create a regional cluster with three zones, enable Workload Identity, Network Policy with Calico, cluster autoscaling, and node auto-repair. I restrict control plane access to corporate IP ranges. The cluster is ready for PCI-DSS scoped workloads after additional hardening.
  • A GKE cluster needs access to Google Cloud Storage without using service account keys. How do you configure it?

    I use GKE Workload Identity. First, I create a Google Cloud service account with the roles/storage.objectViewer role. I create a Kubernetes service account in the application namespace and annotate it with the Google service account email using iam.gke.io/gcp- service-account. I then bind the Kubernetes service account to the Google service account using gcloud iam service-accounts add-iam-policy-binding with the roles/iam.workloadIdentityUser role. The application uses the Google Cloud client library, which automatically obtains tokens through the metadata server. No JSON key is mounted in the Pod. Real-time example: an image processing service uploads files to Cloud Storage. Previously, a JSON key was mounted from a Secret and rotated quarterly. I migrate to Workload Identity, remove the key Secret, bind the Pod's service account to storage- uploader@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com, and the application uploads files using default credentials. Security improves and credential rotation is automatic.
  • A Postgres database needs to run in GKE with persistent storage. How do you design the deployment?

    I use a StatefulSet because Postgres needs stable identity and storage. I create a headless Service for stable DNS names per replica. I define a volumeClaimTemplate in the StatefulSet that requests storage from a premium SSD StorageClass, such as premium-rwo. I store credentials in a Kubernetes Secret and mount them as environment variables. I use a Con gMap for postgresql.conf settings. I configure liveness and readiness probes, and an init container if replication setup is needed. I also set a PodDisruptionBudget with maxUnavailable: 1 to protect quorum or availability during node drains. For production, I prefer Cloud SQL through a Private IP or Cloud SQL Auth Proxy unless the business requires running the database inside GKE. Real-time example: a SaaS product requires a self-managed Postgres cluster in GKE. I deploy a StatefulSet postgres with three replicas, each with a 100 GB premium-rwo PVC. The headless Service postgres gives DNS names postgres-0.postgres, postgres-1.postgres, and postgres-2.postgres. I configure Patroni for leader election and failover. PodDisruptionBudget ensures only one replica is disrupted at a time during node maintenance.
  • A microservice in namespace A cannot reach a service in namespace B. What do you check?

    I verify the Service exists in namespace B and has endpoints with kubectl get svc -n b and kubectl get endpoints -n b. I check that the client is using the full DNS name ..svc.cluster.local. I inspect labels to ensure the Service selector matches the target Pods. I check NetworkPolicies in both namespaces to see if traffic is blocked. I run a debug Pod in namespace A and test connectivity with curl or nslookup. If DNS fails, I check CoreDNS Pods and Con gMap. If NetworkPolicy is the issue, I add an allow rule from the source Pod selector to the destination port. Real-time example: the order service in namespace orders cannot call the payment service in namespace payments. The order service uses only payment-service without the namespace. I update the URL to payment- service.payments.svc.cluster.local. Additionally, a default-deny NetworkPolicy in payments blocks cross-namespace traffic, so I add an allow policy for Pods labeled app: order- service from namespace orders on port 8080.
  • You need to expose a Spring Boot API on api.shop.com with HTTPS. What Kubernetes resources do you create?

    I create a Deployment with the Spring Boot container, a Service of type ClusterIP selecting the Pods, and an Ingress that routes api.shop.com to the Service. I create a Kubernetes TLS Secret containing the certificate and key, and reference it in the Ingress tls section. I install or use the GKE Ingress Controller, which provisions a Google Cloud load balancer. I reserve a static global IP with gcloud compute addresses create and annotate the Ingress with networking.gke.io/static-ip. For DNS, I create an A record pointing api.shop.com to the static IP. I also configure backend health checks by ensuring the Spring Boot actuator health endpoint returns 200. Real-time example: an e-commerce API needs public HTTPS access. I deploy the service with a ClusterIP Service and an Ingress with host api.shop.com, TLS Secret api-shop-tls, and static IP annotation. After DNS propagation, external users reach the API over HTTPS, and Google Cloud Load Balancer terminates TLS.
  • A developer accidentally commits a Kubernetes Secret manifest with a plaintext password to Git. What do you do?

    First, I treat it as a security incident. I rotate the exposed credential immediately because the password is now in Git history and potentially exposed. I remove the Secret from the manifest and use a secret management solution such as Google Secret Manager, External Secrets Operator, or Sealed Secrets. If the password must be in a Kubernetes Secret, I generate it with kubectl create secret generic locally or in a secure CI step, and add the manifest to .gitignore. I also review Git history and consider rewriting history if the repository is private and small. Finally, I update the team runbook to forbid committing Secrets and add a CI check that rejects files containing kind: Secret with base64-looking values. Real-time example: a database password appears in db-secret.yaml pushed to GitHub. I rotate the database password, delete the manifest from the repo, configure External Secrets Operator to fetch the password from Google Secret Manager, and add a pre-commit hook that blocks Kubernetes Secret YAML files.
  • A GitHub Actions work ow builds and deploys to GKE, but the deployment fails because kubectl cannot authenticate. How do you fix it?

    I check that the work ow authenticates to Google Cloud using Workload Identity Federation or a service account key stored as a GitHub secret. I use google-github-actions/auth to authenticate, then google-github-actions/get-gke-credentials to configure kubectl. I verify the service account has roles/container.developer on the GKE cluster. I also confirm that the project ID, cluster name, and region in the work ow match the GKE cluster. If using Workload Identity Federation, I verify the GitHub repository and work ow identity mapping in the GCP workload identity pool provider. If the work ow uses a long-lived JSON key, I migrate to Workload Identity Federation for better security. Real-time example: a pipeline fails with Unable to connect to the server: dial tcp ...: connect: connection refused. The work ow used an old cluster name after a recent cluster recreation. I update the work ow to the new cluster name and region, configure Workload Identity Federation with the provider audience, and get-gke- credentials succeeds.
  • You want to deploy a new image to GKE only after all tests pass. How do you structure the GitHub Actions work ow?

    I split the work ow into jobs with dependencies. The first job checks out code, runs unit tests, integration tests, and code quality checks. The second job builds the Docker image and pushes it to Artifact Registry with a tag such as the Git commit SHA. The third job deploys to a staging environment by updating the Kubernetes manifest and applying it or opening a pull request to the GitOps repository. The fourth job runs smoke tests against staging. A production deployment job requires manual approval using GitHub Environments and protection rules. Each job has needs dependencies so deployment only runs after earlier jobs succeed. This ensures no broken image reaches production. Real-time example: a Spring Boot service pipeline has test, build, deploy-staging, smoke-tests, and deploy-prod jobs. deploy-prod requires a manual approval from the release manager. The commit SHA image tag guarantees traceability from code to cluster.
  • A GKE StatefulSet database loses data when a Pod is deleted. What is the likely cause?

    The StatefulSet is not using persistent storage, or the PVC is not bound correctly. If the Pod uses an emptyDir volume, data is deleted when the Pod is removed. I check the StatefulSet manifest for volumeClaimTemplates and verify that each Pod has a bound PVC. I inspect the StorageClass and ensure it provisions volumes successfully. I also verify the reclaim policy; a Delete policy removes the PV when the PVC is deleted, which may be intentional but must be understood. The correct design is a volumeClaimTemplate so each ordinal Pod gets a stable PVC that is reattached on rescheduling. Real-time example: a Redis StatefulSet uses emptyDir for persistence. A node drains, the Pod moves, and all cache data is gone. I replace emptyDir with a volumeClaimTemplate using premium-rwo, and the new Pod mounts the same PVC after rescheduling, preserving data.
  • You need to update a Con gMap used by a running Deployment. Will the application pick up the change?

    If the Con gMap is mounted as a volume, Kubernetes updates the files in the running containers, but the application must watch or reload the files to use them. If the Con gMap is injected as environment variables, the values are set at Pod creation time and will not update until the Pod restarts. To force a restart, I run kubectl rollout restart deployment/. For applications that support hot reload, I implement a file watcher or periodic refresh. For most Java backend services, rolling restart is the simplest and most reliable method. Real-time example: a feature ag stored in a Con gMap is updated, but the Spring Boot application still uses the old value. I mount the Con gMap as a volume and add a FileSystemWatcher that refreshes the ag on change. I also keep kubectl rollout restart as a fallback for ags that are loaded once at startup.
  • A GKE Ingress returns 502 Bad Gateway for a healthy service. What do you check?

    A 502 from a Google Cloud load balancer usually means the backend Pods are unhealthy or the backend service configuration is wrong. I check the Service targetPort matches the container containerPort. I verify the readiness probe in the Deployment is correct and that Pods are marked ready. I check the Ingress backend health status in the Google Cloud Console. If the application binds to 127.0.0.1 inside the container, the load balancer health check cannot reach it, so I ensure the application listens on 0.0.0.0. I also check rewall rules allowing health checks from Google Cloud IP ranges to the nodes. Real-time example: a Spring Boot application listens on localhost:8080 for security reasons. The GCE health check targets the Pod IP and fails. I change the binding to 0.0.0.0:8080 and add a readiness probe on /actuator/health/readiness. The Ingress returns 200.
  • A GKE cluster cost is too high. What optimizations do you consider?

    I review node utilization with Cloud Monitoring and kubectl top nodes. I enable Cluster Autoscaler with appropriate min and max node counts to remove idle nodes. I use preemptible or spot instances for non-critical workloads. I right-size machine types based on actual usage. I use GKE Autopilot if workloads t its constraints to pay per Pod. I implement Horizontal Pod Autoscaler to scale workloads based on demand. I schedule batch jobs during off-peak hours and use resource requests and limits to avoid over-provisioning. I also review LoadBalancer costs and consolidate external access through Ingress instead of multiple LoadBalancer Services. Real-time example: a staging cluster runs three n2-standard-8 nodes 24/7 but is only used during business hours. I enable Cluster Autoscaler with min 0 and max 5, switch non-production workloads to spot instances, and add a scheduled scale- down script. Monthly cost drops by 60 percent.
  • A database StatefulSet needs zero-downtime node maintenance. How do you proceed?

    I configure a PodDisruptionBudget with maxUnavailable: 1 so Kubernetes drains only one replica at a time. Before maintenance, I verify the database cluster is healthy and has quorum. I cordon the node to prevent new Pods from scheduling on it. I drain the node with kubectl drain which respects PodDisruptionBudgets. The StatefulSet reschedules the evicted Pod on another node and reattaches its PVC. I wait for the Pod to become ready and the cluster to synchronize before draining the next node. For single-replica databases, downtime is unavoidable unless a secondary is promoted. I also ensure the database handles SIGTERM gracefully to close connections cleanly. Real- time example: A three-node MySQL InnoDB cluster runs on GKE. I set maxUnavailable: 1 and use kubectl drain on each node sequentially. Each time, MySQL reschedules, reattaches its PVC, and rejoins the group. The application sees no downtime because the read-write Service always points to the current primary.
  • A team wants GitOps with ArgoCD on GKE. How do you set it up?

    I install ArgoCD in a dedicated namespace on the GKE cluster. I configure it to connect to the Git repository containing Kubernetes manifests or Helm charts. I create an ArgoCD Application for each microservice, pointing to the environment-specific path or branch. I enable auto-sync, self-heal, and prune so the cluster matches Git. I use ApplicationSet for multi-environment or multi-cluster deployments. I configure RBAC in ArgoCD to control who can sync or modify applications. Secrets are managed with Sealed Secrets or External Secrets Operator because ArgoCD should not read plaintext secrets from Git. I also configure webhooks or polling so changes are picked up quickly. Real-time example: a platform team adopts ArgoCD for GKE. They install ArgoCD via Helm, create an app-of- apps pattern, and configure each microservice Application to watch a GitOps repo. A developer merges a manifest change, ArgoCD detects the drift, syncs the cluster, and the Git commit history becomes the audit trail for all deployments.
  • A developer asks how to debug a failing Pod in GKE. What commands and tools do you recommend?

    I recommend a systematic approach. First, kubectl get pods -n to see the status. Second, kubectl describe pod -n to read events. Third, kubectl logs -n and kubectl logs --previous for crash forensics. Fourth, kubectl exec -it -- /bin/sh to inspect the running container if it is still alive. If the image is distroless, I use a debug ephemeral container with kubectl debug. For networking issues, I run a debug Pod with nicolaka/netshoot and test DNS, routing, and connectivity. I also check Google Cloud Logging and Monitoring for cluster-level events and resource saturation. Real-time example: a Pod shows CrashLoopBackOff. kubectl logs --previous shows a missing table error. I exec into the database Pod, confirm the migration did not run, check the init container logs, and find that the migration container used the wrong database host. I fix the Con gMap and the application starts.
  • A GKE Pod needs a GPU. How do you configure the node pool and workload?

    I create a node pool with GPU-enabled machine types using gcloud container node- pools create --cluster --accelerator type=,count=. I install the NVIDIA driver daemonset, which GKE can deploy automatically with --enable-autoprovisioning or manually. I add a nodeSelector or node a nity to the Pod to schedule it on GPU nodes, such as cloud.google.com/gke-accelerator: nvidia-tesla- t4. I set resource limits for nvidia.com/gpu in the Pod spec. I also consider taints and tolerations to prevent non-GPU workloads from scheduling on expensive GPU nodes. Real-time example: a machine learning inference service needs a T4 GPU. I create a GPU node pool with autoscaling from 0 to 2 nodes, add a toleration and nodeSelector to the inference Deployment, and set resources.limits.nvidia.com/gpu: 1. The Pod schedules only when GPU capacity is available, and the node scales to zero when no inference traffic is running.
  • A new release causes errors in production. How do you roll back in GKE?

    If the release was a Deployment update, I run kubectl rollout undo deployment/ -n to revert to the previous revision. I verify the rollback with kubectl rollout status and monitor error rates and latency. If the release was applied through GitOps, I revert the manifest change in Git and let ArgoCD sync the cluster to the previous state. For critical services, I use a blue-green or canary strategy so I can switch traffic back to the stable version quickly without waiting for a full Pod restart. After rollback, I analyze logs and metrics to identify the root cause before attempting the release again. Real-time example: a new version of the payment service has 5 percent error rate. I run kubectl rollout undo deployment/payment-service -n prod, which restores the previous ReplicaSet. Error rate returns to baseline within two minutes. I then inspect the new version's logs and find an incompatible database migration that ran in staging but not with the previous prod schema.
  • A GKE cluster must comply with a no-public-IP policy for control plane access. How do you configure it?

    I create a private cluster with a private control plane endpoint using gcloud container clusters create --enable-private-nodes --enable-private-endpoint --master-ipv4-cidr . With a private endpoint, the control plane is accessible only from authorized networks or through a bastion host or Cloud Interconnect. I add authorized networks for the CI/CD runner IP ranges and the corporate VPN. I also ensure nodes have no external IPs by using -- create-subnetwork with private nodes. For workloads that need outbound internet access, I configure Cloud NAT. This setup is common in regulated industries and prevents direct internet exposure of the Kubernetes API server. Real-time example: a healthcare company requires a private GKE cluster. I create a private cluster with a /28 master CIDR, add authorized networks for the o ce and CI runner, configure Cloud NAT for egress, and restrict worker nodes to private IPs only. Security audits pass without public API exposure.
  • A microservice needs environment-specific feature ags. How do you manage them in GKE?

    I store feature ags in a Con gMap per namespace, one for each environment. The Deployment mounts the Con gMap as a file or injects values as environment variables. The application reads the ag value and toggles behavior accordingly. For more advanced use cases, I use a feature ag service such as LaunchDarkly, Split, or a custom ag API. When a ag changes, I either update the Con gMap and restart the Deployment, or use a ags API that supports dynamic updates. Con gMaps work well for static per-environment ags, while a ag service is better for user-targeted or gradual rollouts. Real-time example: an e-commerce service needs a new checkout UI in staging but not production. I add feature.new-checkout=true to the staging Con gMap and feature.new- checkout=false to the production Con gMap. The same image runs in both environments, and behavior changes based on the mounted configuration.
  • A GKE workload needs to handle traffic spikes automatically. How do you set up autoscaling?

    I configure Horizontal Pod Autoscaler on the Deployment using CPU or custom metrics. I install the GKE Metrics Server if not already available. The HPA scales replicas between a configured minimum and maximum based on target utilization. I also enable Cluster Autoscaler on the node pool so new nodes are added when HPA creates Pods that cannot t. For rapid spikes, I set lower target CPU and buffer capacity with maxSurge. For event-driven workloads, I use Cloud Run or Google Cloud Pub/Sub with a Kubernetes consumer autoscaled on queue depth. I verify autoscaling behavior with load tests. Real-time example: a ticket booking service sees tenfold traffic spikes during sales. I add an HPA targeting 60 percent CPU with minReplicas: 3 and maxReplicas: 50. I enable Cluster Autoscaler on the node pool with max 20 nodes. Load testing shows Pods scale up within 60 seconds and nodes scale up within two minutes, handling spikes without manual intervention.
21
Chapter 21

Java Threads, Executor Framework, CompletableFuture, Spring Boot Scheduling, Async, and Tomcat Threading Model

This chapter covers Java Threads, Executor Framework, CompletableFuture, Spring Boot Scheduling, Async, and Tomcat Threading Model. It includes 49 interview questions and scenarios.

21.1.Key Concepts

  • What is a thread in Java, and how does it differ from a process?

    A thread is the smallest unit of execution within a process. While a process has its own isolated memory space, file descriptors, and system resources, threads within the same process share the heap memory and most resources but maintain separate program counters, registers, and stack memory. Multiple threads allow concurrent execution paths within a single application. In Java, threads are managed by the JVM and mapped to operating system threads through the underlying platform. The main advantage of threads over separate processes is lower creation and context-switching overhead because they share memory. The major risk is that shared memory requires careful synchronization to avoid race conditions, stale reads, and inconsistent state. Real- time example: consider an e-commerce web server handling many customer requests. If each request ran in a separate process, the server would consume enormous memory and CPU just for process management. Instead, the server uses a thread pool where each request runs in a thread. All threads share the connection pool and cache, but each has its own stack for local variables. When two threads try to update the same inventory counter simultaneously, synchronization is required to prevent overselling.
  • What are the ways to create a thread in Java?

    There are two primary ways to create a thread. The first is to extend the Thread class and override the run() method, then call start(). The second, preferred approach is to implement the Runnable interface and pass an instance to a Thread constructor. The Runnable approach is preferred because it separates the task logic from the thread mechanism and avoids the single-inheritance limitation of extending Thread. Since Java 5, a third way is to implement Callable and submit it to an ExecutorService, which returns a Future for result retrieval. Java 8 introduced lambda expressions, making Runnable and Callable creation concise. A backend developer should prefer Runnable, Callable, and executors over raw Thread creation because executors manage thread lifecycle and reuse. Real-time example: a beginner might write new Thread(new MyTask()).start() for a background job. In a production system, this is discouraged because creating and destroying threads constantly is expensive. Instead, a senior developer writes: executorService.submit(() -> processOrder(orderId)); This submits a Runnable to a pool that reuses threads, handles rejection, and can be shut down gracefully.
  • What is the difference between run() and start()?

    Calling start() on a Thread object begins a new thread of execution and invokes the run() method asynchronously in that newly created thread. Calling run() directly does not start a new thread; it executes the method synchronously in the current thread just like any other method call, which defeats the purpose of multithreading. Starting a thread twice by calling start() twice throws IllegalThreadStateException. The run() method is the entry point containing the task logic, while start() is the mechanism that schedules the thread with the JVM and underlying operating system. Real-time example: a junior developer writes: Thread t = new Thread(myRunnable); t.run(); Expecting the task to run in the background, but the code actually blocks the calling thread. The fix is t.start(). Another common mistake is calling start() on the same Thread instance twice, which throws IllegalThreadStateException. The correct pattern is to create a new Thread instance for each new execution.
  • What are the states in the Java thread lifecycle?

    Java threads transition through several states that are accessible through Thread.State. NEW means the thread is created but start() has not been called. RUNNABLE means the thread is ready to run or currently running on a CPU. BLOCKED means the thread is waiting to acquire a monitor lock. WAITING means the thread is waiting indefinitely for another thread to perform an action, such as through Object.wait() without a timeout or Thread.join() without a timeout. TIMED_WAITING means the thread is waiting for a specified time, such as through Thread.sleep(millis), Object.wait(timeout), or Thread.join(timeout). TERMINATED means execution has completed. Understanding the lifecycle is essential for diagnosing deadlocks, thread starvation, and performance issues in production by analyzing thread dumps. Real-time example: a production service hangs during peak load. A thread dump reveals many threads in BLOCKED state waiting for a lock held by a single thread in RUNNABLE state that is doing heavy computation. This points to a lock contention bottleneck. Reducing the scope of the synchronized block reduces the BLOCKED time and restores throughput.
  • What is the difference between sleep(), yield(), and wait()?

    Thread.sleep(millis) pauses the current thread for a specified time without releasing any locks it currently holds. It transitions the thread to TIMED_WAITING. Thread.yield() is a hint to the scheduler that the current thread is willing to give up the CPU, but it is not guaranteed to pause and does not release locks. Object.wait() causes the current thread to release the object's monitor lock and wait until another thread calls notify() or notifyAll() on the same object, transitioning the thread to WAITING or TIMED_WAITING. wait() must be called inside a synchronized block or method because it requires ownership of the object's monitor to release it. In contrast, sleep() and yield() are static methods on Thread and do not require synchronization. Real-time example: in a producer-consumer queue implemented manually, the consumer calls wait() when the queue is empty, releasing the lock so the producer can add items. The producer calls notifyAll() after adding an item. If the consumer used sleep() instead of wait(), it would hold the lock while sleeping, blocking the producer from adding items, which would deadlock the system.
  • What is the Java Memory Model, and why is it important for concurrency?

    The Java Memory Model defines the rules for how threads interact through memory and what behaviors the Java language guarantees. It specifies visibility, ordering, and atomicity rules for shared variable access. Without the JMM, different threads might see different values of the same variable due to CPU caches, compiler optimizations, and instruction reordering.
