  • How do you handle test data setup in integration tests without making tests depend on each other?: Each test should start from a clean, well-defined state. Use the transactional rollback behavior of @Transactional on tests so database changes are undone. For data that must survive across transactions, such as when testing a service that calls @Transactional(propagation = REQUIRES_NEW), use TestEntityManager or a custom cleanup in @AfterEach to delete rows. Avoid relying on data created by a previous test because it makes execution order matter and causes flaky failures. Some teams use SQL scripts or libraries like Flyway for baseline schema and small seed data sets, but test-specific data should be created and cleaned up by the test itself.
  • A developer writes a service test that passes locally but fails on CI because of timezone differences. How do you investigate and fix it?: I first look at any date or timestamp assertions in the test and in the production code. If the test creates an expected value using LocalDateTime.now() or new Date() and compares it against a value returned by the service, the comparison will fail when CI runs in a different zone or at a different millisecond. The fix is to avoid comparing against wall-clock time in unit tests; instead, inject a Clock bean or use a fixed timestamp provided by the test. For integration tests, I ensure the JVM timezone and database timezone are explicit, for example by setting -Duser.timezone=UTC in the CI pipeline and using TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE columns. I also check whether the test relies on the current date for filtering and replace it with a controlled reference date.
  • A @DataJpaTest passes on its own but fails when the whole suite runs because of leftover data. What is the cause?: The cause is usually a test that commits data and does not clean up, or a non-transactional operation that leaks state. @DataJpaTest is transactional by default, so most tests roll back, but a test calling a service method annotated with @Transactional(propagation = REQUIRES_NEW) commits in a new transaction and leaves data behind. Another cause is a @Sql script that inserts shared rows without cleanup, or a test that uses native JdbcTemplate without a transaction. I find the offending test by running the suite with logging enabled and inspecting the database state after each test class. The fix is to add explicit cleanup in @AfterEach, use unique identifiers per test, or refactor the production code so tests can rely on automatic rollback.
  • A controller test with @WebMvcTest returns a 200 status but the response body is empty. How do you debug it?: An empty body usually means the controller returns a response object but it is not serialized, or the mock did not return the expected object. I first verify that the controller method is actually returning the object and that @RestController or @ResponseBody is present. Then I check the mocked service; if when(service.findById(1L)).thenReturn(order) uses a different argument than the controller passes, Mockito returns null and Jackson serializes an empty body. I also check that the object has getters, because Jackson needs them. Finally, I inspect the MockMvc result to see whether an exception was swallowed, and I enable debug logging for org.springframework.web to trace the request through the filters.
  • A service test using Mockito throws an UnnecessaryStubbingException after a refactor. What is happening?: UnnecessaryStubbingException is thrown when strict stubbing is enabled and a stubbed interaction was never used during the test. After the refactor, the production code may no longer call the stubbed method, or the test may have stubbed a method that was only relevant to an older implementation. I review the test to remove unused stubs and to confirm that the remaining stubs are actually required by the scenario being tested. If strict stubbing is too noisy for a class with shared setup, I either split the test into smaller focused tests or apply Mockito.lenient() only where a shared stub is acceptable. The goal is to keep the test honest about what it is verifying.
  • An integration test with @SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = RANDOM_PORT) fails because the actuator health endpoint returns 404. How do you fix it?: The actuator endpoint is not loaded because @SpringBootTest does not auto- configure actuator unless the actuator starter is on the classpath and the endpoint is enabled. I check that spring-boot-starter-actuator is a dependency and that the management.endpoints.web.exposure.include property includes health. If the test uses webEnvironment = NONE, actuator web endpoints are not available, so I switch to RANDOM_PORT or MOCK. I also verify that the test does not override management.server.port to a fixed port that collides with another test. When actuator is intentionally disabled for a test, I either enable it for that test class or assert against an internal service bean instead of the HTTP endpoint.
  • A test with Testcontainers is extremely slow because the database container starts for every test class. How do you speed it up?: I start by moving the container declaration to a base class or a shared utility so the same container instance is reused across multiple test classes. With Testcontainers, I enable the testcontainers.reuse.enable flag in ~/.testcontainers.properties so containers stay alive between test runs during local development. On CI, I ensure Docker layer caching is enabled and that the base image is pulled before the build. I also look for unnecessary @DirtiesContext annotations that force the Spring context to restart. If the suite is still slow, I move the fastest unit-like repository tests to H2 and reserve Testcontainers only for tests that exercise vendor-specific SQL or stored procedures.
  • A developer disables CSRF in a @WebMvcTest and now real requests fail with 403 in production. What went wrong?: The test disabled CSRF to make the mock setup easier, but the developer assumed the controller behavior was the only thing being tested. CSRF is part of Spring Security, and disabling it in tests hides the fact that state-changing browser requests require a valid token. The fix is to configure MockMvc with the same security settings used in production and to include CSRF tokens in state-changing requests using csrf(). For pure API clients using bearer tokens, CSRF is legitimately disabled, but that decision belongs in the production configuration, not hidden in tests. Tests should mirror production security as closely as possible to catch integration issues early.
  • A test with @MockBean runs fine alone but causes context reloads and slow builds across the suite. How do you address it?: @MockBean changes the Spring context fingerprint, so every unique combination of mocked beans triggers a new context cache entry. If many test classes mock different sets of beans, the cache fills up and contexts are constantly created and destroyed. I address this by grouping tests that need the same mocks into the same test class or by using @Import to share a common test configuration that declares the mocks once. For unit tests that do not need the Spring context at all, I replace @MockBean with plain Mockito @Mock and remove @SpringBootTest, which eliminates context reloads entirely. When a slice test such as @WebMvcTest is sufficient, I use it instead of the full context.
  • A repository test using H2 passes, but the same query fails in production on PostgreSQL. Why?: H2 supports a different SQL dialect than PostgreSQL, and some queries that work in H2 may use syntax, functions, or type casting that PostgreSQL does not accept. Native queries, JSON operators, window functions, and custom types are common sources of mismatch. The fix is to move the test from H2 to a Testcontainers PostgreSQL container so the test database matches production. I also review the query for dialect-specific features and consider using JPQL or the Criteria API for portable logic. If a native query is unavoidable, I write a dedicated integration test against the real database and keep the H2 test only for simple repository methods where dialect differences do not matter.
  • A developer writes a unit test that calls a private method using reflection. Is this a good practice, and what would you suggest?: Testing private methods through reflection is usually not a good practice because it couples the test to internal implementation details and makes refactoring painful. Private methods are tested indirectly by testing the public methods that call them. If the private method is complex enough to need its own tests, that is often a sign that it should be extracted into a separate class with public methods that can be tested directly. The exception is legacy code that cannot be refactored safely; in that case, reflection may be a temporary bridge, but the goal should be to refactor the code rather than maintain reflection-based tests.
  • A code coverage report shows 90 percent line coverage, but a bug still reaches production. How is that possible?: Line coverage only measures whether a line was executed, not whether it was verified. A test can execute error-handling code without asserting that the correct exception is thrown, or it can call a method with a weak assertion that misses the bug. Coverage also does not measure edge cases, concurrency, or integration failures. I treat coverage as a hygiene metric, not a quality guarantee. I review tests for meaningful assertions, add mutation testing or property-based testing for critical paths, and run integration tests against realistic data. If a bug escapes, I add a regression test that fails before the fix and passes after it.
  • A test that verifies an email was sent keeps failing intermittently. How do you stabilize it?: Intermittent failures in asynchronous email sending usually come from timing issues. The test asserts before the background thread has finished, or the mock interaction happens on a different thread than the test thread. I stabilize the test by using Awaitility to wait until the mock receives the expected call, or by returning a CompletableFuture from the email service so the test can wait for completion. If the email client runs inside an @Async method, I inject the TaskExecutor and wait for active tasks. I also make the test deterministic by controlling the thread pool or by making the email service synchronous in the test configuration.
  • A build fails because JaCoCo reports zero coverage for classes generated by MapStruct or Lombok. How do you handle it?: Generated code should not count against coverage because it is not hand-written business logic. For Lombok, I add lombok.config with lombok.addLombokGeneratedAnnotation = true so JaCoCo recognizes the @Generated annotation and skips those methods. For MapStruct, I configure the JaCoCo exclusion list to ignore the generated mapper implementation classes, for example with **/mapper/*Impl. I keep the domain and service classes included in coverage so the report reflects actual code quality. If the team uses other generators, I add their output packages to the exclusions and document why those classes are not measured.
  • A controller test passes in isolation but fails after adding Spring Security to the project. How do you update the test?: Adding Spring Security introduces filters that intercept every request, so unauthenticated requests to protected endpoints return 401 instead of 200. I update the test by configuring MockMvc with the security filters and either providing test credentials or disabling authentication for the test. For @WebMvcTest, I add @AutoConfigureMockMvc(addFilters = false) only if the goal is to test controller logic without security, but I prefer to keep security enabled and use @WithMockUser to simulate an authenticated user. I also add tests that verify the endpoint correctly rejects unauthenticated requests and enforces role-based access, so security behavior is covered, not bypassed.
  • A test suite reports IllegalStateException: Failed to load ApplicationContext after adding a new dependency. What is your debugging approach?: I start by reading the full stack trace and the caused-by chain, because the root cause is usually buried several levels deep. Common reasons are a missing property, a bean that fails to initialize, a conditional configuration that activates incorrectly under test, or a dependency that requires a real service not available in the test environment. I run a single failing test with debug logging for org.springframework.boot and org.springframework.test to see which bean fails. If the new dependency is not needed for the test, I exclude it with @SpringBootTest(classes = { ... }) or a test-specific profile. If it is needed, I provide a mock or a lightweight implementation with @TestConfiguration.
25
Chapter 25

Generative AI, Spring AI, Embeddings, RAG, Advisors, and Tool Calling

This chapter covers Generative AI, Spring AI, Embeddings, RAG, Advisors, and Tool Calling. It includes 44 interview questions and scenarios.

25.1.Key Concepts

  • What is Generative AI, and how does it differ from traditional AI?: Generative AI refers to models that can create new content such as text, images, audio, or code by learning patterns from training data. Unlike traditional AI, which is often used for classification, regression, or clustering, generative AI produces outputs that resemble its training distribution. Large language models such as GPT, Gemini, and Claude are generative AI systems trained to predict the next token in a sequence. For backend developers, generative AI enables features like chatbots, code assistants, content generation, and summarization. The key difference is that traditional AI maps inputs to known labels, while generative AI synthesizes novel outputs conditioned on inputs.
  • What is a Large Language Model, and how does it work at a high level?: A Large Language Model is a deep neural network, typically based on the Transformer architecture, trained on vast amounts of text to predict the next token in a sequence. During training, the model learns statistical relationships between words, grammar, facts, and reasoning patterns. At inference, it takes a prompt, converts it into tokens, processes it through many layers of self-attention and feed-forward networks, and generates tokens one at a time until a stop condition is reached. The model does not truly understand text in a human sense; it predicts likely sequences based on learned patterns. Modern LLMs have billions of parameters and are ne-tuned for instruction following and safety.
  • What is tokenization, and why does it matter?: Tokenization is the process of splitting text into smaller units called tokens, which can be words, subwords, or characters. LLMs operate on tokens rather than raw characters, so the input text is first converted to tokens, and the model predicts the next token. The number of tokens affects cost, context window usage, and latency. Different models use different tokenizers, so the same sentence may produce different token counts across providers. For backend developers, tokenization matters because APIs charge per token, prompt length is limited by the context window, and output must often be bounded by max tokens.
  • What is the Transformer architecture?: The Transformer is a neural network architecture introduced in the paper Attention Is All You Need. It is built from encoder and decoder stacks, though modern LLMs typically use decoder-only variants. The core component is the self-attention mechanism, which allows the model to weigh the importance of different tokens in the input when producing each output token. Transformers also use positional encoding to understand token order, feed-forward layers to transform representations, layer normalization, and residual connections. The architecture is highly parallelizable during training and scales well to billions of parameters.
  • What is self-attention, and why is it important?: Self-attention is a mechanism that computes a weighted representation of each token by considering all other tokens in the sequence. For every token, the model generates query, key, and value vectors, compares the query with all keys to produce attention scores, normalizes them with softmax, and uses the weighted sum of values as the output. This allows the model to capture long-range dependencies and contextual relationships regardless of token distance. It is important because it replaces sequential processing with parallel computation and gives the model the ability to focus on relevant parts of the input when generating each token.
  • What are embeddings in the context of generative AI?: Embeddings are dense numerical vector representations of text, images, or other data. They map semantic meaning into a high-dimensional space where similar items are closer together. In LLMs, token embeddings represent individual tokens, while sentence or document embeddings capture the meaning of larger text chunks. Embeddings are produced by passing text through an embedding model. They are used for tasks such as semantic search, classification, clustering, recommendation, and retrieval in RAG systems. The quality of embeddings depends on the model and the domain of the training data.
  • What is a vector store and vector search?: A vector store is a database optimized for storing and searching high-dimensional vectors. Vector search, also called similarity search, nds vectors that are closest to a query vector using distance metrics such as cosine similarity or Euclidean distance. Popular vector stores include pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Milvus, and Redis. They often use approximate nearest neighbor algorithms such as HNSW or IVF to search efficiently at scale. In generative AI applications, vector stores are used to retrieve relevant documents or facts based on semantic similarity, which improves response accuracy beyond what the model memorized during training.
  • What is Retrieval Augmented Generation?: Retrieval Augmented Generation is a pattern that combines information retrieval with text generation. Instead of relying solely on the model's training knowledge, the system retrieves relevant documents from a vector store or database and includes them in the prompt as context. The model then generates an answer grounded in the retrieved content. RAG reduces hallucination, keeps responses up to date, and allows the model to answer questions about private or proprietary data. It is commonly used for enterprise knowledge bases, documentation assistants, and customer support bots.
  • What is the difference between naive RAG and modular RAG?: Naive RAG embeds the user query, performs a single similarity search against a vector store, and injects the top documents into the prompt. It is simple to implement and works well for many cases. Modular RAG breaks the process into con gurable stages such as query transformation, retrieval, document post-processing, and augmentation. It can use query rewriting, compression, translation, multi-query expansion, reranking, and hybrid search. Modular RAG is more exible and accurate but also more complex. Spring AI supports naive RAG through QuestionAnswerAdvisor and modular RAG through RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor.
  • What is Spring AI?: Spring AI is a framework that brings AI capabilities to Spring applications. It provides a consistent, portable API for working with large language models, embedding models, vector stores, and AI patterns such as RAG and tool calling. It supports multiple model providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Ollama, Azure OpenAI, and Mistral. Spring AI is designed with the same philosophy as Spring: abstractions over vendor APIs, easy configuration, and integration with Spring Boot. Backend developers can use it to add chat, search, classification, and agentic features to Java applications without writing provider-specific boilerplate.
  • What is ChatClient in Spring AI?: ChatClient is the high-level uent API in Spring AI for interacting with chat models. It allows developers to build prompts, set model options, attach advisors, register tools, and call or stream responses. It is constructed from a ChatModel bean and configured through the builder. ChatClient abstracts the underlying provider, so switching from OpenAI to Anthropic requires changing configuration rather than application code. It supports synchronous calls with call(), streaming with stream(), structured output with entity(), and content extraction with content().
  • How do you create a ChatClient in Spring Boot?: You add the appropriate Spring AI starter, such as spring-ai-openai-spring-boot-starter, and configure properties like spring.ai.openai.api-key and spring.ai.openai.chat.options.model. Spring Boot auto-configures a ChatModel bean. You then create a ChatClient bean by calling ChatClient.builder(chatModel).build(). You can also set default advisors, default tools, and default options during construction. The ChatClient can be injected into services and used in REST controllers or background jobs. This pattern keeps AI logic inside Spring-managed services and integrates with the rest of the application.
  • What are Advisors in Spring AI?: Advisors are interceptors in the ChatClient request and response pipeline. They can modify the request before it reaches the model, process the response after it returns, or implement reusable patterns such as logging, chat memory, RAG retrieval, and tool calling. Advisors implement CallAdvisor or StreamAdvisor and run in a chain ordered by getOrder. Built-in advisors include MessageChatMemoryAdvisor for conversation memory, QuestionAnswerAdvisor for RAG, ToolCallingAdvisor for tool execution, and RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor for modular RAG. Custom advisors allow developers to add cross-cutting concerns like audit logging, prompt injection prevention, or cost tracking.
  • How do Advisors compare to Spring AOP interceptors?: Both Advisors and Spring AOP interceptors wrap behavior around a core operation. AOP intercepts method calls in the Spring application context, while Spring AI Advisors intercept calls to the language model. AOP is general purpose for business methods; Advisors are specialized for the ChatClient pipeline. In Spring AI, advisors receive ChatClientRequest and ChatClientResponse objects and can update context, modify prompts, or transform responses. They are specific to AI work ows and integrate with the ChatClient builder, whereas AOP interceptors work with any Spring bean method.
  • What is chat memory, and how is it managed in Spring AI?: Chat memory stores the history of a conversation so the model can maintain context across multiple turns. Without memory, each request is stateless and the model does not remember previous messages. Spring AI provides MessageChatMemoryAdvisor, which stores messages in an in-memory or persistent ChatMemory implementation. The advisor retrieves recent messages and adds them to the prompt. For longer conversations, VectorStoreChatMemoryAdvisor can retrieve semantically relevant past messages from a vector store. A conversation ID is used to scope messages to a specific user session.
  • What is Tool Calling in Spring AI?: Tool calling, also known as function calling, allows an LLM to invoke application methods to retrieve data or perform actions. In Spring AI, tools are defined by methods annotated with @Tool or by implementing ToolCallback. The ChatClient registers tools, and the ToolCallingAdvisor manages the loop: the model decides to call a tool, Spring AI executes the method, and the result is sent back to the model. The loop continues until the model produces a final response. Tool calling enables agents that can access real-time data, query databases, call external APIs, or take actions in the system.
  • How do you define a tool in Spring AI?: You create a method annotated with @Tool inside a Spring bean and pass the bean to ChatClient.defaultTools. The method should have a descriptive name and parameters, optionally with @ToolParameter annotations to describe them to the model. When the model needs the capability, it requests the tool with JSON arguments. Spring AI deserializes the arguments, invokes the method, and serializes the return value back to the model. Tools should be idempotent when possible and avoid side effects that cannot be undone. Complex tools may also use a ToolCallback implementation for full control over behavior and metadata.
  • What is dynamic tool discovery in Spring AI?: Dynamic tool discovery is a pattern for applications with many tools. Instead of sending all tool definitions to the model on every request, which consumes tokens and may confuse the model, a ToolSearchToolCallingAdvisor indexes tools and exposes a search tool. The model calls the search tool to find relevant tools, and only those tools are added to the context. This reduces token usage and improves accuracy when the application has dozens or hundreds of tools. Tool indexes can be semantic using VectorToolIndex, keyword-based using LuceneToolIndex, or pattern-based using RegexToolIndex.
  • What is structured output in Spring AI?: Structured output is the ability to ask an LLM to return data in a specific format, usually JSON, and map it to a Java object. Spring AI supports this through the entity() method on ChatClient, which parses the response into a POJO using a schema or instructions provided in the prompt. It can also use JSON mode or constrained decoding if the model supports it. Structured output is useful when you need to consume AI output programmatically, such as extracting entities, classifying text, or generating configuration objects. It is more reliable than parsing free-form text.
  • What are query transformers in modular RAG?: Query transformers rewrite or expand the user query before retrieval to improve search quality. Examples include RewriteQueryTransformer, which reformulates the query; CompressionQueryTransformer, which shortens conversational queries; TranslationQueryTransformer, which translates the query into another language; and MultiQueryExpander, which generates multiple related queries to broaden retrieval. These transformers help when user queries are ambiguous, conversational, or domain-specific. They are part of the RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor pipeline in Spring AI.
  • What are common vector stores used with Spring AI?: Spring AI supports several vector stores including pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Milvus, Redis, Neo4j, and Elasticsearch. The choice depends on existing infrastructure, scale, and query requirements. pgvector is convenient when the application already uses PostgreSQL. Pinecone and Weaviate are managed services optimized for vector search. Redis is useful when the application already uses Redis for caching. Milvus and Elasticsearch are suitable for large-scale or hybrid search scenarios. Each has a Spring Boot starter with auto-configuration.
  • How do you ingest documents into a vector store for RAG?: Ingestion typically involves reading documents from files or APIs, splitting them into chunks, computing embeddings for each chunk using an EmbeddingModel, and storing the resulting vectors and metadata in a VectorStore. Chunking strategy matters: too large chunks dilute relevance, while too small chunks lose context. Spring AI provides document readers, transformers, and splitters such as TokenTextSplitter and PagePdfDocumentReader. Metadata such as source, page number, or document type is stored alongside the vector to enable filtering. After ingestion, RAG queries can retrieve the most relevant chunks.
  • What are hallucinations, and how can RAG reduce them?: Hallucinations are cases where a language model generates plausible-sounding but incorrect or unsupported information. They happen because models are trained to predict likely tokens, not to verify facts against a knowledge source. RAG reduces hallucinations by grounding the model's response in retrieved documents. The prompt explicitly instructs the model to answer based only on the provided context. If the context does not contain the answer, the model can be instructed to say it does not know. Combining RAG with citations, reranking, and strict prompt instructions further improves factual reliability.
  • What are prompt templates, and why are they used?: A prompt template is a reusable text pattern with placeholders that are lled at runtime. It allows developers to separate prompt engineering from application code and to inject dynamic values such as user input, retrieved context, or conversation history. Spring AI provides PromptTemplate and a uent prompt builder. Templates improve maintainability because prompts can be adjusted without changing Java code. They also support system messages, user messages, and assistant messages, which help structure the conversation and guide model behavior.
  • A Spring AI chatbot gives outdated answers about company policy. How do you fix it?: The chatbot is relying on the model's training knowledge, which has a cutoff and may not include the latest company documents. I implement RAG by ingesting the company policy documents into a vector store. I split the documents into chunks, compute embeddings, and store them with metadata such as document name and effective date. I add a QuestionAnswerAdvisor to the ChatClient so each user query retrieves the most relevant policy chunks and includes them in the prompt. I instruct the model to answer only from the provided context and to say it does not know if the answer is not in the documents. I also schedule periodic re-ingestion when policies are updated. Real-time example: an HR chatbot tells users the old remote-work policy. I create a pipeline that loads the latest policy PDFs, splits them into 500-token chunks, embeds them with the default embedding model, and stores them in pgvector. The ChatClient uses QuestionAnswerAdvisor with a filter on the latest policy version. Responses now re ect current rules.
  • A RAG application retrieves irrelevant documents. What do you improve?: I investigate the retrieval pipeline end to end. I check chunk size and overlap; chunks that are too large dilute relevance, and chunks that are too small lose context. I try different embedding models ne-tuned for the domain. I add query transformation such as query rewriting or multi-query expansion before retrieval. I implement hybrid search combining vector similarity with keyword matching. I add a reranking step using a cross-encoder model that scores retrieved documents against the query. I also verify metadata filters are correct and that the vector store is populated with clean, representative documents. Real-time example: a legal assistant retrieves contracts instead of clauses. I reduce chunk size to 512 tokens with 128 overlap, switch to a domain-specific embedding model, add a keyword boost for clause titles, and use a reranker. Precision improves from 40 percent to 85 percent.
  • A chatbot does not remember the previous question. How do you add memory?: I add a chat memory advisor to the ChatClient. I configure MessageChatMemoryAdvisor with a ChatMemory implementation such as InMemoryChatMemory or a persistent store. I pass a conversation ID with each request so the advisor can retrieve and store messages for that session. For long conversations, I use VectorStoreChatMemoryAdvisor to retrieve semantically relevant past turns instead of sending the entire history. I also set a maximum history length to avoid exceeding the model's context window. Real-time example: a customer support bot forgets the user's order ID after one turn. I add MessageChatMemoryAdvisor to the ChatClient, generate a session ID when the conversation starts, and pass it in the advisors parameter. The bot now references earlier messages and asks clarifying questions only when needed.
  • A Spring AI application needs to answer questions about live order status. How do you design it?: I use tool calling to fetch live data. I define a @Tool method such as getOrderStatus(String orderId) that queries the order database or order service. I register the tool with ChatClient.defaultTools. When the user asks about an order, the model calls the tool with the order ID, the application returns the current status, and the model answers in natural language. I combine this with RAG for general policy or product questions. I also add validation to ensure users can only query their own orders, which prevents unauthorized data access. Real-time example: a user asks, Where is my order 12345? The ChatClient recognizes it needs order status, calls getOrderStatus("12345"), which returns {"status":"shipped","eta":"2025-08-10"}, and the model responds: Your order 12345 has been shipped and is expected to arrive on August 10.
  • A model's tool call fails because arguments are malformed. How do you debug it?: I inspect the chat response to see the exact tool name and arguments the model produced. Malformed arguments often happen when the tool description or parameter names are ambiguous. I improve the @Tool method name and add @ToolParameter descriptions so the model understands the expected format. I validate tool inputs and return clear error messages so the model can retry with corrected arguments. I also log every tool invocation and response for debugging. If the issue persists, I switch to a more capable model or use constrained output for critical tools. Real-time example: a weather tool receives dates in different formats. I update the @ToolParameter description to say ISO- 8601 date only, add input validation, and return an error like Invalid date format, expected YYYY-MM-DD when the format is wrong. The model corrects itself on the next call.
  • An AI application exposes sensitive internal tools to the model. How do you secure it?: I apply the principle of least privilege to tool registration. Only tools that are necessary for the use case are registered with ChatClient. Tools that perform destructive or sensitive operations require authorization checks inside the tool method, verifying the user's identity and permissions. I avoid registering admin-only tools on public-facing chatbots. I also add a SafeGuardAdvisor or custom advisor to review tool calls and block dangerous operations. Tools should be idempotent or reversible when possible, and all invocations should be logged for audit. Real-time example: a support chatbot could accidentally call a deleteUserAccount tool. I remove that tool from the public ChatClient, wrap remaining tools with permission checks that verify the caller is an authorized admin, and log every tool call with user ID and arguments.
  • A Spring AI service streams responses to a frontend but the output is choppy. What do you check?: I check whether the issue is on the model side or the network side. Streaming from the model returns tokens as they are generated, so latency is expected. Choppy output may be caused by buffering in the web layer or frontend. I ensure the Spring controller returns a Flux and does not collect the entire response before sending. I verify the client consumes the stream correctly with Server-Sent Events or WebFlux. I also check model parameters; some models or configurations buffer more than others. If the backend is buffering, I return the Flux directly and avoid calling block(). Real-time example: a React frontend receives the entire response at once. The Spring controller returns ResponseEntity by calling content(). I change it to return Flux from chatClient.prompt().user(question).stream().content(), and the frontend now shows typing in real time.
  • A RAG pipeline returns too many chunks and exceeds the context window. How do you limit it?: I reduce the number of top-K documents retrieved, typically to 3 or 5. I also reduce chunk size so more chunks t within the context window. I add a compression step using a query compressor to remove redundant content. I use a reranker to select only the most relevant chunks before injecting them into the prompt. I can also use a contextual query augmenter that summarizes or filters chunks. If the answer still needs more context, I implement a multi-turn strategy where the model asks follow-up questions to re ne retrieval. Real-time example: a technical documentation assistant retrieves 10 large chunks and over ows the context window. I set topK to 4, reduce chunk size to 300 tokens, and add a reranker. The prompt now ts and the answers remain accurate.
  • A Spring AI application switches from OpenAI to Azure OpenAI. What changes are needed?: Because Spring AI abstracts the model provider, the main change is dependency and configuration. I replace the OpenAI starter with the Azure OpenAI starter and update properties such as spring.ai.azure.openai.api-key, endpoint, and deployment-name. The ChatClient code, advisors, and tools usually remain unchanged. I verify that model capabilities such as JSON mode or tool calling are supported by the chosen Azure deployment. I also update environment-specific configuration without changing the application logic. This portability is one of the main benefits of using Spring AI. Real-time example: a company moves from OpenAI to Azure OpenAI for enterprise compliance. I change the Maven dependency, update application.yml with the Azure endpoint and deployment name, and rerun tests. The same service now calls Azure without any Java code changes.
  • A custom advisor in Spring AI needs to log all prompts and responses. How do you implement it?: I create a class that implements CallAdvisor or StreamAdvisor, depending on whether I need to intercept synchronous or streaming calls. In the around method, I access the ChatClientRequest, log the prompt text and metadata, proceed to the next advisor or model, receive the ChatClientResponse, and log the response content. I set an order value so the logging advisor runs early in the request chain and late in the response chain. I register it with the ChatClient builder as a default advisor. I avoid logging sensitive user data unless required and ensure logs comply with privacy policies. Real-time example: a compliance team requires an audit trail of AI interactions. I implement a LoggingAdvisor that writes prompt and response to SLF4J with conversation ID. I set order to LOWEST_PRECEDENCE so it wraps the entire chain, and I register it as a default advisor. Audit logs now contain request and response for every AI call.
  • An application uses RAG but users get different answers for the same question. How do you stabilize it?: Variability can come from the embedding model, the retrieval algorithm, the LLM, or the prompt. I fix retrieval by setting deterministic parameters such as a fixed top-K and a consistent similarity threshold. I use a reranker to make scoring more stable. I use a deterministic LLM with temperature set to 0 when consistency is more important than creativity. I version the prompt template and the document corpus so the same query produces the same answer against the same data version. I also add citations so users can verify the source. Real-time example: a support bot gives inconsistent refund policy answers. I set topK=5, temperature=0, add a reranker, and pin the embedding model version. I also include citations in the response. The same question now returns nearly identical answers.
  • A tool-calling agent calls the same tool repeatedly in a loop. How do you stop it?: I configure a maximum number of tool call iterations on the ToolCallingAdvisor or in application logic. I improve tool descriptions so the model understands when to stop. I add a guard advisor that detects loops and interrupts them. I ensure tools return concise, complete answers rather than partial data that forces repeated calls. For complex work ows, I break the task into explicit steps instead of relying on the model to coordinate everything through tools. Real-time example: an agent calling searchProduct repeatedly with slightly different keywords. I add a loop limit of 5, update the tool description to say call only once and re ne the query internally, and add an advisor that aborts if the same tool is called with the same arguments twice. The loop stops.
  • A vector search returns results but the model ignores them and answers from its training data. How do you fix it?: I improve the system prompt to explicitly instruct the model to answer only from the provided context and to say it does not know if the answer is not in the context. I make the retrieved context prominent in the prompt, usually by placing it before the user question. I use a clear delimiter such as "Context:" and "Question:" to help the model distinguish sources. I also reduce the temperature so the model is less creative and more grounded. If the model still ignores context, I switch to a model that follows instructions better or ne-tune with examples of grounded answers. Real-time example: a medical assistant answers with generic advice despite retrieved clinical guidelines. I update the system message to "You are a clinical assistant. Answer strictly from the provided guidelines. If the answer is not in the context, say 'I do not have that information.'" and place the context first. The answers now stick to the retrieved documents.
  • A Spring AI application must handle high request volume. What do you optimize?: I use streaming to reduce time-to- rst-byte and avoid blocking threads. I configure connection pooling for the model provider HTTP client. I cache similar requests where appropriate. I add rate limiting and backpressure to prevent overwhelming the provider API. For RAG, I precompute embeddings and keep the vector store query fast. I use smaller models for simple tasks and reserve large models for complex reasoning. I also consider running a local model such as Ollama for non-critical traffic to reduce latency and cost. Horizontal scaling of the Spring Boot service behind a load balancer handles concurrent users. Real-time example: a content generation API receives 1000 requests per minute. I enable streaming responses, add a 1000-request-per-minute rate limiter, cache prompts with deterministic parameters, and scale the service to ve replicas. Latency drops and costs remain controlled.
  • A chatbot needs to switch language based on the user's message. How do you implement it?: I can use a TranslationQueryTransformer before retrieval if the knowledge base is in one language. If the model itself supports multilingual responses, I detect the language from the user message, add a system instruction to respond in that language, and proceed normally. For RAG, I either translate the query to the document language before retrieval or retrieve documents in the detected language using metadata filters. Spring AI advisors make it easy to add a translation step in the modular RAG pipeline. Real-time example: users ask questions in English, Spanish, and German. I add a language detection step, append "Respond in the same language as the user" to the system prompt, and translate the query to English before vector search because the knowledge base is in English. The model answers in the user's language using retrieved English content.
  • An advisor needs to add user identity context to every prompt. How do you write it?: I implement a CallAdvisor and StreamAdvisor that retrieves the current user identity from the security context and injects it into the prompt or context map. In the advise method, I access the ChatClientRequest, extract the user principal, and either add a system message with user details or update the advisor context with user attributes. The next advisors and the model can use this context. I register the advisor early in the chain so subsequent advisors such as RAG or tool calling can also use the identity. I ensure sensitive user data is handled according to privacy requirements. Real-time example: an enterprise assistant needs to know the user's department to filter documents. I write a UserContextAdvisor that reads the authenticated user from Spring Security, adds a context parameter "department=engineering", and a later RAG advisor uses that parameter to filter vector search results. Responses are personalized and scoped.
  • A RAG system needs to cite sources in its answers. How do you add citations?: I include metadata such as source lename, page number, and URL in the retrieved chunks. In the prompt, I instruct the model to cite the source after each claim using the metadata provided. I use a structured prompt template that includes the context chunks with identifiers such as [1], [2], and asks the model to reference those identifiers. The post-processing step can map identifiers back to full source URLs and display them to the user. Spring AI's document metadata ows through the advisors, so the model has access to the citation fields. Real-time example: a documentation assistant needs to show source links. Each chunk has metadata {"source":"/docs/spring-ai.md","page":42}. The prompt includes these identifiers. The model answers: "Spring AI supports multiple model providers [1]." The frontend maps [1] to the URL and displays it as a clickable citation.
  • A generative AI feature returns nonsensical output under load. What could be wrong?: Under load, the issue may be context window over ow due to long prompts, concurrency limits on the model provider, thread exhaustion in the application, or cache inconsistency. I check the prompt size and reduce retrieved context or chat history. I monitor provider rate limits and errors. I verify that the Spring Boot application uses reactive or properly pooled resources and does not block event loops. I also check whether the model temperature or sampling parameters change behavior at scale if requests are batched differently. I add retries with backoff for transient provider errors and circuit breakers for provider outages. Real-time example: during a product launch, an AI summary endpoint returns garbled text. Investigation shows the vector store query times out under load and returns empty results, so the model answers without context. I add a cache for common queries, scale the vector store, and add a fallback that returns a default message instead of calling the model with no context.
  • A Spring AI application needs to evaluate the quality of generated answers. How do you design evaluation?: I implement an evaluation pipeline that compares generated answers against reference answers or ground truth. Metrics include relevance, faithfulness to retrieved context, answer correctness, and hallucination detection. I use LLM-based evaluators that score responses with structured output. I also collect user feedback with thumbs up or thumbs down and store it for analysis. I run evaluations on a held-out test set before deploying prompt or model changes. Spring AI provides evaluation utilities, and custom advisors can log inputs and outputs for offline analysis. Real-time example: a legal Q&A system needs to verify answer quality. I build a batch evaluator that sends 500 known questions to the chatbot, compares answers to reference answers using an LLM judge, and reports precision, recall, and faithfulness scores. Changes that drop scores below a threshold are blocked from deployment.
  • A company wants to use a local open-source model instead of a cloud provider. How do you do it with Spring AI?: I use Ollama or a similar local model server. I add the spring-ai-ollama-spring-boot-starter dependency and configure spring.ai.ollama.base-url and spring.ai.ollama.chat.options.model. Spring AI abstracts the provider, so ChatClient code remains the same. The local model runs on company infrastructure, keeping data on premises. This is useful for regulated data or cost control. The trade-off is that local models may be less capable than large cloud models, so they work best for simpler tasks or as a fallback. I also ensure the hardware has sufficient GPU or CPU resources to run the model efficiently. Real-time example: a healthcare company cannot send patient data to cloud LLMs. I deploy a Llama model with Ollama on an on-premise GPU server, configure Spring AI to point to the local Ollama endpoint, and use the same RAG and tool-calling advisors. Patient data never leaves the data center.
Side by side

Comparison Tables

The concepts that interviewers love to ask "what's the difference between…" about, laid out so you can scan the answer in seconds.

Spring Cloud

GatewayFilter vs GlobalFilter

Feature GatewayFilter GlobalFilter
Scope Route-specific. Applies only to the individual routes where it is configured via filter factories. Global. Applies unconditionally to all routes in the Gateway.
Use Case Modifying requests/responses for specific downstream services (e.g., Adding a specific header for Auth service). Cross-cutting concerns like metrics, global logging, global authentication, or load balancing.
Implementation Implemented by extending AbstractGatewayFilterFactory. Implemented by implementing the GlobalFilter interface.
Messaging

Apache Kafka vs RabbitMQ

Feature Apache Kafka RabbitMQ
Architecture Distributed commit log (pub/sub stream processing). Traditional message broker (smart broker / dumb consumer).
Message Storage Messages are persisted to disk and retained based on a time/size policy. Can be re-read. Messages are transient. Deleted immediately after being consumed and acknowledged.
Routing Very basic routing (publish to a topic partition). Complex routing capabilities (Direct, Topic, Fanout exchanges).
Throughput Extremely high (millions of messages per second). High (tens of thousands of messages per second), but struggles with massive scale.
Spring Boot

@Controller vs @RestController

Feature @Controller @RestController
Primary Use Case Traditional Spring MVC applications where the server renders HTML views (e.g., Thymeleaf, JSP). Modern RESTful APIs where the server returns data (JSON/XML).
Return Value Methods return a `String` representing the view name to be resolved by the ViewResolver. Methods return domain objects directly, which are serialized to JSON/XML.
@ResponseBody Requires `@ResponseBody` on every method if you want to return JSON instead of a view. A convenience annotation that combines `@Controller` + `@ResponseBody`. You don't need to add `@ResponseBody`.
Spring Boot

Mono vs Flux

Feature Mono<T> Flux<T>
Cardinality Emits 0 or 1 element. Emits 0 to N elements (potentially infinite).
Use Case Fetching a single user by ID, saving a record, or making a single HTTP request. Fetching a list of users, streaming real-time events (Server-Sent Events), or processing a large file chunk by chunk.
RxJS Equivalent Similar to Java's `Optional` or `CompletableFuture`, but reactive. Similar to Java's `Stream`, but asynchronous and non-blocking.
Spring Boot

@Bean vs @Component

Feature @Bean @Component
Where is it used? Applied on methods inside a `@Configuration` class. Applied on classes directly.
Control over instantiation You manually instantiate the object in the method body. Great for 3rd-party libraries. Spring automatically instantiates the class via component scanning.
Source Code Access Required when you don't have access to the source code (e.g. creating a `RestTemplate` bean). Required when you own the source code and want Spring to automatically manage it.
Core Java

JVM vs JRE vs JDK

Aspect JVM JRE JDK
What it is Abstract execution engine JVM + core class libraries JRE + development tools
Purpose Runs bytecode Runs Java applications Develops + runs Java applications
Contains Class loader, bytecode verifier, execution engine JVM + java.lang, java.util, etc. JRE + javac, javadoc, jar, debugger
Platform dependent? Yes Yes Yes
Who needs it Anyone running Java End users running Java apps Developers writing Java code
OOP

Abstract Class vs Interface

Aspect Abstract Class Interface
Methods Can have abstract + concrete methods All abstract by default (Java 8+ allows default/static) —
Fields Instance variables allowed Only public static final constants —
Constructors Can have a constructor Cannot have a constructor —
Inheritance Single inheritance only (extends) A class can implement multiple interfaces —
When to use Shared base state/behavior among related classes Contract for unrelated classes to implement —
OOP

Method Overloading vs Overriding

Aspect Overloading Overriding
Definition Same method name, different parameters Subclass redefines a parent's method —
Binding Compile-time (static) Runtime (dynamic) —
Class relation Same class Parent-child (inheritance) —
Return type Can differ Must be same or covariant —
Access modifier Can differ freely Cannot be more restrictive than parent —
OOP

== vs equals() vs hashCode()

Method Compares Default behavior
== Reference (memory address) for objects; value for primitives Identity check
equals() Logical equality (if overridden) Same as == unless overridden
hashCode() Produces an int used by hash-based collections Derived from memory address unless overridden
Collections

ArrayList vs LinkedList

Aspect ArrayList LinkedList
Backing structure Dynamic array Doubly linked list
Random access (get) O(1) O(n)
Insert/delete at middle O(n) — shifts elements O(1) once node is found
Memory overhead Lower Higher (node pointers)
Best for Frequent reads, rare inserts Frequent inserts/deletes
Collections

HashMap vs Hashtable vs ConcurrentHashMap

Aspect HashMap Hashtable ConcurrentHashMap
Thread-safe No Yes (fully synchronized) Yes (segment/bucket-level locking)
Null keys/values 1 null key, many null values Not allowed Not allowed
Performance Fast (no locking) Slow (whole-map lock) Fast (fine-grained locking)
Introduced Java 1.2 Java 1.0 (legacy) Java 1.5 (java.util.concurrent)
Collections

Fail-Fast vs Fail-Safe Iterators

Aspect Fail-Fast Fail-Safe
Behavior on modification Throws ConcurrentModificationException Iterates over a clone/snapshot
Examples ArrayList, HashMap iterators CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap
Memory use Low (no copy) Higher (copy or snapshot)
Consistency Reflects live data May not reflect latest updates
Core Java

Checked vs Unchecked Exceptions

Aspect Checked Unchecked
Checked at Compile-time Runtime
Must be declared/handled? Yes (throws or try-catch) No
Extends Exception (not RuntimeException) RuntimeException
Examples IOException, SQLException NullPointerException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
Multithreading

Runnable vs Callable

Aspect Runnable Callable
Return value void — no result Returns a value (V)
Exceptions Cannot throw checked exceptions Can throw checked exceptions
Method run() call()
Used with Thread, ExecutorService ExecutorService (returns Future)
Multithreading

Thread vs Runnable (creating threads)

Aspect Extending Thread Implementing Runnable
Inheritance Uses up single inheritance slot Leaves class free to extend another class
Reusability Less flexible More flexible — can be shared across threads
Recommended? Only for simple cases Generally preferred
Core Java

String vs StringBuilder vs StringBuffer

Aspect String StringBuilder StringBuffer
Mutability Immutable Mutable Mutable
Thread-safe N/A (immutable) No Yes (synchronized)
Performance Slow for repeated concatenation Fast Slower than StringBuilder
Use case Fixed/rare-change text Single-threaded string building Multi-threaded string building
Spring Boot

Spring vs Spring Boot

Aspect Spring Framework Spring Boot
Configuration Mostly manual XML/Java config Auto-configuration, convention over configuration
Server Requires external server (e.g., Tomcat) deployment Embedded server (Tomcat/Jetty) — runnable JAR
Setup time Slower, more boilerplate Fast — starter dependencies
Microservices Possible but manual Built for it (Actuator, Cloud support)
Database

SQL vs NoSQL Databases

Aspect SQL (Relational) NoSQL
Schema Fixed, predefined schema Dynamic / schema-less
Scaling Vertical (scale-up) Horizontal (scale-out)
Data model Tables with rows/columns Document, key-value, column, graph
Consistency Strong (ACID) Often eventual (BASE)
Examples MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle MongoDB, Cassandra, Redis
Database

JPA vs Hibernate

Aspect JPA Hibernate
What it is A specification (interface) An implementation of JPA (+ extra features)
Vendor lock-in None — spec is provider-agnostic Tied to Hibernate-specific APIs if using extras
Query language JPQL HQL (Hibernate Query Language)
Testing

Unit Testing vs Integration Testing

Aspect Unit Testing Integration Testing
Scope Single class/method in isolation Multiple components working together
Speed Fast Slower
Dependencies Mocked (e.g., Mockito) Real or test-configured (e.g., in-memory DB)
Tooling JUnit, Mockito Spring Boot Test, Testcontainers
Git

Git Merge vs Rebase

Aspect Merge Rebase
History Preserves full branch history (merge commit) Rewrites commit history — linear
Commit graph Non-linear, shows branching Clean, linear log
Safety on shared branches Safe Risky — avoid rebasing public/shared history
Use case Integrating feature branches Cleaning up local commits before pushing

Practice

Interview Q&A

Common questions pulled from real interview patterns, answered concisely. Tap a question to reveal the answer.

"Say it out loud" mode (15-second verbal answers)
Enable Flashcard Review Mode (One by one)
1 / X

The JVM's class loader locates and loads MyClass.class, the bytecode verifier checks it for safety, then the execution engine (interpreter + JIT compiler) runs main(). The JIT compiles hot code paths to native machine code for speed.

"The ClassLoader loads the bytecode, the verifier checks it, and the execution engine runs it, using the JIT to compile hot paths into native machine code."

Follow-up Trap "Does the JVM interpret or compile?" — Say both. It interprets initially, then compiles hot code paths.

Immutability enables safe sharing in the String pool (saving memory), makes String safe to use as a HashMap key (stable hashCode), and makes it thread-safe by default since no thread can modify a shared instance.

"It makes Strings thread-safe, allows caching in the String Pool to save memory, and guarantees a stable hash code for use as map keys."

Follow-up Trap "How does it help HashMap specifically?" — If a String could change after insertion, its hashCode would change, and the map would never find the bucket again.

final is a keyword that locks variables, methods, or classes from being changed/overridden/extended. finally is a block that always executes after try-catch, used for cleanup. finalize() was a method called by the garbage collector before reclaiming an object (deprecated since Java 9).

"Final is a modifier to prevent changes, finally is a try-catch block for cleanup, and finalize is a deprecated GC callback method."

Follow-up Trap "Can a finally block ever NOT execute?" — Yes, if System.exit() is called, or if the JVM crashes/loses power.

Whenever you're building a string inside a loop or through many sequential appends. Each String concatenation creates a new object, which is O(n²) over a loop; StringBuilder mutates an internal buffer for O(n) total work.

"Use it inside loops. String concatenation creates a new object every time, which kills performance, while StringBuilder mutates a single internal array."

Follow-up Trap "Should you use StringBuffer instead?" — No, StringBuffer is synchronized and slower. Use StringBuilder unless you explicitly need thread safety.

GC runs automatically when the JVM decides heap pressure warrants it (e.g., Eden space fills up). System.gc() only *suggests* a collection to the JVM — it's not guaranteed to run immediately or at all.

Security: Strings are used as parameters for network connections, file paths, and database URLs; if they were mutable, they could be altered after security checks. Thread Safety: Immutable objects are inherently thread-safe and can be shared freely across threads. Performance/Memory: It enables the String Pool, where identical string literals share the same memory address, massively reducing memory overhead.

The == operator compares object references, meaning it checks if both references point to the exact same memory location. The .equals() method, when properly overridden, compares the actual value or content of the objects. For primitives, == compares their values.

You break the general contract between the two methods, which states that two objects that are equal according to equals() must have the same hash code. If you violate this, your objects will not function correctly when stored in hash-based collections like HashMap or HashSet, often leading to "lost" data.

final is a modifier: applied to a variable it makes it a constant, to a method it prevents overriding, and to a class it prevents inheritance. finally is a block used in try-catch structures that is guaranteed to execute regardless of whether an exception is thrown, usually used for closing resources. finalize() is a method called by the Garbage Collector before an object is destroyed (deprecated in Java 9+ due to unpredictability and performance issues).

Java uses a generational garbage collector dividing the heap into Young, Old, and Metaspace generations. It operates on the premise that most objects die young. The GC identifies "live" objects tracing from GC Roots (local variables, active threads, static fields). Any object unreachable from these roots is considered "dead" and its memory is reclaimed automatically.

To avoid the 'diamond problem' — ambiguity when two parent classes define a method with the same signature and the compiler can't determine which one a subclass should inherit. Java sidesteps this by allowing multiple interface implementation instead, where Java 8+ default method conflicts must be resolved explicitly.

Polymorphism lets an object take many forms. Compile-time (static) polymorphism is method overloading, resolved at compile time. Runtime (dynamic) polymorphism is method overriding, resolved at runtime via the actual object type.

Yes — commonly used in the Singleton pattern to prevent external instantiation, forcing all access through a static factory method that controls how many instances exist.

Inheritance models an 'is-a' relationship (Dog is an Animal) and is fixed at compile time. Composition models a 'has-a' relationship (Car has an Engine) and is more flexible — favored in modern design since it avoids tight coupling to a parent's implementation.

Polymorphism is the ability of an object to take on many forms. Java supports Compile-time polymorphism (Method Overloading: same method name, different parameters) and Runtime polymorphism (Method Overriding: subclass provides a specific implementation of a superclass method). Runtime polymorphism is achieved via dynamic method dispatch.

No, you cannot override a static method. If you declare a static method in a subclass with the same signature as a static method in the superclass, it "hides" the superclass method. This is known as Method Hiding. Because static methods are bound to the class at compile-time (static binding), runtime polymorphism does not apply.

An abstract class can hold state (instance variables) and constructors, whereas an interface cannot. A class can extend only one abstract class, but it can implement multiple interfaces. Since Java 8, interfaces can have default and static methods with implementations, blurring the lines, but they still cannot maintain instance state.

Encapsulation is the bundling of data (attributes) and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (a class), while hiding the internal state from the outside world using access modifiers (e.g., private). It protects data integrity by preventing direct modification and allows the internal implementation to change without breaking external code.

The "L" in SOLID principles. It states that objects of a superclass should be replaceable with objects of its subclasses without breaking the application. In Java, this means a subclass must fulfill the contract defined by its superclass. If a subclass overrides a method and throws an unexpected UnsupportedOperationException, it violates LSP.

HashMap was designed later (Java 1.2, non-legacy) with more lenient null handling as a convenience; Hashtable predates that design decision and throws NullPointerException for null keys/values by design.

Each bucket holds a linked list of entries that hash to the same index. Since Java 8, if a bucket's list grows beyond a threshold (8), it converts to a red-black tree for O(log n) worst-case lookup instead of O(n).

When you need keys sorted (natural order or a custom Comparator) or need range operations like firstKey()/higherKey(). You trade O(1) average lookup for O(log n) but gain ordering.

It's thrown when a collection is structurally modified while being iterated with a fail-fast iterator. Avoid it by using an Iterator's own remove() method, iterating over a copy, or using a concurrent collection like CopyOnWriteArrayList.

synchronized provides mutual exclusion (only one thread executes the block) AND visibility. volatile only guarantees visibility (reads always see the latest write) — it doesn't provide atomicity for compound operations like i++.

A bug that occurs when multiple threads access shared mutable state concurrently and the outcome depends on unpredictable thread scheduling — e.g., two threads incrementing a counter can lose an update if not synchronized.

It manages a pool of reusable threads instead of paying thread-creation cost per task, gives you back a Future for results/exceptions, and centralizes lifecycle control (shutdown, timeout) instead of scattering it across the code.

Two or more threads each hold a lock the other needs, and neither releases it — so both wait forever. Classic cause: acquiring multiple locks in inconsistent order across threads.

Spring is a lightweight Java framework for building enterprise applications. It gives you a container that manages objects called beans and connects them through dependency injection. In a banking system, Spring can manage your AccountService, TransactionRepository, and PaymentGateway objects without you manually creating them with new.

Spring reduces boilerplate code, supports transactions, integrates with databases and messaging, and makes unit testing easy through dependency injection. For example, in a supply chain application, you can swap a real ShippingClient with a mock during tests because Spring injects the dependency from outside.

Core modules include Spring Core (beans and DI), Spring AOP (cross-cutting concerns), Spring Data Access (JDBC, JPA), Spring MVC/Web (REST and web apps), Spring Security (authentication and authorization), and Spring Test. A banking ledger app might use Core, Data Access, and Security together.

Spring does not force you to use heavy application servers or inherit from specific classes. You use plain Java objects called POJOs and configure them with annotations or XML. This makes a payment microservice small and fast to start.

Inversion of Control means the Spring container controls object creation and wiring instead of your code doing it. You declare what you need, and Spring provides it. In a supply chain order service, instead of the service creating a WarehouseClient, Spring injects a ready-made one.

You can use Spring Initializr (start.spring.io) in a browser, the Spring Boot CLI, IDE plugins in IntelliJ IDEA or Eclipse, or manually create a Maven/Gradle project. In a banking team, developers usually use Spring Initializr to generate a project with spring-boot-starter-web and spring-boot-starter-data-jpa.

Spring Initializr is a web tool that generates a ready-to-import Spring Boot project. You pick the language, build tool, Spring Boot version, and dependencies, and it creates the project structure, pom.xml, and main class. It saves time when starting a new payment API service.

A typical project has src/main/java for code, src/main/resources for config and static files, src/test/java for tests, a main class with @SpringBootApplication, and application.properties or application.yml. In a supply chain app, packages are often controller, service, repository, model, and config.

The main class contains the main method and is annotated with

@SpringBootApplication. Calling SpringApplication.run() starts the Spring

context, triggers auto-configuration, begins component scanning, and starts the embedded server. It is the single entry point for a banking microservice.

The default embedded server is Tomcat. Spring Boot also supports Jetty and Undertow. You can switch by excluding Tomcat and adding the Jetty starter. This is useful in high-throughput supply chain APIs where Jetty's threading model may perform better.

A Spring Bean is any Java object that is created, configured, and managed by the Spring IoC container. Beans are usually annotated with @Component, @Service, @Repository, or

@Controller. In a banking app, a FundTransferService is a bean that Spring injects

wherever it is needed.

The common scopes are singleton (one instance per context), prototype (new instance on every request), request (one per HTTP request), and session (one per HTTP session). Singleton is the default. A supply chain CurrencyConverter might be singleton, while a per-request OrderBuilder could be prototype.

The default scope is singleton. Spring creates exactly one instance of the bean and shares it across the entire application context. This is efficient for stateless services, such as a banking InterestCalculationService.

No, singleton beans are not automatically thread-safe just because Spring creates one instance. If the bean holds mutable shared state, multiple threads can corrupt it. You should make beans stateless or use synchronization. A banking AccountBalanceCache must use thread-safe collections or locks.

Spring creates the bean, injects dependencies, runs initialization callbacks like

@PostConstruct, makes it available for use, and finally calls destruction callbacks like 
@PreDestroy when the context closes. In a supply chain app, you might use 
@PostConstruct to load region codes into memory on startup.

All four create Spring beans, but they signal different layers. @Component is generic.

@Service marks business logic. @Repository marks data access and enables exception

translation. @Controller marks web controllers. A banking app uses @Service for PaymentService and @Repository for TransactionRepository.

@Component is used on classes that Spring scans automatically. @Bean is used on

methods inside a @Configuration class to create beans manually, often for third-party objects you cannot annotate. For example, you might define a @Bean for a banking SDK client that is not under your control.

Dependency Injection means an object receives its dependencies from outside instead of creating them itself. Spring provides the dependencies at runtime. In a supply chain app, OrderService does not create InventoryClient; Spring injects it, so the service is easier to test and change.

Spring supports constructor injection, setter injection, and field injection. Constructor injection is recommended because it makes dependencies required and immutable. Field injection is easy but hides dependencies. In a banking LoanApprovalService, constructor injection ensures all required services are present at startup.

Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit, allows immutable objects, makes unit testing easy, and prevents a bean from being created with missing dependencies. It also avoids the circular dependency problems that constructor injection itself detects at startup.

@Autowired tells Spring to inject a matching bean automatically by type. It can be

placed on a constructor, setter, or field. If multiple beans match, you can resolve the conflict with

@Qualifier or @Primary. In a banking app, @Autowired injects a

NotificationSender into a PaymentService.

@Primary marks one bean as the default choice when multiple beans of the same type

exist. @Qualifier is used at the injection point to pick a specific bean by name. If you have both EmailNotificationSender and SmsNotificationSender, you can mark one

@Primary or use @Qualifier("email").

A circular dependency happens when Bean A depends on Bean B and Bean B depends on Bean A. Spring fails to start with constructor injection. You can fix it by redesigning the classes, using setter or field injection, or using @Lazy. In a banking app, separating a PaymentService and AuditService via events can break the cycle.

Inversion of Control is the principle where the framework manages object creation and flow. Dependency Injection is one way to implement IoC by supplying dependencies from outside. So DI is the mechanism, and IoC is the broader design goal behind it.

Spring is the core framework that provides IoC, transactions, data access, and web support. Spring Boot is built on top of Spring and removes boilerplate by providing auto- configuration, embedded servers, and starter dependencies. In simple words, Spring gives you the engine, and Spring Boot gives you a ready-to-drive car.

Spring Boot reduces setup time with auto-configuration, starter POMs, embedded servers, and production-ready monitoring through Actuator. It lets a banking team create a working REST API in minutes instead of spending hours on XML configuration.

Starters are convenient dependency bundles. spring-boot-starter-web includes Spring MVC, Tomcat, and Jackson. spring-boot-starter-data-jpa includes Hibernate, Spring Data JPA, and a connection pool. For a supply chain app, adding one starter pulls in everything needed for a layer.

No, Spring Boot does not replace Spring. It makes Spring easier to use by providing defaults and conventions. Underneath, Spring Boot still uses Spring Core, Spring MVC, Spring Data, and other Spring modules.

You might choose plain Spring when you need full control over every configuration detail, when you deploy to an external application server as a WAR, or when integrating with a legacy codebase that already has custom Spring setup. Most modern banking microservices, however, use Spring Boot.

Auto-configuration automatically sets up Spring beans based on the dependencies present in your classpath. If spring-boot-starter-data-jpa is present, Spring Boot configures a DataSource and EntityManagerFactory for you. You can override defaults in application.properties. In a banking app, this means the database connection is ready without manual bean definitions.

Spring Boot reads META-INF/spring.factories or META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfigu ration.imports to find auto-configuration classes. Each class is decorated with

@Conditional annotations, such as @ConditionalOnClass and 
@ConditionalOnMissingBean, so beans are only created when appropriate. If your supply

chain app adds Redis, the Redis auto-configuration kicks in.

@SpringBootApplication is a convenience annotation that combines 
@Configuration, @EnableAutoConfiguration, and @ComponentScan. It marks the

main class, enables auto-configuration, and tells Spring where to look for components. Without it, you would need three separate annotations.

The ApplicationContext is the Spring IoC container. It holds bean definitions, creates beans, wires dependencies, and provides services like event publishing and internationalization. When a banking application starts, SpringApplication.run() creates the ApplicationContext.

BeanFactory is the basic container with lazy initialization and minimal features. ApplicationContext extends it with eager initialization, event propagation, AOP support, and easier integration. Modern Spring Boot apps always use ApplicationContext.

Spring Boot creates the application context, loads property sources, runs ApplicationRunner and CommandLineRunner beans, starts the embedded web server, and enables auto-configuration. By the end, your banking payment API is listening on its configured port.

Component scanning is how Spring automatically discovers beans in specified packages.

@ComponentScan tells Spring to look for @Component, @Service, @Repository, and 
@Controller annotations. In a supply chain app, Spring scans

com.company.supplychain.* and registers all discovered beans.

You can use @SpringBootApplication(exclude = DataSourceAutoConfiguration.class) or set spring.autoconfigure.exclude in application.properties. This is useful when a banking app needs to turn off default datasource setup because it uses a custom connection factory.

Conditional annotations control when beans are created. @ConditionalOnClass creates a bean only if a class is on the classpath. @ConditionalOnProperty creates it only if a property matches. @ConditionalOnMissingBean creates it only if the bean is not already defined. These make auto-configuration smart and safe.

Inject the ApplicationContext and call getBeanDefinitionNames(). Spring Boot Actuator also provides the /beans endpoint. This helps during debugging when a banking app fails to start due to a missing bean.

Maven is a build automation and dependency management tool for Java. It standardizes project structure, downloads required libraries from repositories, and provides lifecycle phases like compile, test, package, and deploy. In a banking project, Maven ensures every developer uses the same Spring Boot version.

pom.xml is the Project Object Model file. It contains project metadata, dependencies, plugins, build settings, and parent POM references. It is the recipe Maven follows to build the project. A supply chain microservice pom.xml lists starters like spring-boot-starter-web and spring-boot-starter-test.

The default lifecycle includes validate, compile, test, package, verify, install, and deploy. The clean lifecycle removes generated files. Running mvn clean install cleans old output, compiles code, runs tests, packages a JAR, and installs it in the local repository.

mvn clean deletes the target directory from previous builds. mvn install compiles, tests, packages, and copies the artifact to your local Maven repository. In a banking CI pipeline, you usually run mvn clean install to get a fresh, tested artifact.

Compile-scope dependencies are needed to compile and run the application, such as spring-boot-starter-web. Test-scope dependencies are only needed during testing, such as JUnit and Mockito. They are not included in the final production artifact.

The local repository is a folder on your machine, usually ~/.m2/repository, where Maven stores downloaded jars. When a teammate in a banking team adds a new dependency, Maven first checks this local cache before downloading from the internet or a corporate Nexus.

A repository manager is a private server that stores internal artifacts and proxies public repositories. Banks use Nexus or Artifactory to share private libraries across teams, control versions, and improve build speed by caching external dependencies.

dependencyManagement declares dependency versions centrally without adding them to the project directly. Child modules or dependencies can then omit the version. Spring Boot's parent POM uses this to align all starter versions automatically.

You add an block inside the tag. For example, if a banking library pulls in an old version of Jackson that conflicts with Spring Boot, you exclude it so Spring Boot's managed version is used.
