It does not load the full application context, so web components and services are excluded. You write tests that save entities through the repository and assert that custom query methods return the expected results. Because @DataJpaTest is transactional, each test runs inside a transaction that rolls back automatically, leaving the database clean. For complex queries, an in-memory database may behave differently from production, so many teams switch to Testcontainers with the real database vendor.

Testcontainers is a Java library that starts lightweight, throwaway Docker containers for databases, message brokers, and other services during tests. For repository tests, it lets you run PostgreSQL, MySQL, or Oracle inside a container instead of relying on H2. This eliminates subtle differences in SQL dialect, transaction behavior, and vendor-specific features between the test database and production. The container starts once per test class, tests run against the real database, and the container is stopped after the tests finish. The trade-off is slower startup, but the confidence gained is worth it for queries that use native SQL, JSON columns, or stored procedures.

You define a container as a static field annotated with @Container, start it in

@BeforeAll, and set Spring DataSource properties so the test context connects to the running

container. With the latest Spring Boot and Testcontainers integration, you can also use

@ServiceConnection so Spring Boot automatically resolves the connection details from the

container. You add the database driver and Testcontainers dependency, and you may reuse the same container across tests with a singleton pattern or the Testcontainers reuse feature to reduce startup time. The key is to keep container lifecycle separate from the test transaction so the container keeps running while individual tests still roll back.

@WebMvcTest is a slice test annotation that loads only the Spring MVC web layer. It

auto-configures MockMvc, registers @Controller and @ControllerAdvice beans, and excludes the full service, repository, and component scan unless explicitly imported. It is ideal for testing REST controllers because you can simulate HTTP requests, validate status codes, response bodies, and exception handling without starting an embedded server. Collaborating services are

provided with @MockBean. A common mistake is expecting security filters to be fully configured;

while Spring Security filters are registered, authentication must be mocked or disabled depending on the test intent.

MockMvc is a Spring testing utility that dispatches HTTP requests through the DispatcherServlet without starting a real servlet container. It tests controller behavior, binding, validation, exception handling, and filter interactions in a lightweight way. TestRestTemplate and WebTestClient are used with @SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = RANDOM_PORT) to send real HTTP requests to an embedded server. MockMvc is faster and more focused, while the real-client approach verifies the full stack including actual serialization and container filters. For most controller tests, MockMvc is sufficient; reserve the real server approach for integration tests that need end-to-end HTTP semantics.

@SpringBootTest loads the complete Spring Boot application context and is used for

full integration tests. Its webEnvironment attribute controls how the web layer is started. MOCK loads a web context but does not start a real server, RANDOM_PORT starts an embedded server on a random port, DEFINED_PORT uses the configured server.port, and NONE does not start a web environment. RANDOM_PORT is the safest choice for integration tests because it avoids port conflicts on CI and lets TestRestTemplate or WebTestClient send real requests. MOCK is useful when you want the web beans configured but prefer MockMvc.

The Spring TestContext framework manages the application context for tests, including dependency injection, transaction management, and context caching. When multiple test classes use the same context configuration, Spring reuses the cached context instead of creating a new one, which saves significant startup time. However, adding or replacing beans with

@MockBean, @SpyBean, or different @TestPropertySource values changes the context key

and forces a new context to load. A suite that uses many different @MockBean combinations can suffer from context cache pollution and slow builds, so it is worth grouping tests that share the same context.

Asynchronous methods are usually annotated with @Async and executed by a TaskExecutor. In a unit test, you call the method and use CompletableFuture.get() or Future.get() with a timeout to wait for the result. In an integration test, you may inject the executor and wait for active tasks to finish, or use Awaitility to poll until the expected side effect occurs. A common mistake is asserting immediately after the async call, which usually fails because the task has not run yet. You should also test the error path by verifying that exceptions are handled by the returned Future or by an AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler for void async methods.

JaCoCo is a Java code coverage library that instruments bytecode during test execution and reports how much of the code was executed. It measures instruction coverage, branch coverage, line coverage, method coverage, and class coverage. The HTML report shows which lines were covered and which branches were missed, while the XML report is consumed by CI tools like SonarQube. Coverage is a useful signal but not a goal by itself; 100 percent line coverage with weak assertions is misleading because the code can be exercised without being verified. JaCoCo should be paired with meaningful assertions and reviewed alongside the test suite quality.

You configure exclusions in the JaCoCo Maven or Gradle plugin using patterns such as **/config/*, **/dto/**, or **/*Exception. Lombok-generated methods can be excluded with the lombok.addLombokGeneratedAnnotation = true setting, which marks generated code so JaCoCo ignores it. Exclusions are useful for boilerplate such as equals and hashCode, mapping classes, or generated sources, but excluding real business logic to hit a coverage target is a harmful practice. The goal is to reduce noise, not to game the metric.

Parameterized tests let you run the same test logic with multiple sets of inputs. You can use @ValueSource for simple values, @CsvSource for tabular data, @MethodSource for complex objects, or @ArgumentsSource for custom suppliers. They are useful for validation rules, pricing calculations, or any logic that must behave consistently across many cases. For example, a single parameterized test can check that a discount calculator returns the correct amount for customer tiers GOLD, SILVER, and BRONZE. Without parameterized tests, you would copy- paste nearly identical test methods, which is hard to maintain.

@MockBean completely replaces a bean in the Spring context with a Mockito mock, so

calls return stubbed values by default. @SpyBean wraps the real bean, so real behavior runs unless a specific method is stubbed. Use @MockBean when the real implementation should not execute, such as an external payment gateway or a repository that is not needed for a controller test. Use

@SpyBean when you want to verify that the real bean was called or when you need to override one

method of an otherwise real service. Spies are useful but can make tests harder to debug because the real code runs and may trigger unexpected side effects.

Each test should start from a clean, well-defined state. Use the transactional rollback behavior of @Transactional on tests so database changes are undone. For data that must survive across transactions, such as when testing a service that calls

@Transactional(propagation = REQUIRES_NEW), use TestEntityManager or a

custom cleanup in @AfterEach to delete rows. Avoid relying on data created by a previous test because it makes execution order matter and causes flaky failures. Some teams use SQL scripts or libraries like Flyway for baseline schema and small seed data sets, but test-specific data should be created and cleaned up by the test itself.

I first look at any date or timestamp assertions in the test and in the production code. If the test creates an expected value using LocalDateTime.now() or new Date() and compares it against a value returned by the service, the comparison will fail when CI runs in a different zone or

at a different millisecond. The fix is to avoid comparing against wall-clock time in unit tests;

instead, inject a Clock bean or use a fixed timestamp provided by the test. For integration tests, I ensure the JVM timezone and database timezone are explicit, for example by setting -Duser.timezone=UTC in the CI pipeline and using TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE columns. I also check whether the test relies on the current date for filtering and replace it with a controlled reference date.

The cause is usually a test that commits data and does not clean up, or a non-transactional operation that leaks state. @DataJpaTest is transactional by default, so most tests roll back, but a test calling a service method annotated with @Transactional(propagation = REQUIRES_NEW) commits in a new transaction and leaves data behind. Another cause is a @Sql script that inserts shared rows without cleanup, or a test that uses native JdbcTemplate without a transaction. I find the offending test by running the suite with logging enabled and inspecting the database state after each test class. The fix is to add explicit cleanup in @AfterEach, use unique identifiers per test, or refactor the production code so tests can rely on automatic rollback.

An empty body usually means the controller returns a response object but it is not serialized, or the mock did not return the expected object. I first verify that the controller method is actually returning the object and that @RestController or @ResponseBody is present. Then I check the mocked service; if when(service.findById(1L)).thenReturn(order) uses a different argument than the controller passes, Mockito returns null and Jackson serializes an empty body. I also check that the object has getters, because Jackson needs them. Finally, I inspect the MockMvc result to see whether an exception was swallowed, and I enable debug logging for org.springframework.web to trace the request through the filters.

UnnecessaryStubbingException is thrown when strict stubbing is enabled and a stubbed interaction was never used during the test. After the refactor, the production code may no longer call the stubbed method, or the test may have stubbed a method that was only relevant to an older implementation. I review the test to remove unused stubs and to confirm that the remaining stubs are actually required by the scenario being tested. If strict stubbing is too noisy for a class with shared setup, I either split the test into smaller focused tests or apply Mockito.lenient() only where a shared stub is acceptable. The goal is to keep the test honest about what it is verifying.

The actuator endpoint is not loaded because @SpringBootTest does not auto- configure actuator unless the actuator starter is on the classpath and the endpoint is enabled. I check that spring-boot-starter-actuator is a dependency and that the management.endpoints.web.exposure.include property includes health. If the test uses webEnvironment = NONE, actuator web endpoints are not available, so I switch to RANDOM_PORT or MOCK. I also verify that the test does not override management.server.port to a fixed port that collides with another test. When actuator is intentionally disabled for a test, I either enable it for that test class or assert against an internal service bean instead of the HTTP endpoint.

I start by moving the container declaration to a base class or a shared utility so the same container instance is reused across multiple test classes. With Testcontainers, I enable the testcontainers.reuse.enable flag in ~/.testcontainers.properties so containers stay alive between test runs during local development. On CI, I ensure Docker layer caching is enabled and that the base image is pulled before the build. I also look for unnecessary

@DirtiesContext annotations that force the Spring context to restart. If the suite is still slow, I

move the fastest unit-like repository tests to H2 and reserve Testcontainers only for tests that exercise vendor-specific SQL or stored procedures.

The test disabled CSRF to make the mock setup easier, but the developer assumed the controller behavior was the only thing being tested. CSRF is part of Spring Security, and disabling it in tests hides the fact that state-changing browser requests require a valid token. The fix is to configure MockMvc with the same security settings used in production and to include CSRF tokens in state-changing requests using csrf(). For pure API clients using bearer tokens, CSRF is legitimately disabled, but that decision belongs in the production configuration, not hidden in tests. Tests should mirror production security as closely as possible to catch integration issues early.

@MockBean changes the Spring context fingerprint, so every unique combination of

mocked beans triggers a new context cache entry. If many test classes mock different sets of beans, the cache fills up and contexts are constantly created and destroyed. I address this by grouping tests that need the same mocks into the same test class or by using @Import to share a common test configuration that declares the mocks once. For unit tests that do not need the Spring context at all, I replace @MockBean with plain Mockito @Mock and remove @SpringBootTest, which eliminates context reloads entirely. When a slice test such as @WebMvcTest is sufficient, I use it instead of the full context.

H2 supports a different SQL dialect than PostgreSQL, and some queries that work in H2 may use syntax, functions, or type casting that PostgreSQL does not accept. Native queries, JSON operators, window functions, and custom types are common sources of mismatch. The fix is to move the test from H2 to a Testcontainers PostgreSQL container so the test database matches production. I also review the query for dialect-specific features and consider using JPQL or the Criteria API for portable logic. If a native query is unavoidable, I write a dedicated integration test against the real database and keep the H2 test only for simple repository methods where dialect differences do not matter.

Testing private methods through reflection is usually not a good practice because it couples the test to internal implementation details and makes refactoring painful. Private methods are tested indirectly by testing the public methods that call them. If the private method is complex enough to need its own tests, that is often a sign that it should be extracted into a separate class with

public methods that can be tested directly. The exception is legacy code that cannot be refactored

safely; in that case, reflection may be a temporary bridge, but the goal should be to refactor the code rather than maintain reflection-based tests.

Line coverage only measures whether a line was executed, not whether it was verified. A test can execute error-handling code without asserting that the correct exception is thrown, or it can call a method with a weak assertion that misses the bug. Coverage also does not measure edge cases, concurrency, or integration failures. I treat coverage as a hygiene metric, not a quality guarantee. I review tests for meaningful assertions, add mutation testing or property-based testing for critical paths, and run integration tests against realistic data. If a bug escapes, I add a regression test that fails before the fix and passes after it.

Intermittent failures in asynchronous email sending usually come from timing issues. The test asserts before the background thread has finished, or the mock interaction happens on a different thread than the test thread. I stabilize the test by using Awaitility to wait until the mock receives the expected call, or by returning a CompletableFuture from the email service so the test can wait for completion. If the email client runs inside an @Async method, I inject the TaskExecutor and wait for active tasks. I also make the test deterministic by controlling the thread pool or by making the email service synchronous in the test configuration.

Generated code should not count against coverage because it is not hand-written business logic. For Lombok, I add lombok.config with lombok.addLombokGeneratedAnnotation = true so JaCoCo recognizes the

@Generated annotation and skips those methods. For MapStruct, I configure the JaCoCo

exclusion list to ignore the generated mapper implementation classes, for example with **/mapper/*Impl. I keep the domain and service classes included in coverage so the report reflects actual code quality. If the team uses other generators, I add their output packages to the exclusions and document why those classes are not measured.

Adding Spring Security introduces filters that intercept every request, so unauthenticated requests to protected endpoints return 401 instead of 200. I update the test by configuring MockMvc with the security filters and either providing test credentials or disabling authentication for the test. For @WebMvcTest, I add @AutoConfigureMockMvc(addFilters = false) only if the goal is to test controller logic without security, but I prefer to keep security enabled and use @WithMockUser to simulate an authenticated user. I also add tests that verify the endpoint correctly rejects unauthenticated requests and enforces role-based access, so security behavior is covered, not bypassed.

I start by reading the full stack trace and the caused-by chain, because the root cause is usually buried several levels deep. Common reasons are a missing property, a bean that fails to initialize, a conditional configuration that activates incorrectly under test, or a dependency that requires a real service not available in the test environment. I run a single failing test with debug logging for org.springframework.boot and org.springframework.test to see which bean fails. If the new dependency is not needed for the test, I exclude it with

@SpringBootTest(classes = { ... }) or a test-specific profile. If it is needed, I

provide a mock or a lightweight implementation with @TestConfiguration.

Production readiness is much more than deploying a JAR file. You need a managed database with backups, environment-specific configuration, secrets management, health monitoring, centralized logging, and a repeatable deployment pipeline. On AWS this usually means RDS for the database, Elastic Beanstalk or ECS/EKS for compute, a load balancer for traffic distribution, and CloudWatch for metrics and alarms. You also need to externalize configuration using Spring profiles and environment variables so the same artifact runs in dev, staging, and production without code changes. A common pitfall is shipping the production database password inside the JAR or leaving debug endpoints exposed.

I create a DB subnet group that spans at least two availability zones for high availability, then provision the RDS instance or Aurora cluster with the desired engine, instance class, storage, and backup retention. Security groups restrict inbound traffic to the application tier only, and the database is placed in private subnets so it is unreachable from the internet. I enable automated backups, multi-AZ failover for production workloads, and encryption at rest using KMS. The application connects through an environment variable such as SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL, which points to the RDS endpoint, rather than hard-coding the host. I also create a read replica if reporting queries need to offload traffic from the primary instance.

Multi-AZ maintains a synchronous standby in another availability zone for high availability. If the primary fails, RDS automatically fails over to the standby without manual intervention. It is for availability, not scaling. A read replica is an asynchronous copy of the primary that can serve read traffic and be promoted to a standalone database. It is for scaling read-heavy workloads and disaster recovery, but it may lag behind the primary by a small amount. Teams sometimes confuse the two and enable Multi-AZ expecting read scaling, or create a read replica expecting automatic failover, so it is important to choose based on the actual goal.

Credentials should never be committed to source control or baked into the Docker image. In AWS, the cleanest approach is to store the username and password in AWS Secrets Manager and have the application retrieve them at startup, either by integrating Spring Cloud AWS or by mounting the secret as environment variables through Elastic Beanstalk or ECS. Another option is to use IAM database authentication for supported engines, which issues short-lived tokens and removes long-lived passwords. In a pinch you can pass the secret as environment variables, but rotation then becomes manual. Whatever the mechanism, the application must fail clearly at startup if the secret cannot be read, so misconfiguration is discovered immediately rather than masked by a connection error hours later.

Spring profiles let you define sets of beans and properties that apply only in specific environments. You activate a profile with spring.profiles.active=prod, and Spring loads application-prod.properties or application-prod.yml alongside the main configuration. Profiles are useful for switching datasources, logging levels, feature flags, and external service URLs. The real value is that the same deployable artifact can run anywhere. A common mistake is putting environment-specific values directly in application.properties; instead, put defaults there and override them per profile or through environment variables so the artifact remains portable.

I keep common settings in the main application.yml, such as application name, Jackson settings, and logging format. I then create profile-specific files like application- dev.yml, application-staging.yml, and application-prod.yml that contain environment-specific overrides. Sensitive values such as passwords are not stored in any file; they are supplied through environment variables or a secret manager. For example, spring.datasource.url can point to a local Docker database in dev and an RDS endpoint in prod. I also use a local profile for developers that disables cloud integrations and uses an in- memory or Dockerized database.

@Profile lets you register a bean only when a specific profile is active. For example,

you might have a MockNotificationService annotated with @Profile("local") and a real AwsSesNotificationService annotated with @Profile("!local"). This keeps the application runnable locally without external credentials. It is also useful for feature flags during gradual rollouts. The risk is overusing profiles to toggle behavior; if every environment behaves differently, the production path is not well tested. Profiles should mainly handle externalized configuration and infrastructure wiring, not business logic branches.

Elastic Beanstalk is a managed platform service that handles capacity provisioning, load balancing, auto-scaling, and application health monitoring for you. You upload your JAR or Docker image, choose a platform such as Java 17, and Elastic Beanstalk deploys it onto EC2 instances behind a load balancer. It is a common choice for Spring Boot because it reduces operational overhead; you get a production-grade environment without manually configuring EC2, load balancers, and scaling policies. The trade-off is less control than ECS, EKS, or raw EC2, and some teams outgrow it as they need custom networking or advanced deployment strategies.

I create an Elastic Beanstalk web server environment and choose a load-balanced configuration. The environment provisions an Application Load Balancer, an Auto Scaling group, and EC2 instances running the Java platform. I upload the Spring Boot JAR as an application version and deploy it to the environment. In the environment configuration, I set health check URL to /actuator/health, configure auto-scaling triggers based on CPU or request count, and set instance type and minimum/maximum counts. The load balancer forwards traffic on port 80 or 443 to the instances on the port Spring Boot listens on, usually 5000 for Elastic Beanstalk Java platforms.

Elastic Beanstalk Java platforms default to running the application on port 5000, and the nginx proxy forwards requests from port 80 to port 5000. If your Spring Boot application uses a different port, you must set SERVER_PORT to 5000 or update the nginx configuration. The platform can pass the port through the PORT environment variable. A common issue is that the application starts on 8080 locally and fails health checks on Elastic Beanstalk because the load balancer expects it on 5000. I always verify the port setting in the environment properties and use server.port=5000 or the equivalent environment variable.

I create the RDS instance first and then configure the Elastic Beanstalk environment properties to point to it. The properties include SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL, SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME, and SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD. The security group of the RDS instance must allow inbound traffic from the security group attached to the Elastic Beanstalk EC2 instances. It is best not to use Elastic Beanstalk's integrated RDS option for production because it ties the database lifecycle to the environment; instead, create RDS separately and reference it through environment properties. This keeps the database alive if the environment is recreated or scaled down.

Auto-scaling automatically adds or removes EC2 instances based on demand to maintain performance and control cost. Common triggers are CPU utilization above a threshold for several minutes, request count per target, network traffic, or custom CloudWatch metrics such as queue depth. For Spring Boot APIs, CPU is the simplest starting point, but request count can scale faster for IO-bound workloads. Scaling policies should have cooldowns so instances are not added and removed too quickly. I also configure a minimum instance count for availability and a maximum to prevent runaway costs during traffic spikes or attacks.

CI/CD is the practice of automatically building, testing, and deploying code changes. AWS CodeBuild compiles the code, runs tests, and produces artifacts such as a JAR or Docker image. AWS CodePipeline orchestrates the stages: source from GitHub, build with CodeBuild, and deploy to Elastic Beanstalk, ECS, or S3. GitHub hooks trigger the pipeline whenever code is pushed, so there is no manual handoff. The benefit is fast, repeatable, and auditable releases. A common mistake is skipping tests in the pipeline or deploying directly to production without a staging stage, which defeats the purpose of having a pipeline.

In CodePipeline, I set the source provider to GitHub version 2, which uses a GitHub App connection to authenticate. During setup, CodePipeline creates a webhook in the GitHub repository that sends push events to the pipeline. Alternatively, you can use a webhook created through API Gateway or a Lambda function that starts the pipeline. The webhook payload is filtered by branch, so only pushes to main or release branches trigger a build. I also ensure the pipeline has a manual approval stage between staging and production so a human can gate the final deployment.

CodeBuild uses a buildspec.yml file to define phases and artifacts. The phases typically include install, pre-build, build, and post-build. In the build phase, I run mvn clean verify to compile, run unit and integration tests, and package the JAR. In post-build, I copy the JAR to the artifacts directory. The artifacts section tells CodeBuild which files to pass to the next pipeline stage, such as target/*.jar and an application.properties if needed. I also cache the Maven dependencies in ~/.m2 to speed up subsequent builds. If tests fail, the build stops and nothing is deployed.

Database migration is the controlled process of applying schema changes such as adding tables, columns, indexes, or constraints as the application evolves. In production, manual changes are risky because they are hard to reproduce, review, and roll back. Migration tools such as Flyway or Liquibase version schema changes as scripts and apply them automatically in a defined order. This makes deployments repeatable across environments and keeps the schema in sync with the code. A production incident often happens when a code change expects a column that was never added to the production schema.

Flyway is a database migration tool that applies SQL or Java migration scripts stored in db/migration. Scripts are named with a version prefix such as V1__create_users_table.sql, and Flyway executes them in order. It maintains a flyway_schema_history table to track which migrations have already been applied, so it never runs the same script twice. In Spring Boot, adding flyway-core to the classpath enables auto-configuration that runs migrations at startup. This means the application will not start if a migration fails, which prevents running code against an incompatible schema.

Versioned migrations are numbered scripts that run exactly once and are used for schema changes that must not be repeated, such as adding a column or creating a table. Repeatable migrations have a description but no version number, and they run whenever their checksum changes. They are useful for views, stored procedures, functions, and reference data that should be recreated when the script is updated. A typical setup uses versioned migrations for structural changes and repeatable migrations for database logic that depends on those structures.

Destructive changes such as dropping a column or table should be done in multiple deployments to avoid data loss and downtime. Instead of dropping a column immediately, I first deploy a migration that adds a new column and copies data, update the application to read from both, then deploy another migration that removes the old column once the application no longer uses it. Flyway's versioning guarantees order, but it does not protect you from the business impact of a bad script. I always review migrations in a pull request, test them against a copy of production data, and have a rollback plan such as restoring from a snapshot.

Flyway reads properties from the active Spring profile, so you can have different schemas, locations, or baseline settings per environment. For example, application-prod.yml might set spring.flyway.locations=classpath:db/migration,classpath:db/migrat ion/prod and disable clean so the production database is never wiped. In dev, you might enable spring.flyway.clean-disabled=false and use a Dockerized database. Sensitive connection details come from environment variables or AWS Secrets Manager, not from the Flyway configuration file. I also set spring.flyway.baseline-on-migrate=true when introducing Flyway to an existing database that already has a schema.

A rolling deployment updates instances in place a few at a time, which is simple but means old and new versions run simultaneously during the transition. A blue-green deployment creates a separate environment with the new version, verifies it, and then swaps the load balancer to point to the new environment. Blue-green deployments are safer because you can rollback by swapping back, but they cost more because both environments run briefly. Elastic Beanstalk supports swap URLs for blue-green deployments, while rolling updates are a built-in deployment policy.

I expose health, metrics, and info endpoints through Spring Boot Actuator and secure them so only authorized systems can read them. CloudWatch can collect logs from the Elastic Beanstalk environment and trigger alarms on error counts, CPU, or memory. For application-level metrics, I use Micrometer to publish custom metrics such as request duration, order creation count, or payment failure rate to CloudWatch or Prometheus. I also set up SNS notifications so the team is alerted when alarms fire. Health checks on the load balancer should use /actuator/health so unhealthy instances are replaced automatically.

In Elastic Beanstalk, instances come and go, so logs stored locally are lost when an instance is terminated. I configure the environment to stream logs to CloudWatch Logs using the Elastic Beanstalk log streaming option, or I use the CloudWatch agent inside Docker deployments. The application should write structured JSON logs with correlation IDs so log entries can be traced across services. For larger systems, I forward logs to a centralized tool such as ELK, Splunk, or Datadog. I also ensure that sensitive data is not logged, and that log levels are configurable per profile.

Infrastructure as code is the practice of defining AWS resources such as RDS, Elastic Beanstalk, load balancers, and IAM roles in declarative templates using tools like Terraform, AWS CloudFormation, or the AWS CDK. It makes deployments repeatable, version-controlled, and reviewable. If a production environment is hand-built, recreating it after an incident requires guessing what was configured. With infrastructure as code, you recreate the environment by running the same template. It also supports separate environments that are identical except for size and secrets, which reduces "works in dev, fails in prod" issues.

I begin by reading the application startup logs carefully, because the exact exception tells me whether this is a network reachability problem or an authentication problem. A connection timeout usually means the security group is blocking traffic, the RDS instance is in a different VPC, or the subnet routing is wrong, while an authentication failure means the username, password, or database name is incorrect. I verify the environment variables in Elastic Beanstalk or ECS match the values shown in the RDS console, and I confirm that the RDS security group has an inbound rule allowing the application security group on the database port. I also check that the RDS instance is in an available state and that the subnet group spans availability zones compatible with the application. If the database was recently restored or recreated, the endpoint will have changed and the application configuration must be updated before the next deployment. I avoid the trap of trying different credentials blindly or opening the security group to the internet, because both increase risk and do not fix a routing problem.

I first search the codebase for any hard-coded localhost URLs, default usernames, or passwords that are checked into the repository, because these are the values the application will use if no environment override is present. I move all dev-only values out of application.properties and into application-dev.yml, and I make the main configuration file contain only safe defaults or placeholders. For production, I set SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=prod and supply SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL, SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME, and SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD through Elastic Beanstalk environment properties or, better, AWS Secrets Manager. I validate the fix by running the application locally with the prod profile and dummy credentials to confirm it attempts to connect to the supplied URL rather than a hard-coded dev database. I also add a CI step that scans the repository for hard-coded secrets and localhost database URLs, failing the build if any are found. The lesson is that the same artifact should run everywhere, with environment-specific values injected at runtime rather than baked into the build.

I open the Elastic Beanstalk events and the application logs in CloudWatch immediately, because a successful deployment only means the files were copied, not that the application is healthy. The most common reasons for failed health checks are the application listening on the wrong port, the health endpoint returning a non-200 status, or the application crashing during startup because it cannot reach the database or apply migrations. I verify that the platform expects the application on port 5000 and that SERVER_PORT is set accordingly. I check that /actuator/health is accessible anonymously or that the load balancer health check path is configured to a public endpoint. If Spring Security is blocking the health check, I permit access to /actuator/health and /actuator/info in the security configuration. I also verify that Flyway migrations run successfully at startup, because a failed migration can leave the application unable to start and therefore unable to pass health checks. Once the cause is fixed, I deploy again and monitor the health dashboard before declaring the release successful.

This typically happens when Flyway is introduced to a database that was already created and modified manually, so the production schema is at a later state than the first migration script assumes. I start by checking the flyway_schema_history table, or its absence, to understand what Flyway thinks has been applied. I then baseline the production database by setting spring.flyway.baseline-on-migrate=true and choosing a baseline version that matches the current schema state, which tells Flyway to mark earlier migrations as applied without running them. After baselining, all future migrations must be written against the current production schema. I never manually edit the flyway_schema_history table to force a migration through, because that corrupts Flyway's internal state and leads to unpredictable failures later. Before applying the baseline in production, I restore a snapshot to a staging environment, run the baseline and the planned migrations there, and run the application smoke tests to confirm everything works. The long-term fix is to ensure every environment is created from the same migration sequence so manual drift does not happen again.

I restructure the pipeline into clear stages: source, build, staging, manual approval, and production. In the build stage, CodeBuild compiles the code, runs unit and integration tests, and produces the deployable artifact. In the staging stage, the artifact is deployed to a staging Elastic Beanstalk environment that mirrors production as closely as possible, and a set of automated smoke tests and integration tests run against it. I then add a manual approval action in CodePipeline that requires a team member to approve the deployment after reviewing the test results, the change log, and any monitoring anomalies. For production, I keep the previous application version available and use blue-green deployment so that if the new version fails health checks, the load balancer can be swapped back quickly. I also add GitHub branch protection rules requiring pull request reviews before code can reach main, so the pipeline is not triggered by unreviewed changes. The key principle is that no change should reach customers without being exercised in a staging environment first.

This is a classic destructive migration mistake, and the immediate lesson is that dropping a column should always be the final step of a multi-release process. The first step is to remove every application reference to the column and deploy that change. The second step is to verify that no code, report, or downstream consumer reads from or writes to the column. Only then do I write a migration that drops it, and before applying that migration I take a manual snapshot of the production database. I also run the migration against a restored copy of production data in staging to confirm it behaves as expected. For columns that might contain useful historical data, I copy the data to an archive table or a data lake before removal. In the pull request, I label the migration as destructive and require two approvals. If the column was already dropped and data is needed, I restore the pre-drop snapshot to a separate instance and extract the data, rather than trying to undo the migration by hand.

I begin by reviewing the auto-scaling history and CloudWatch metrics to see exactly when and why instances were added. CPU is the default trigger, but for an IO-bound Spring Boot application the CPU may stay low while response time degrades, so scaling based on CPU alone can add instances too late or unnecessarily. I consider switching the scaling metric to request count per target, average response time, or a custom CloudWatch metric such as queue depth. I set a maximum instance count as a hard cost ceiling and use scheduled scaling to reduce capacity during known quiet periods. I also examine whether the application can handle more load per instance by tuning the JVM heap, database connection pool size, or slow queries, because adding instances is not always the cheapest fix. For predictable baseline load, I use Reserved Instances or Savings Plans to reduce the per-hour cost, and I reserve Spot instances only for stateless, fault-tolerant batch work. The caution is that aggressive cost optimization should never remove the minimum instances needed for availability.

I first check the health check configuration on the target group, because an incorrect path, a long interval, or a high number of healthy thresholds can delay the load balancer from detecting a failure. If the health check path is a static page that always returns 200 even when the application cannot reach the database, the load balancer will continue sending traffic to a broken instance. I change the health check to /actuator/health or another endpoint that genuinely reflects application readiness. Even with a correct health check, there is a short window between the first failed check and deregistration when the instance can still receive requests, and connection draining or deregistration delay lengthens that window. I tune these values to balance fast failure detection with graceful shutdown of in-flight requests. I also enable the load balancer access logs and target group metrics so I can see exactly when an instance was marked unhealthy and how long it remained in rotation.

I treat the keys as compromised the moment they touch version control, even if they were removed quickly, because Git history retains them. I immediately rotate the credentials in the AWS IAM console by deactivating and deleting the exposed access keys and creating new ones for the affected user or service. I then inspect CloudTrail logs for any unauthorized API calls made with the old keys, paying attention to unusual regions, resource creation, or data access. I run GitHub secret scanning or git-secrets across the repository history to find any other exposed credentials, and if necessary I rewrite history to remove them. I add pre-commit hooks and repository scanning to prevent future commits of secrets, and I update the team's onboarding documentation to explain why access keys must be provided through environment variables or IAM roles rather than code. I also recommend switching from long-term access keys to IAM roles wherever possible, because roles do not expose static credentials.

The biggest change is that the build artifact becomes a Docker image rather than an executable JAR. In CodeBuild, I update the buildspec.yml to run docker build, tag the image with the commit hash or build ID, and push it to Amazon ECR. The deploy stage then uses ECS task definitions and service updates instead of Elastic Beanstalk application versions, so the pipeline must generate or update a task definition with the new image URI and call ecs update- service. Load balancing shifts to an Application Load Balancer that points at an ECS target group, and auto-scaling becomes ECS service auto-scaling. Environment configuration is still passed through environment variables, but in ECS these live in the task definition or are sourced from AWS Secrets Manager and Systems Manager Parameter Store. I also add container health checks and CloudWatch logging sidecars so observability is consistent with the previous setup. The transition requires the team to understand container networking, task lifecycle, and image versioning, but it gives more flexibility for blue-green and canary deployments.

I add a validation step early in the pipeline that compares the environment configuration against a required manifest. This can be a simple script in CodeBuild that reads a required- env.properties file and checks that every listed variable is present in the target environment before deployment proceeds. In the Spring Boot application, I make the critical property required using constructor binding with validation annotations or by implementing an ApplicationRunner that fails fast with a clear message if a required value is missing. I keep environment-specific configuration documented in version control, ideally as Terraform or CloudFormation templates, so the infrastructure and the application expectations stay in sync. I also run the application with the prod profile and minimal environment variables in a CI smoke test to catch missing configuration before it reaches production. The broader lesson is that configuration changes should be reviewed and applied through the same pipeline as code changes.

Flyway requires every versioned migration to have a unique prefix, so two files named V5__... will cause validate to fail. I coordinate with the other developer to decide which script logically comes first, then I rename one script to the next available version number and make sure the ordering still makes sense from a database state perspective. If either script was already applied to a shared environment, I check the flyway_schema_history table before renaming, because renaming an applied script will change its checksum and cause validation errors. To prevent this from recurring, I introduce a naming convention based on ticket numbers or the current date, and I add a CI step that runs flyway validate or checks for duplicate version prefixes before the pull request can be merged. I also encourage the team to announce when they are adding migrations on shared branches so two people do not independently pick the same version number.

I start by adding a correlation ID at the entry point of every request, usually through a servlet filter or interceptor, and I propagate that ID through the MDC so that every log statement emitted during the request includes the same identifier. I then change the log format to structured JSON with fields such as timestamp, level, logger, message, correlation ID, request path, and user ID, and I send those logs to CloudWatch Logs. This lets the team use CloudWatch Logs Insights to query all log lines for a single request in seconds. I also classify errors more carefully: business validation failures return a clear 4xx response with a short message and log at WARN, while unexpected internal errors log the full stack trace at ERROR and return only a generic incident message to the client. I make sure sensitive data such as passwords or tokens are never logged, and I add alerts on ERROR rate spikes so the team knows about problems before users report them.

I first move the deployment window outside business hours as a short-term measure, but the real fix is to change the deployment strategy. In Elastic Beanstalk, I use blue-green deployment by cloning the environment, deploying the new version, running smoke tests, and swapping the load balancer URL to point to the new environment; if anything goes wrong, I swap back. In ECS, I configure a rolling update with minimum healthy percent of 100 and maximum percent of 200 so new containers start and pass health checks before old containers stop. For higher confidence, I add a canary stage that routes a small percentage of traffic to the new version while monitoring error rates and latency. All database migrations are applied before the code deployment and must be backward-compatible, meaning old code can still run against the new schema until the deployment finishes. I also verify that the load balancer health checks are strict enough to prevent traffic from reaching instances that have started but are not yet ready.

Sharing a database between staging and production is one of the riskiest cost optimizations because it breaks environment isolation completely. A bug in a staging feature can corrupt or delete production data, a load test can starve production queries and cause timeouts for real users, and a schema migration tested in staging is applied directly to the production schema. There is also no safe way to test destructive changes or data fixes without affecting production. The right approach is to use a separate RDS instance or Aurora cluster for staging, ideally restored periodically from a sanitized copy of production data so tests run against realistic schema and volumes. If budget is tight, I use a smaller instance class for staging, schedule it to stop during nights and weekends, or use RDS snapshots to recreate it on demand. The database is the one resource where the cost of sharing always outweighs the savings.

I first identify what is consuming storage by checking CloudWatch metrics for database size growth, transaction logs, and temporary files. If the growth comes from normal data accumulation, I enable RDS storage auto-scaling so the volume grows automatically up to a defined maximum without manual intervention. If storage auto-scaling is not available for the engine or instance class, I plan a maintenance window to increase the allocated storage manually. If the growth is caused by excessive transaction logs, I review the backup retention period and log expiration settings, and I make sure long-running transactions or replication lag are not preventing log cleanup. For audit or historical data that does not need to be in the primary database, I archive old rows to S3 or a data warehouse and purge them in batches. I avoid filling the disk to 100 percent, because many databases become read-only or crash at that point, and I set CloudWatch alarms at 70 and 85 percent so the team has time to act.

I check whether the Application Load Balancer or the instance itself is terminating TLS. In most Elastic Beanstalk setups, the load balancer terminates HTTPS using an ACM certificate, and the instances handle HTTP. I open the load balancer listener configuration and verify that port 443 is present, that it has a valid ACM certificate attached, and that the certificate covers the domain being requested. If the certificate has expired, I renew it in ACM and update the listener. If the application is accessed directly by IP or an old DNS name that is not on the certificate, the browser will show a name mismatch error, so I make sure the DNS record points to the load balancer's canonical name. I also confirm that the security group allows inbound HTTPS traffic on 443. I avoid putting self-signed certificates on the instances unless absolutely necessary, because ACM provides free managed certificates that renew automatically.

I start by reading the CodeBuild log to see exactly which tests failed and how they failed, because environment differences are the most common cause. The pipeline may run tests against a different database profile, a different timezone, or missing environment variables that are present on the developer's machine. I check the buildspec.yml to confirm that the correct Maven profile is active and that any required services or resources are available during the build. I also verify that the build does not rely on files that are in the developer's local workspace but not in the repository, such as untracked configuration files. Differences in Java version, Maven version, or locale between the laptop and the CodeBuild image can also cause test failures, so I pin those versions in the buildspec. Once I identify the difference, I make the build environment reproducible, often by adding a Docker-based build or by running the same buildspec in a local CodeBuild agent.

Running out of connections usually means the total pool size across all instances exceeds the database's maximum connection limit. I calculate the expected total connections by multiplying the per-instance HikariCP maximum pool size by the number of instances and compare that to the RDS max_connections setting. If the total is close to or above the limit, I reduce the per- instance pool size, because a larger pool on each instance does not help if the database cannot accept them. I also check for connection leaks caused by missing try-with-resources or long-running queries that hold connections. For read-heavy workloads, I add read replicas and route read traffic to them, which both scales capacity and reduces pressure on the primary. If connection demand is genuinely high, I scale the RDS instance class vertically to support more connections or use RDS Proxy to pool and share database connections efficiently. I avoid simply raising the pool size as the first fix, because that usually makes the problem worse.

The problem is likely that the application reads the secret only at startup and never refreshes it, so when the secret value changes the cached credentials become invalid. I first restore service by restarting the affected instances or rolling back the secret rotation if possible. For a proper fix, I change the application to refresh the secret periodically or on authentication failure, using the AWS SDK to retrieve the latest value from Secrets Manager. When rotating database credentials, I use the Secrets Manager rotation feature that stores both the current and pending versions during the rotation window, so the application has time to pick up the new value while the old value remains valid. I also make sure the database user is not dropped immediately after rotation, because open connections from the old credential must finish their work. Ideally, the application uses IAM database authentication or RDS Proxy so password rotation is handled outside the application entirely. The key is that secret rotation should be graceful, not an all-at-once event.

Generative AI refers to models that can create new content such as text, images, audio, or code by learning patterns from training data. Unlike traditional AI, which is often used for classification, regression, or clustering, generative AI produces outputs that resemble its training distribution. Large language models such as GPT, Gemini, and Claude are generative AI systems trained to predict the next token in a sequence. For backend developers, generative AI enables features like chatbots, code assistants, content generation, and summarization. The key difference is that traditional AI maps inputs to known labels, while generative AI synthesizes novel outputs conditioned on inputs.

A Large Language Model is a deep neural network, typically based on the Transformer architecture, trained on vast amounts of text to predict the next token in a sequence. During training, the model learns statistical relationships between words, grammar, facts, and reasoning patterns. At inference, it takes a prompt, converts it into tokens, processes it through many layers of self-attention and feed-forward networks, and generates tokens one at a time until a stop condition is reached. The model does not truly understand text in a human sense; it predicts likely sequences based on learned patterns. Modern LLMs have billions of parameters and are ne-tuned for instruction following and safety.

Tokenization is the process of splitting text into smaller units called tokens, which can be words, subwords, or characters. LLMs operate on tokens rather than raw characters, so the input text is first converted to tokens, and the model predicts the next token. The number of tokens affects cost, context window usage, and latency. Different models use different tokenizers, so the same sentence may produce different token counts across providers. For backend developers, tokenization matters because APIs charge per token, prompt length is limited by the context window, and output must often be bounded by max tokens.

The Transformer is a neural network architecture introduced in the paper Attention Is All You Need. It is built from encoder and decoder stacks, though modern LLMs typically use decoder-only variants. The core component is the self-attention mechanism, which allows the model to weigh the importance of different tokens in the input when producing each output token. Transformers also use positional encoding to understand token order, feed-forward layers to transform representations, layer normalization, and residual connections. The architecture is highly parallelizable during training and scales well to billions of parameters.

Self-attention is a mechanism that computes a weighted representation of each token by considering all other tokens in the sequence. For every token, the model generates query, key, and value vectors, compares the query with all keys to produce attention scores, normalizes them with softmax, and uses the weighted sum of values as the output. This allows the model to capture long-range dependencies and contextual relationships regardless of token distance. It is important because it replaces sequential processing with parallel computation and gives the model the ability to focus on relevant parts of the input when generating each token.

Embeddings are dense numerical vector representations of text, images, or other data. They map semantic meaning into a high-dimensional space where similar items are closer together. In LLMs, token embeddings represent individual tokens, while sentence or document embeddings capture the meaning of larger text chunks. Embeddings are produced by passing text through an embedding model. They are used for tasks such as semantic search, classification, clustering, recommendation, and retrieval in RAG systems. The quality of embeddings depends on the model and the domain of the training data.

A vector store is a database optimized for storing and searching high-dimensional vectors. Vector search, also called similarity search, nds vectors that are closest to a query vector using distance metrics such as cosine similarity or Euclidean distance. Popular vector stores include pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Milvus, and Redis. They often use approximate nearest neighbor algorithms such as HNSW or IVF to search efficiently at scale. In generative AI applications, vector stores are used to retrieve relevant documents or facts based on semantic similarity, which improves response accuracy beyond what the model memorized during training.

Retrieval Augmented Generation is a pattern that combines information retrieval with text generation. Instead of relying solely on the model's training knowledge, the system retrieves relevant documents from a vector store or database and includes them in the prompt as context. The model then generates an answer grounded in the retrieved content. RAG reduces hallucination, keeps responses up to date, and allows the model to answer questions about private or proprietary data. It is commonly used for enterprise knowledge bases, documentation assistants, and customer support bots.

Naive RAG embeds the user query, performs a single similarity search against a vector store, and injects the top documents into the prompt. It is simple to implement and works well for many cases. Modular RAG breaks the process into con gurable stages such as query transformation, retrieval, document post-processing, and augmentation. It can use query rewriting, compression, translation, multi-query expansion, reranking, and hybrid search. Modular RAG is more exible and accurate but also more complex. Spring AI supports naive RAG through QuestionAnswerAdvisor and modular RAG through RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor.

Spring AI is a framework that brings AI capabilities to Spring applications. It provides a consistent, portable API for working with large language models, embedding models, vector stores, and AI patterns such as RAG and tool calling. It supports multiple model providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Ollama, Azure OpenAI, and Mistral. Spring AI is designed with the same philosophy as Spring: abstractions over vendor APIs, easy configuration, and integration with Spring Boot. Backend developers can use it to add chat, search, classification, and agentic features to Java applications without writing provider-specific boilerplate.

ChatClient is the high-level uent API in Spring AI for interacting with chat models. It allows developers to build prompts, set model options, attach advisors, register tools, and call or stream responses. It is constructed from a ChatModel bean and configured through the builder. ChatClient abstracts the underlying provider, so switching from OpenAI to Anthropic requires changing configuration rather than application code. It supports synchronous calls with call(), streaming with stream(), structured output with entity(), and content extraction with content().

You add the appropriate Spring AI starter, such as spring-ai-openai-spring-boot-starter, and configure properties like spring.ai.openai.api-key and spring.ai.openai.chat.options.model. Spring Boot auto-configures a ChatModel bean. You then create a ChatClient bean by calling ChatClient.builder(chatModel).build(). You can also set default advisors, default tools, and default options during construction. The ChatClient can be injected into services and used in REST controllers or background jobs. This pattern keeps AI logic inside Spring-managed services and integrates with the rest of the application.

Advisors are interceptors in the ChatClient request and response pipeline. They can modify the request before it reaches the model, process the response after it returns, or implement reusable patterns such as logging, chat memory, RAG retrieval, and tool calling. Advisors implement CallAdvisor or StreamAdvisor and run in a chain ordered by getOrder. Built-in advisors include MessageChatMemoryAdvisor for conversation memory, QuestionAnswerAdvisor for RAG, ToolCallingAdvisor for tool execution, and RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor for modular RAG. Custom advisors allow developers to add cross-cutting concerns like audit logging, prompt injection prevention, or cost tracking.

Both Advisors and Spring AOP interceptors wrap behavior around a core operation. AOP intercepts method calls in the Spring application context, while Spring AI Advisors intercept calls to the language model. AOP is general purpose for business methods; Advisors are specialized for the ChatClient pipeline. In Spring AI, advisors receive ChatClientRequest and ChatClientResponse objects and can update context, modify prompts, or transform responses. They are specific to AI work ows and integrate with the ChatClient builder, whereas AOP interceptors work with any Spring bean method.

Chat memory stores the history of a conversation so the model can maintain context across multiple turns. Without memory, each request is stateless and the model does not remember previous messages. Spring AI provides MessageChatMemoryAdvisor, which stores messages in an in-memory or persistent ChatMemory implementation. The advisor retrieves recent messages and adds them to the prompt. For longer conversations, VectorStoreChatMemoryAdvisor can retrieve semantically relevant past messages from a vector store. A conversation ID is used to scope messages to a specific user session.

Tool calling, also known as function calling, allows an LLM to invoke application methods to retrieve data or perform actions. In Spring AI, tools are defined by methods annotated with @Tool or by implementing ToolCallback. The ChatClient registers tools, and the ToolCallingAdvisor manages the loop: the model decides to call a tool, Spring AI executes the method, and the result is sent back to the model. The loop continues until the model produces a final response. Tool calling enables agents that can access real-time data, query databases, call external APIs, or take actions in the system.

You create a method annotated with @Tool inside a Spring bean and pass the bean to ChatClient.defaultTools. The method should have a descriptive name and parameters, optionally with

@ToolParameter annotations to describe them to the model. When the model needs the capability, it

requests the tool with JSON arguments. Spring AI deserializes the arguments, invokes the method, and serializes the return value back to the model. Tools should be idempotent when possible and avoid side effects that cannot be undone. Complex tools may also use a ToolCallback implementation for full control over behavior and metadata.

Dynamic tool discovery is a pattern for applications with many tools. Instead of sending all tool definitions to the model on every request, which consumes tokens and may confuse the model, a ToolSearchToolCallingAdvisor indexes tools and exposes a search tool. The model calls the search tool to find relevant tools, and only those tools are added to the context. This reduces token usage and improves accuracy when the application has dozens or hundreds of tools. Tool indexes can be semantic using VectorToolIndex, keyword-based using LuceneToolIndex, or pattern-based using RegexToolIndex.

Structured output is the ability to ask an LLM to return data in a specific format, usually JSON, and map it to a Java object. Spring AI supports this through the entity() method on ChatClient, which parses the response into a POJO using a schema or instructions provided in the prompt. It can also use JSON mode or constrained decoding if the model supports it. Structured output is useful when you need to consume AI output programmatically, such as extracting entities, classifying text, or generating configuration objects. It is more reliable than parsing free-form text.

Query transformers rewrite or expand the user query before retrieval to improve search quality.

Examples include RewriteQueryTransformer, which reformulates the query;

CompressionQueryTransformer, which shortens conversational queries; TranslationQueryTransformer, which translates the query into another language; and MultiQueryExpander, which generates multiple related queries to broaden retrieval. These transformers help when user queries are ambiguous, conversational, or domain-specific. They are part of the RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor pipeline in Spring AI.

Spring AI supports several vector stores including pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Milvus, Redis, Neo4j, and Elasticsearch. The choice depends on existing infrastructure, scale, and query requirements. pgvector is convenient when the application already uses PostgreSQL. Pinecone and Weaviate are managed services optimized for vector search. Redis is useful when the application already uses Redis for caching. Milvus and Elasticsearch are suitable for large-scale or hybrid search scenarios. Each has a Spring Boot starter with auto-configuration.

Ingestion typically involves reading documents from files or APIs, splitting them into chunks, computing embeddings for each chunk using an EmbeddingModel, and storing the resulting vectors and metadata in a VectorStore. Chunking strategy matters: too large chunks dilute relevance, while too small chunks lose context. Spring AI provides document readers, transformers, and splitters such as TokenTextSplitter and PagePdfDocumentReader. Metadata such as source, page number, or document type is stored alongside the vector to enable filtering. After ingestion, RAG queries can retrieve the most relevant chunks.

Hallucinations are cases where a language model generates plausible-sounding but incorrect or unsupported information. They happen because models are trained to predict likely tokens, not to verify facts against a knowledge source. RAG reduces hallucinations by grounding the model's response in retrieved documents. The prompt explicitly instructs the model to answer based only on the provided context. If the context does not contain the answer, the model can be instructed to say it does not know. Combining RAG with citations, reranking, and strict prompt instructions further improves factual reliability.

A prompt template is a reusable text pattern with placeholders that are lled at runtime. It allows developers to separate prompt engineering from application code and to inject dynamic values such as user input, retrieved context, or conversation history. Spring AI provides PromptTemplate and a uent prompt builder. Templates improve maintainability because prompts can be adjusted without changing Java code. They also support system messages, user messages, and assistant messages, which help structure the conversation and guide model behavior.

The chatbot is relying on the model's training knowledge, which has a cutoff and may not include the latest company documents. I implement RAG by ingesting the company policy documents into a vector store. I split the documents into chunks, compute embeddings, and store them with metadata such as document name and effective date. I add a QuestionAnswerAdvisor to the ChatClient so each user query retrieves the most relevant policy chunks and includes them in the prompt. I instruct the model to answer only from the provided context and to say it does not know if the answer is not in the documents. I also schedule periodic re-ingestion when policies are updated. Real-time example: an HR chatbot tells users the old remote-work policy. I create a pipeline that loads the latest policy PDFs, splits them into 500-token chunks, embeds them with the default embedding model, and stores them in pgvector. The ChatClient uses QuestionAnswerAdvisor with a filter on the latest policy version. Responses now re ect current rules.

I investigate the retrieval pipeline end to end. I check chunk size and overlap; chunks that are too large dilute relevance, and chunks that are too small lose context. I try different embedding models ne-tuned for the domain. I add query transformation such as query rewriting or multi-query expansion before retrieval. I implement hybrid search combining vector similarity with keyword matching. I add a reranking step using a cross-encoder model that scores retrieved documents against the query. I also verify metadata filters are correct and that the vector store is populated with clean, representative documents. Real-time example: a legal assistant retrieves contracts instead of clauses. I reduce chunk size to 512 tokens with 128 overlap, switch to a domain-specific embedding model, add a keyword boost for clause titles, and use a reranker. Precision improves from 40 percent to 85 percent.

I add a chat memory advisor to the ChatClient. I configure MessageChatMemoryAdvisor with a ChatMemory implementation such as InMemoryChatMemory or a persistent store. I pass a conversation ID with each request so the advisor can retrieve and store messages for that session. For long conversations, I use VectorStoreChatMemoryAdvisor to retrieve semantically relevant past turns instead of sending the entire history. I also set a maximum history length to avoid exceeding the model's context window. Real-time example: a customer support bot forgets the user's order ID after one turn. I add MessageChatMemoryAdvisor to the ChatClient, generate a session ID when the conversation starts, and pass it in the advisors parameter. The bot now references earlier messages and asks clarifying questions only when needed.

I use tool calling to fetch live data. I define a @Tool method such as getOrderStatus(String orderId) that queries the order database or order service. I register the tool with ChatClient.defaultTools. When the user asks about an order, the model calls the tool with the order ID, the application returns the current status, and the model answers in natural language. I combine this with RAG for general policy or product questions. I also add validation to ensure users can only query their own orders, which prevents unauthorized data access. Real-time example: a user asks, Where is my order 12345? The ChatClient recognizes it needs order status, calls getOrderStatus("12345"), which returns {"status":"shipped","eta":"2025-08-10"}, and the model responds: Your order 12345 has been shipped and is expected to arrive on August 10.

I inspect the chat response to see the exact tool name and arguments the model produced. Malformed arguments often happen when the tool description or parameter names are ambiguous. I improve the @Tool method name and add @ToolParameter descriptions so the model understands the expected format. I validate tool inputs and return clear error messages so the model can retry with corrected arguments. I also log every tool invocation and response for debugging. If the issue persists, I switch to a more capable model or use constrained output for critical tools. Real-time example: a weather tool receives dates in different formats. I update the @ToolParameter description to say ISO- 8601 date only, add input validation, and return an error like Invalid date format, expected YYYY-MM-DD when the format is wrong. The model corrects itself on the next call.

I apply the principle of least privilege to tool registration. Only tools that are necessary for the use case are registered with ChatClient. Tools that perform destructive or sensitive operations require authorization checks inside the tool method, verifying the user's identity and permissions. I avoid registering admin-only tools on public-facing chatbots. I also add a SafeGuardAdvisor or custom advisor to review tool calls and block dangerous operations. Tools should be idempotent or reversible when possible, and all invocations should be logged for audit. Real-time example: a support chatbot could accidentally call a deleteUserAccount tool. I remove that tool from the public ChatClient, wrap remaining tools with permission checks that verify the caller is an authorized admin, and log every tool call with user ID and arguments.

I check whether the issue is on the model side or the network side. Streaming from the model returns tokens as they are generated, so latency is expected. Choppy output may be caused by buffering in the web layer or frontend. I ensure the Spring controller returns a Flux and does not collect the entire response before sending. I verify the client consumes the stream correctly with Server-Sent Events or WebFlux. I also check model parameters; some models or configurations buffer more than others. If the backend is buffering, I return the Flux directly and avoid calling block(). Real-time example: a React frontend receives the entire response at once. The Spring controller returns ResponseEntity by calling content(). I change it to return Flux from chatClient.prompt().user(question).stream().content(), and the frontend now shows typing in real time.

I reduce the number of top-K documents retrieved, typically to 3 or 5. I also reduce chunk size so more chunks t within the context window. I add a compression step using a query compressor to remove redundant content. I use a reranker to select only the most relevant chunks before injecting them into the prompt. I can also use a contextual query augmenter that summarizes or filters chunks. If the answer still needs more context, I implement a multi-turn strategy where the model asks follow-up questions to re ne retrieval. Real-time example: a technical documentation assistant retrieves 10 large chunks and over ows the context window. I set topK to 4, reduce chunk size to 300 tokens, and add a reranker. The prompt now ts and the answers remain accurate.

Because Spring AI abstracts the model provider, the main change is dependency and configuration. I replace the OpenAI starter with the Azure OpenAI starter and update properties such as spring.ai.azure.openai.api-key, endpoint, and deployment-name. The ChatClient code, advisors, and tools usually remain unchanged. I verify that model capabilities such as JSON mode or tool calling are supported by the chosen Azure deployment. I also update environment-specific configuration without changing the application logic. This portability is one of the main benefits of using Spring AI. Real-time example: a company moves from OpenAI to Azure OpenAI for enterprise compliance. I change the Maven dependency, update application.yml with the Azure endpoint and deployment name, and rerun tests. The same service now calls Azure without any Java code changes.

I create a class that implements CallAdvisor or StreamAdvisor, depending on whether I need to intercept synchronous or streaming calls. In the around method, I access the ChatClientRequest, log the prompt text and metadata, proceed to the next advisor or model, receive the ChatClientResponse, and log the response content. I set an order value so the logging advisor runs early in the request chain and late in the response chain. I register it with the ChatClient builder as a default advisor. I avoid logging sensitive user data unless required and ensure logs comply with privacy policies. Real-time example: a compliance team requires an audit trail of AI interactions. I implement a LoggingAdvisor that writes prompt and response to SLF4J with conversation ID. I set order to LOWEST_PRECEDENCE so it wraps the entire chain, and I register it as a default advisor. Audit logs now contain request and response for every AI call.

Variability can come from the embedding model, the retrieval algorithm, the LLM, or the prompt. I fix retrieval by setting deterministic parameters such as a fixed top-K and a consistent similarity threshold. I use a reranker to make scoring more stable. I use a deterministic LLM with temperature set to 0 when consistency is more important than creativity. I version the prompt template and the document corpus so the same query produces the same answer against the same data version. I also add citations so users can verify the source. Real-time example: a support bot gives inconsistent refund policy answers. I set topK=5, temperature=0, add a reranker, and pin the embedding model version. I also include citations in the response. The same question now returns nearly identical answers.

I configure a maximum number of tool call iterations on the ToolCallingAdvisor or in application logic. I improve tool descriptions so the model understands when to stop. I add a guard advisor that detects loops and interrupts them. I ensure tools return concise, complete answers rather than partial data that forces repeated calls. For complex work ows, I break the task into explicit steps instead of relying on the model to coordinate everything through tools. Real-time example: an agent calling searchProduct repeatedly with slightly different keywords. I add a loop limit of 5, update the tool description to say call only once and re ne the query internally, and add an advisor that aborts if the same tool is called with the same arguments twice. The loop stops.

I improve the system prompt to explicitly instruct the model to answer only from the provided context and to say it does not know if the answer is not in the context. I make the retrieved context prominent in the prompt, usually by placing it before the user question. I use a clear delimiter such as "Context:" and "Question:" to help the model distinguish sources. I also reduce the temperature so the model is less creative and more grounded.
