Alternatively, switch to JWT in an HttpOnly cookie or an Authorization header, where CSRF is not relevant.

I check whether the production signing key matches the one used to sign the token. If the token was issued in dev and production uses a different secret, it will fail validation. I verify the token has not expired and that the issuer and audience claims match the production configuration. I also check the clock skew between the issuer and verifier, and I confirm the filter reads the Authorization header correctly without being blocked by a proxy or gateway. The fix is to align key management and claims validation across environments, ideally using a single issuer or public key distribution.

In a stateless JWT system, logout only works if the server keeps a deny list of tokens until they expire. I implement token blacklisting using Redis or a database, storing the token identifier or a hash keyed by expiration time. A custom filter checks every request against this list and rejects blacklisted tokens. Alternatively, I keep access tokens very short-lived and revoke refresh tokens on logout, so the user must re-authenticate when the short token expires. The client must also delete tokens from storage.

A stack trace with secrets means an unhandled exception leaked through the default error response. I add a global @RestControllerAdvice that catches exceptions and returns a sanitized JSON response with only a generic message and a correlation ID. I set server.error.include-stacktrace=never and server.error.include- message=never in production. I ensure no secrets are placed in exception messages. Finally, I review the error logging pipeline to make sure full stack traces go to the centralized logging system, not the API response.

Custom filters must be registered at the correct position in the filter chain using addFilterBefore, addFilterAfter, or addFilterAt. If the filter is added at the wrong position, authentication or authorization may run before it, causing the request to be processed or rejected before your filter executes. Also, the filter must be a Spring bean if it depends on other beans, but it should not be registered as a servlet filter automatically unless you want it to run twice. I verify the filter order in the logs with debug logging enabled and adjust the registration accordingly.

I check the TLS truststore used by the JVM to verify the provider’s new certificate. If the certificate chain is not trusted, the token exchange or userinfo call fails with a certificate validation error. I update the truststore with the new issuer certificates or switch to a well-known CA bundle. I also verify the client ID, client secret, redirect URI, and scopes match the provider’s current configuration. Finally, I confirm that the token issuer and userinfo endpoints have not changed.

If the method is called from another method in the same bean, the Spring proxy is bypassed and the annotation is ignored. This is a classic self-invocation problem. I move the annotated method to another bean and inject it, or use AopContext.currentProxy() to force a proxied call. I also verify that @EnableMethodSecurity is present and that the SpEL expression uses the correct role or authority names, remembering that hasRole adds the ROLE_ prefix.

By default, Spring Security assigns limited authorities based on the OAuth2 provider response. I implement a custom OAuth2UserService that calls Google’s userinfo endpoint and maps returned attributes to local roles stored in the database. For example, I might check a domain, email pattern, or a pre-registered admin list. I then build a new OAuth2User with the mapped GrantedAuthority collection. I also ensure that roles are not hard-coded and can be updated without redeploying the application.

I first check whether validation uses symmetric key signing with a shared secret that requires expensive HMAC operations on every request, and whether the token is parsed from scratch each time. Switching to an asymmetric key pair lets verification services use only the public key, which is faster and safer. I cache the parsed token or the decoded public key. I also verify the token format before parsing to fail fast on malformed tokens. If the bottleneck persists, I move token validation to an API Gateway or sidecar so backend services receive pre-validated claims.

For session-based applications, I invalidate all sessions associated with the user by using Spring Security’s session registry, specifically for concurrent session control. For JWT-based applications, I issue new access and refresh tokens and revoke or blacklist all existing refresh tokens for that user. The client must re-authenticate on other devices. I also add a tokenVersion or passwordChangedAt claim in the JWT and reject tokens issued before the password change.

Token rotation means issuing a new refresh token every time a refresh request is made and invalidating the old one. I update the refresh endpoint to verify the incoming refresh token, delete or mark the stored token as used, and issue a new access token along with a new refresh token. This limits the window in which a stolen refresh token can be used. I also bind refresh tokens to the device fingerprint or a rotating family identifier so that reuse of an old token invalidates the entire family, preventing silent token theft.

The balance is between security and user experience. Access tokens should remain short, for example 5 to 15 minutes, because they travel with every request. Refresh tokens can live longer, for example days or weeks, but must be stored securely and rotated. For mobile apps, using a secure enclave or encrypted storage for the refresh token and implementing silent refresh on 401 responses gives a smooth experience without sacrificing safety. Long-lived access tokens are not the right answer because theft becomes much more dangerous.

I treat the exposed secret as compromised and rotate it immediately. The secret key must be removed from source control and moved to a secret manager such as Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, or Azure Key Vault. For microservices, I recommend switching to asymmetric signing so verification services only need the public key. I add the properties file to .gitignore, scan the repository history for other secrets, and implement pre-commit hooks to prevent future commits of credentials.

This is an insecure direct object reference and missing authorization. The fix is to add an ownership check inside the service layer, for example by comparing the record’s owner to the current principal. Use @PreAuthorize with a SpEL expression that checks the owner, or perform the check in code after loading the entity from the database. Never trust the client to send the correct ID without verifying it against the authenticated user. I also add tests that confirm a user cannot update another user’s records.

I immediately implement rate limiting on the login endpoint by IP address and username using Bucket4j, Resilience4j, or the API Gateway. I add progressive delays and CAPTCHA after repeated failures. I log and alert on attack patterns and consider temporarily blocking IPs at the firewall or WAF level. I verify that passwords are hashed with BCryptPasswordEncoder so offline cracking is slow if the database is breached. I also enable monitoring for credential-stuffing patterns and encourage users with weak or reused passwords to change them.

CORS errors happen when the browser blocks a cross-origin request. I configure CORS in the SecurityFilterChain using a CorsConfigurationSource bean that defines allowed origins, methods, headers, and whether credentials are allowed. I do not use a wildcard origin when credentials are enabled, because that allows any website to make authenticated requests. Instead, I explicitly list trusted origins and keep allowed methods minimal. I also ensure the preflight OPTIONS request is permitted without authentication.

Because JWTs are stateless, I cannot revoke an access token directly unless it is expired. I implement token blacklisting using Redis keyed by token identifier or expiration time, and I update the authentication filter to reject blacklisted tokens. For faster response, I keep access tokens very short-lived and revoke all refresh tokens for the user. I also update the user record to mark the account as locked or disabled so that any new login attempt fails. If the incident is severe, I rotate the signing key, which invalidates all existing tokens across the system.

I distinguish service accounts from human users by claim values such as client_id, sub, or a custom account_type claim. Service accounts get authorities that represent API scopes or permissions, not UI roles. I use @PreAuthorize("hasAuthority('service:invoice:read')") to restrict service endpoints and keep service account tokens short-lived, ideally with a lifetime tied to the request. I also log service-to-service calls separately and monitor for unusual call volumes or scope creep.

The endpoint might be configured in the wrong SecurityFilterChain or the request might be hitting a different base path. If management endpoints are on a separate port or context path, the main SecurityFilterChain may not apply. Another cause is that the request is being rejected by an earlier filter, such as a custom JWT filter that returns 401 for any request without a token before the authorization filter can evaluate permitAll. I debug by enabling Spring Security logging and checking the exact chain that processes the request.

Spring Security supports role hierarchies through a RoleHierarchy bean. I define that ROLE_MANAGER implies ROLE_USER, and then use hasRole("USER") in authorization rules. A manager will automatically be granted the implied authority. This avoids duplicating permission checks across roles and keeps the authority model maintainable. Without role hierarchy, I would have to explicitly check both roles in every rule, which is error-prone.

The best approach is to disable Swagger UI in production using Spring profiles, for example by only enabling the SpringDoc configuration when the dev or qa profile is active. Alternatively, I restrict Swagger endpoints with Spring Security to internal users only. If Swagger is not needed in production at all, I remove the dependency from the production build. I keep OpenAPI spec generation in CI so documentation stays accurate, but the interactive UI is not reachable publicly.

I check whether the deployment changed authority strings or role mappings. For example, a role renamed from ADMIN to ROLE_ADMIN or an authority renamed from user.write to user:create breaks existing @PreAuthorize expressions. I verify the UserDetailsService or token claims builder populates the same authorities the rules expect. I also check whether the endpoints moved to a new path that is no longer covered by the URL authorization rule. Logging the authentication object and the denied request path quickly shows the mismatch.

I configure multiple SecurityFilterChain beans with different request matchers and authentication mechanisms. One chain handles browser users with OAuth2 login or form login and session cookies. Another chain handles API clients with JWT bearer tokens and a stateless session policy. Each chain matches based on path or header patterns. This separation prevents a misconfiguration meant for browser users, such as CSRF protection, from breaking API clients, and vice versa. Both chains still enforce authorization based on roles and authorities.

Testing is important because it catches regressions early, documents expected behavior, and gives confidence during refactoring or production incidents. Spring Boot provides the spring-boot-starter-test starter, which bundles JUnit 5, Mockito, AssertJ, Hamcrest, JSONassert, and JsonPath. It also offers slice test annotations such as @WebMvcTest, @DataJpaTest, and @RestClientTest, which load only the relevant slice of the application context so tests run faster than a full @SpringBootTest. The starter also configures the build tools to separate unit tests from integration tests, which is useful on CI where you want quick feedback before running slower integration suites.

A unit test isolates a single class or method and replaces all collaborators with test doubles, so a failure points directly to the unit under test. An integration test loads part or all of the Spring context and exercises real collaborators such as the database, the web layer, or message brokers. Unit tests are fast, deterministic, and run without a server or database. Integration tests are slower but verify wiring, transaction behavior, serialization, and configuration that unit tests cannot. A healthy project runs unit tests on every commit and integration tests in a separate CI stage or nightly job.

JUnit 5 is the current generation of the JUnit testing framework. It has a modular architecture made of JUnit Platform, which discovers and runs tests; JUnit Jupiter, which provides the modern programming and extension model; and JUnit Vintage, which can run JUnit 3 and 4 tests. Unlike JUnit 4, it uses @Test, @BeforeEach, @AfterEach, @BeforeAll, and @AfterAll annotations, supports parameterized tests, dynamic tests, and a powerful extension API. The extension model replaces JUnit 4 runners and rules, so Spring uses SpringExtension instead of the old SpringRunner.

@BeforeAll runs once before any test in the class and is typically used for expensive setup such as starting a database container. @BeforeEach runs before every test and is used to reset state, create fresh mocks, or reinitialize the object under test. @AfterEach runs after every test to clean up resources like open files or database rows inserted by the test. @AfterAll runs once after all tests complete and is used to stop containers or close connection pools. Static setup methods must be static, which is a common trap when you need shared state across tests.

AssertJ is a fluent assertion library that makes test failures readable and assertions easy to write. Instead of assertEquals(expected, actual), you write assertThat(actual).isEqualTo(expected). It has rich support for collections, exceptions, strings, dates, and JSON, and the failure messages are often clearer than JUnit's defaults. For example, assertThatThrownBy(() -> service.findById(-1)).isInstanceOf(NotFoundException.class).hasMes sageContaining("not found") reads like a sentence and bundles multiple checks. AssertJ also avoids confusion about the expected-versus-actual parameter order, which is a common source of misleading failure messages.

You test the service class directly without loading the Spring context. You create the service instance yourself or use Mockito's @InjectMocks, and you provide its dependencies with @Mock. In the test you arrange the inputs and mock behavior, act by calling the service method, and assert the result or verify interactions. For example, if OrderService depends on OrderRepository, you mock the repository to return an order, call orderService.findById(1L), and assert that the returned order has the expected customer name. Since the Spring context is not loaded, the test starts in milliseconds.

Mockito is a mocking framework that lets you create test doubles for dependencies so you can isolate the class you are testing. It solves the problem of slow or unavailable collaborators such as databases, external REST services, or message queues during unit tests. With Mockito you can define stubbed responses, verify that certain methods were called, and capture the arguments passed to mocked methods. This keeps unit tests focused, fast, and deterministic. A common mistake is mocking everything including simple value objects, which makes tests brittle and less trustworthy.

@Mock creates a fully mocked object where every method returns default values unless stubbed. @Spy creates a partial mock that wraps a real instance and calls real methods by default, unless you stub a specific method. @InjectMocks creates an instance of the class under test and tries to inject mocks or spies into its constructor, fields, or setter methods. @InjectMocks is convenient for service-layer tests, but constructor injection is cleaner because it makes dependencies explicit and avoids reflection-based injection, which can silently fail if field names do not match.

You stub a method with when(mock.method(args)).thenReturn(value), or with the BDD-style given(mock.method(args)).willReturn(value). If the method is called with arguments that do not match any stubbing, Mockito returns the default value for the return type, such as null for objects, 0 for numbers, or false for booleans. This silent default return is a frequent source of confusion because the test may pass even though the mock interaction is wrong. To catch this, use Mockito.strictness(Strictness.STRICT_STUBS), which fails the test if a stubbed mock is called with unexpected arguments or if a stubbed method is never called.

verify() checks that a specific interaction happened with a mock, such as a method being called a certain number of times or with specific arguments. You use it when the side effect of the method under test is more important than the returned value, for example when a service saves a record or sends an email. verify(repository).save(order) confirms that persistence happened, while verify(emailClient, never()).send(any()) confirms that no email was sent for an invalid order. However, overusing verify() on every collaborator makes tests brittle; prefer asserting outcomes and verify only meaningful interactions.

@MockBean is a Spring test annotation that replaces an existing bean in the Spring application context with a Mockito mock. You use it in integration or slice tests where the context is loaded, such as @WebMvcTest or @SpringBootTest, and you need a real controller but a fake service. @Mock is used in plain unit tests where there is no Spring context. A common pitfall is using @MockBean in a class with many tests; it triggers context caching invalidation because Spring sees a modified context, which can slow down the whole suite.

The persistence layer is tested with @DataJpaTest, which configures an in-memory or real database, loads @Entity classes, and creates a default TestEntityManager and repository beans. It does not load the full application context, so web components and services are excluded. You write tests that save entities through the repository and assert that custom query methods return the expected results. Because @DataJpaTest is transactional, each test runs inside a transaction that rolls back automatically, leaving the database clean. For complex queries, an in-memory database may behave differently from production, so many teams switch to Testcontainers with the real database vendor.

Testcontainers is a Java library that starts lightweight, throwaway Docker containers for databases, message brokers, and other services during tests. For repository tests, it lets you run PostgreSQL, MySQL, or Oracle inside a container instead of relying on H2. This eliminates subtle differences in SQL dialect, transaction behavior, and vendor-specific features between the test database and production. The container starts once per test class, tests run against the real database, and the container is stopped after the tests finish. The trade-off is slower startup, but the confidence gained is worth it for queries that use native SQL, JSON columns, or stored procedures.

You define a container as a static field annotated with @Container, start it in @BeforeAll, and set Spring DataSource properties so the test context connects to the running container. With the latest Spring Boot and Testcontainers integration, you can also use @ServiceConnection so Spring Boot automatically resolves the connection details from the container. You add the database driver and Testcontainers dependency, and you may reuse the same container across tests with a singleton pattern or the Testcontainers reuse feature to reduce startup time. The key is to keep container lifecycle separate from the test transaction so the container keeps running while individual tests still roll back.

@WebMvcTest is a slice test annotation that loads only the Spring MVC web layer. It auto-configures MockMvc, registers @Controller and @ControllerAdvice beans, and excludes the full service, repository, and component scan unless explicitly imported. It is ideal for testing REST controllers because you can simulate HTTP requests, validate status codes, response bodies, and exception handling without starting an embedded server. Collaborating services are provided with @MockBean. A common mistake is expecting security filters to be fully configured; while Spring Security filters are registered, authentication must be mocked or disabled depending on the test intent.

MockMvc is a Spring testing utility that dispatches HTTP requests through the DispatcherServlet without starting a real servlet container. It tests controller behavior, binding, validation, exception handling, and filter interactions in a lightweight way. TestRestTemplate and WebTestClient are used with @SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = RANDOM_PORT) to send real HTTP requests to an embedded server. MockMvc is faster and more focused, while the real-client approach verifies the full stack including actual serialization and container filters. For most controller tests, MockMvc is sufficient; reserve the real server approach for integration tests that need end-to-end HTTP semantics.

@SpringBootTest loads the complete Spring Boot application context and is used for full integration tests. Its webEnvironment attribute controls how the web layer is started. MOCK loads a web context but does not start a real server, RANDOM_PORT starts an embedded server on a random port, DEFINED_PORT uses the configured server.port, and NONE does not start a web environment. RANDOM_PORT is the safest choice for integration tests because it avoids port conflicts on CI and lets TestRestTemplate or WebTestClient send real requests. MOCK is useful when you want the web beans configured but prefer MockMvc.

The Spring TestContext framework manages the application context for tests, including dependency injection, transaction management, and context caching. When multiple test classes use the same context configuration, Spring reuses the cached context instead of creating a new one, which saves significant startup time. However, adding or replacing beans with @MockBean, @SpyBean, or different @TestPropertySource values changes the context key and forces a new context to load. A suite that uses many different @MockBean combinations can suffer from context cache pollution and slow builds, so it is worth grouping tests that share the same context.

Asynchronous methods are usually annotated with @Async and executed by a TaskExecutor. In a unit test, you call the method and use CompletableFuture.get() or Future.get() with a timeout to wait for the result. In an integration test, you may inject the executor and wait for active tasks to finish, or use Awaitility to poll until the expected side effect occurs. A common mistake is asserting immediately after the async call, which usually fails because the task has not run yet. You should also test the error path by verifying that exceptions are handled by the returned Future or by an AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler for void async methods.

JaCoCo is a Java code coverage library that instruments bytecode during test execution and reports how much of the code was executed. It measures instruction coverage, branch coverage, line coverage, method coverage, and class coverage. The HTML report shows which lines were covered and which branches were missed, while the XML report is consumed by CI tools like SonarQube. Coverage is a useful signal but not a goal by itself; 100 percent line coverage with weak assertions is misleading because the code can be exercised without being verified. JaCoCo should be paired with meaningful assertions and reviewed alongside the test suite quality.

You configure exclusions in the JaCoCo Maven or Gradle plugin using patterns such as **/config/*, **/dto/**, or **/*Exception. Lombok-generated methods can be excluded with the lombok.addLombokGeneratedAnnotation = true setting, which marks generated code so JaCoCo ignores it. Exclusions are useful for boilerplate such as equals and hashCode, mapping classes, or generated sources, but excluding real business logic to hit a coverage target is a harmful practice. The goal is to reduce noise, not to game the metric.

Parameterized tests let you run the same test logic with multiple sets of inputs. You can use @ValueSource for simple values, @CsvSource for tabular data, @MethodSource for complex objects, or @ArgumentsSource for custom suppliers. They are useful for validation rules, pricing calculations, or any logic that must behave consistently across many cases. For example, a single parameterized test can check that a discount calculator returns the correct amount for customer tiers GOLD, SILVER, and BRONZE. Without parameterized tests, you would copy- paste nearly identical test methods, which is hard to maintain.

@MockBean completely replaces a bean in the Spring context with a Mockito mock, so calls return stubbed values by default. @SpyBean wraps the real bean, so real behavior runs unless a specific method is stubbed. Use @MockBean when the real implementation should not execute, such as an external payment gateway or a repository that is not needed for a controller test. Use @SpyBean when you want to verify that the real bean was called or when you need to override one method of an otherwise real service. Spies are useful but can make tests harder to debug because the real code runs and may trigger unexpected side effects.

Each test should start from a clean, well-defined state. Use the transactional rollback behavior of @Transactional on tests so database changes are undone. For data that must survive across transactions, such as when testing a service that calls @Transactional(propagation = REQUIRES_NEW), use TestEntityManager or a custom cleanup in @AfterEach to delete rows. Avoid relying on data created by a previous test because it makes execution order matter and causes flaky failures. Some teams use SQL scripts or libraries like Flyway for baseline schema and small seed data sets, but test-specific data should be created and cleaned up by the test itself.

I first look at any date or timestamp assertions in the test and in the production code. If the test creates an expected value using LocalDateTime.now() or new Date() and compares it against a value returned by the service, the comparison will fail when CI runs in a different zone or at a different millisecond. The fix is to avoid comparing against wall-clock time in unit tests; instead, inject a Clock bean or use a fixed timestamp provided by the test. For integration tests, I ensure the JVM timezone and database timezone are explicit, for example by setting -Duser.timezone=UTC in the CI pipeline and using TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE columns. I also check whether the test relies on the current date for filtering and replace it with a controlled reference date.

The cause is usually a test that commits data and does not clean up, or a non-transactional operation that leaks state. @DataJpaTest is transactional by default, so most tests roll back, but a test calling a service method annotated with @Transactional(propagation = REQUIRES_NEW) commits in a new transaction and leaves data behind. Another cause is a @Sql script that inserts shared rows without cleanup, or a test that uses native JdbcTemplate without a transaction. I find the offending test by running the suite with logging enabled and inspecting the database state after each test class. The fix is to add explicit cleanup in @AfterEach, use unique identifiers per test, or refactor the production code so tests can rely on automatic rollback.

An empty body usually means the controller returns a response object but it is not serialized, or the mock did not return the expected object. I first verify that the controller method is actually returning the object and that @RestController or @ResponseBody is present. Then I check the mocked service; if when(service.findById(1L)).thenReturn(order) uses a different argument than the controller passes, Mockito returns null and Jackson serializes an empty body. I also check that the object has getters, because Jackson needs them. Finally, I inspect the MockMvc result to see whether an exception was swallowed, and I enable debug logging for org.springframework.web to trace the request through the filters.

UnnecessaryStubbingException is thrown when strict stubbing is enabled and a stubbed interaction was never used during the test. After the refactor, the production code may no longer call the stubbed method, or the test may have stubbed a method that was only relevant to an older implementation. I review the test to remove unused stubs and to confirm that the remaining stubs are actually required by the scenario being tested. If strict stubbing is too noisy for a class with shared setup, I either split the test into smaller focused tests or apply Mockito.lenient() only where a shared stub is acceptable. The goal is to keep the test honest about what it is verifying.

The actuator endpoint is not loaded because @SpringBootTest does not auto- configure actuator unless the actuator starter is on the classpath and the endpoint is enabled. I check that spring-boot-starter-actuator is a dependency and that the management.endpoints.web.exposure.include property includes health. If the test uses webEnvironment = NONE, actuator web endpoints are not available, so I switch to RANDOM_PORT or MOCK. I also verify that the test does not override management.server.port to a fixed port that collides with another test. When actuator is intentionally disabled for a test, I either enable it for that test class or assert against an internal service bean instead of the HTTP endpoint.

I start by moving the container declaration to a base class or a shared utility so the same container instance is reused across multiple test classes. With Testcontainers, I enable the testcontainers.reuse.enable flag in ~/.testcontainers.properties so containers stay alive between test runs during local development. On CI, I ensure Docker layer caching is enabled and that the base image is pulled before the build. I also look for unnecessary @DirtiesContext annotations that force the Spring context to restart. If the suite is still slow, I move the fastest unit-like repository tests to H2 and reserve Testcontainers only for tests that exercise vendor-specific SQL or stored procedures.

The test disabled CSRF to make the mock setup easier, but the developer assumed the controller behavior was the only thing being tested. CSRF is part of Spring Security, and disabling it in tests hides the fact that state-changing browser requests require a valid token. The fix is to configure MockMvc with the same security settings used in production and to include CSRF tokens in state-changing requests using csrf(). For pure API clients using bearer tokens, CSRF is legitimately disabled, but that decision belongs in the production configuration, not hidden in tests. Tests should mirror production security as closely as possible to catch integration issues early.

@MockBean changes the Spring context fingerprint, so every unique combination of mocked beans triggers a new context cache entry. If many test classes mock different sets of beans, the cache fills up and contexts are constantly created and destroyed. I address this by grouping tests that need the same mocks into the same test class or by using @Import to share a common test configuration that declares the mocks once. For unit tests that do not need the Spring context at all, I replace @MockBean with plain Mockito @Mock and remove @SpringBootTest, which eliminates context reloads entirely. When a slice test such as @WebMvcTest is sufficient, I use it instead of the full context.

H2 supports a different SQL dialect than PostgreSQL, and some queries that work in H2 may use syntax, functions, or type casting that PostgreSQL does not accept. Native queries, JSON operators, window functions, and custom types are common sources of mismatch. The fix is to move the test from H2 to a Testcontainers PostgreSQL container so the test database matches production. I also review the query for dialect-specific features and consider using JPQL or the Criteria API for portable logic. If a native query is unavoidable, I write a dedicated integration test against the real database and keep the H2 test only for simple repository methods where dialect differences do not matter.

Testing private methods through reflection is usually not a good practice because it couples the test to internal implementation details and makes refactoring painful. Private methods are tested indirectly by testing the public methods that call them. If the private method is complex enough to need its own tests, that is often a sign that it should be extracted into a separate class with public methods that can be tested directly. The exception is legacy code that cannot be refactored safely; in that case, reflection may be a temporary bridge, but the goal should be to refactor the code rather than maintain reflection-based tests.

Line coverage only measures whether a line was executed, not whether it was verified. A test can execute error-handling code without asserting that the correct exception is thrown, or it can call a method with a weak assertion that misses the bug. Coverage also does not measure edge cases, concurrency, or integration failures. I treat coverage as a hygiene metric, not a quality guarantee. I review tests for meaningful assertions, add mutation testing or property-based testing for critical paths, and run integration tests against realistic data. If a bug escapes, I add a regression test that fails before the fix and passes after it.

Intermittent failures in asynchronous email sending usually come from timing issues. The test asserts before the background thread has finished, or the mock interaction happens on a different thread than the test thread. I stabilize the test by using Awaitility to wait until the mock receives the expected call, or by returning a CompletableFuture from the email service so the test can wait for completion. If the email client runs inside an @Async method, I inject the TaskExecutor and wait for active tasks. I also make the test deterministic by controlling the thread pool or by making the email service synchronous in the test configuration.

Generated code should not count against coverage because it is not hand-written business logic. For Lombok, I add lombok.config with lombok.addLombokGeneratedAnnotation = true so JaCoCo recognizes the @Generated annotation and skips those methods. For MapStruct, I configure the JaCoCo exclusion list to ignore the generated mapper implementation classes, for example with **/mapper/*Impl. I keep the domain and service classes included in coverage so the report reflects actual code quality. If the team uses other generators, I add their output packages to the exclusions and document why those classes are not measured.

Adding Spring Security introduces filters that intercept every request, so unauthenticated requests to protected endpoints return 401 instead of 200. I update the test by configuring MockMvc with the security filters and either providing test credentials or disabling authentication for the test. For @WebMvcTest, I add @AutoConfigureMockMvc(addFilters = false) only if the goal is to test controller logic without security, but I prefer to keep security enabled and use @WithMockUser to simulate an authenticated user. I also add tests that verify the endpoint correctly rejects unauthenticated requests and enforces role-based access, so security behavior is covered, not bypassed.

I start by reading the full stack trace and the caused-by chain, because the root cause is usually buried several levels deep. Common reasons are a missing property, a bean that fails to initialize, a conditional configuration that activates incorrectly under test, or a dependency that requires a real service not available in the test environment. I run a single failing test with debug logging for org.springframework.boot and org.springframework.test to see which bean fails. If the new dependency is not needed for the test, I exclude it with @SpringBootTest(classes = { ... }) or a test-specific profile. If it is needed, I provide a mock or a lightweight implementation with @TestConfiguration.

Generative AI refers to models that can create new content such as text, images, audio, or code by learning patterns from training data. Unlike traditional AI, which is often used for classification, regression, or clustering, generative AI produces outputs that resemble its training distribution. Large language models such as GPT, Gemini, and Claude are generative AI systems trained to predict the next token in a sequence. For backend developers, generative AI enables features like chatbots, code assistants, content generation, and summarization. The key difference is that traditional AI maps inputs to known labels, while generative AI synthesizes novel outputs conditioned on inputs.

A Large Language Model is a deep neural network, typically based on the Transformer architecture, trained on vast amounts of text to predict the next token in a sequence. During training, the model learns statistical relationships between words, grammar, facts, and reasoning patterns. At inference, it takes a prompt, converts it into tokens, processes it through many layers of self-attention and feed-forward networks, and generates tokens one at a time until a stop condition is reached. The model does not truly understand text in a human sense; it predicts likely sequences based on learned patterns. Modern LLMs have billions of parameters and are ne-tuned for instruction following and safety.

Tokenization is the process of splitting text into smaller units called tokens, which can be words, subwords, or characters. LLMs operate on tokens rather than raw characters, so the input text is first converted to tokens, and the model predicts the next token. The number of tokens affects cost, context window usage, and latency. Different models use different tokenizers, so the same sentence may produce different token counts across providers. For backend developers, tokenization matters because APIs charge per token, prompt length is limited by the context window, and output must often be bounded by max tokens.

The Transformer is a neural network architecture introduced in the paper Attention Is All You Need. It is built from encoder and decoder stacks, though modern LLMs typically use decoder-only variants. The core component is the self-attention mechanism, which allows the model to weigh the importance of different tokens in the input when producing each output token. Transformers also use positional encoding to understand token order, feed-forward layers to transform representations, layer normalization, and residual connections. The architecture is highly parallelizable during training and scales well to billions of parameters.

Self-attention is a mechanism that computes a weighted representation of each token by considering all other tokens in the sequence. For every token, the model generates query, key, and value vectors, compares the query with all keys to produce attention scores, normalizes them with softmax, and uses the weighted sum of values as the output. This allows the model to capture long-range dependencies and contextual relationships regardless of token distance. It is important because it replaces sequential processing with parallel computation and gives the model the ability to focus on relevant parts of the input when generating each token.

Embeddings are dense numerical vector representations of text, images, or other data. They map semantic meaning into a high-dimensional space where similar items are closer together. In LLMs, token embeddings represent individual tokens, while sentence or document embeddings capture the meaning of larger text chunks. Embeddings are produced by passing text through an embedding model. They are used for tasks such as semantic search, classification, clustering, recommendation, and retrieval in RAG systems. The quality of embeddings depends on the model and the domain of the training data.

A vector store is a database optimized for storing and searching high-dimensional vectors. Vector search, also called similarity search, nds vectors that are closest to a query vector using distance metrics such as cosine similarity or Euclidean distance. Popular vector stores include pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Milvus, and Redis. They often use approximate nearest neighbor algorithms such as HNSW or IVF to search efficiently at scale. In generative AI applications, vector stores are used to retrieve relevant documents or facts based on semantic similarity, which improves response accuracy beyond what the model memorized during training.

Retrieval Augmented Generation is a pattern that combines information retrieval with text generation. Instead of relying solely on the model's training knowledge, the system retrieves relevant documents from a vector store or database and includes them in the prompt as context. The model then generates an answer grounded in the retrieved content. RAG reduces hallucination, keeps responses up to date, and allows the model to answer questions about private or proprietary data. It is commonly used for enterprise knowledge bases, documentation assistants, and customer support bots.

Naive RAG embeds the user query, performs a single similarity search against a vector store, and injects the top documents into the prompt. It is simple to implement and works well for many cases. Modular RAG breaks the process into con gurable stages such as query transformation, retrieval, document post-processing, and augmentation. It can use query rewriting, compression, translation, multi-query expansion, reranking, and hybrid search. Modular RAG is more exible and accurate but also more complex. Spring AI supports naive RAG through QuestionAnswerAdvisor and modular RAG through RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor.

Spring AI is a framework that brings AI capabilities to Spring applications. It provides a consistent, portable API for working with large language models, embedding models, vector stores, and AI patterns such as RAG and tool calling. It supports multiple model providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, Ollama, Azure OpenAI, and Mistral. Spring AI is designed with the same philosophy as Spring: abstractions over vendor APIs, easy configuration, and integration with Spring Boot. Backend developers can use it to add chat, search, classification, and agentic features to Java applications without writing provider-specific boilerplate.

ChatClient is the high-level uent API in Spring AI for interacting with chat models. It allows developers to build prompts, set model options, attach advisors, register tools, and call or stream responses. It is constructed from a ChatModel bean and configured through the builder. ChatClient abstracts the underlying provider, so switching from OpenAI to Anthropic requires changing configuration rather than application code. It supports synchronous calls with call(), streaming with stream(), structured output with entity(), and content extraction with content().

You add the appropriate Spring AI starter, such as spring-ai-openai-spring-boot-starter, and configure properties like spring.ai.openai.api-key and spring.ai.openai.chat.options.model. Spring Boot auto-configures a ChatModel bean. You then create a ChatClient bean by calling ChatClient.builder(chatModel).build(). You can also set default advisors, default tools, and default options during construction. The ChatClient can be injected into services and used in REST controllers or background jobs. This pattern keeps AI logic inside Spring-managed services and integrates with the rest of the application.

Advisors are interceptors in the ChatClient request and response pipeline. They can modify the request before it reaches the model, process the response after it returns, or implement reusable patterns such as logging, chat memory, RAG retrieval, and tool calling. Advisors implement CallAdvisor or StreamAdvisor and run in a chain ordered by getOrder. Built-in advisors include MessageChatMemoryAdvisor for conversation memory, QuestionAnswerAdvisor for RAG, ToolCallingAdvisor for tool execution, and RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor for modular RAG. Custom advisors allow developers to add cross-cutting concerns like audit logging, prompt injection prevention, or cost tracking.

Both Advisors and Spring AOP interceptors wrap behavior around a core operation. AOP intercepts method calls in the Spring application context, while Spring AI Advisors intercept calls to the language model. AOP is general purpose for business methods; Advisors are specialized for the ChatClient pipeline. In Spring AI, advisors receive ChatClientRequest and ChatClientResponse objects and can update context, modify prompts, or transform responses. They are specific to AI work ows and integrate with the ChatClient builder, whereas AOP interceptors work with any Spring bean method.

Chat memory stores the history of a conversation so the model can maintain context across multiple turns. Without memory, each request is stateless and the model does not remember previous messages. Spring AI provides MessageChatMemoryAdvisor, which stores messages in an in-memory or persistent ChatMemory implementation. The advisor retrieves recent messages and adds them to the prompt. For longer conversations, VectorStoreChatMemoryAdvisor can retrieve semantically relevant past messages from a vector store. A conversation ID is used to scope messages to a specific user session.

Tool calling, also known as function calling, allows an LLM to invoke application methods to retrieve data or perform actions. In Spring AI, tools are defined by methods annotated with @Tool or by implementing ToolCallback. The ChatClient registers tools, and the ToolCallingAdvisor manages the loop: the model decides to call a tool, Spring AI executes the method, and the result is sent back to the model. The loop continues until the model produces a final response. Tool calling enables agents that can access real-time data, query databases, call external APIs, or take actions in the system.

You create a method annotated with @Tool inside a Spring bean and pass the bean to ChatClient.defaultTools. The method should have a descriptive name and parameters, optionally with @ToolParameter annotations to describe them to the model. When the model needs the capability, it requests the tool with JSON arguments. Spring AI deserializes the arguments, invokes the method, and serializes the return value back to the model. Tools should be idempotent when possible and avoid side effects that cannot be undone. Complex tools may also use a ToolCallback implementation for full control over behavior and metadata.

Dynamic tool discovery is a pattern for applications with many tools. Instead of sending all tool definitions to the model on every request, which consumes tokens and may confuse the model, a ToolSearchToolCallingAdvisor indexes tools and exposes a search tool. The model calls the search tool to find relevant tools, and only those tools are added to the context. This reduces token usage and improves accuracy when the application has dozens or hundreds of tools. Tool indexes can be semantic using VectorToolIndex, keyword-based using LuceneToolIndex, or pattern-based using RegexToolIndex.

Structured output is the ability to ask an LLM to return data in a specific format, usually JSON, and map it to a Java object. Spring AI supports this through the entity() method on ChatClient, which parses the response into a POJO using a schema or instructions provided in the prompt. It can also use JSON mode or constrained decoding if the model supports it. Structured output is useful when you need to consume AI output programmatically, such as extracting entities, classifying text, or generating configuration objects. It is more reliable than parsing free-form text.

Query transformers rewrite or expand the user query before retrieval to improve search quality. Examples include RewriteQueryTransformer, which reformulates the query; CompressionQueryTransformer, which shortens conversational queries; TranslationQueryTransformer, which translates the query into another language; and MultiQueryExpander, which generates multiple related queries to broaden retrieval. These transformers help when user queries are ambiguous, conversational, or domain-specific. They are part of the RetrievalAugmentationAdvisor pipeline in Spring AI.

Spring AI supports several vector stores including pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate, Chroma, Milvus, Redis, Neo4j, and Elasticsearch. The choice depends on existing infrastructure, scale, and query requirements. pgvector is convenient when the application already uses PostgreSQL. Pinecone and Weaviate are managed services optimized for vector search. Redis is useful when the application already uses Redis for caching. Milvus and Elasticsearch are suitable for large-scale or hybrid search scenarios. Each has a Spring Boot starter with auto-configuration.

Ingestion typically involves reading documents from files or APIs, splitting them into chunks, computing embeddings for each chunk using an EmbeddingModel, and storing the resulting vectors and metadata in a VectorStore. Chunking strategy matters: too large chunks dilute relevance, while too small chunks lose context. Spring AI provides document readers, transformers, and splitters such as TokenTextSplitter and PagePdfDocumentReader. Metadata such as source, page number, or document type is stored alongside the vector to enable filtering. After ingestion, RAG queries can retrieve the most relevant chunks.

Hallucinations are cases where a language model generates plausible-sounding but incorrect or unsupported information. They happen because models are trained to predict likely tokens, not to verify facts against a knowledge source. RAG reduces hallucinations by grounding the model's response in retrieved documents. The prompt explicitly instructs the model to answer based only on the provided context. If the context does not contain the answer, the model can be instructed to say it does not know. Combining RAG with citations, reranking, and strict prompt instructions further improves factual reliability.

A prompt template is a reusable text pattern with placeholders that are lled at runtime. It allows developers to separate prompt engineering from application code and to inject dynamic values such as user input, retrieved context, or conversation history. Spring AI provides PromptTemplate and a uent prompt builder. Templates improve maintainability because prompts can be adjusted without changing Java code. They also support system messages, user messages, and assistant messages, which help structure the conversation and guide model behavior.

The chatbot is relying on the model's training knowledge, which has a cutoff and may not include the latest company documents. I implement RAG by ingesting the company policy documents into a vector store. I split the documents into chunks, compute embeddings, and store them with metadata such as document name and effective date. I add a QuestionAnswerAdvisor to the ChatClient so each user query retrieves the most relevant policy chunks and includes them in the prompt. I instruct the model to answer only from the provided context and to say it does not know if the answer is not in the documents. I also schedule periodic re-ingestion when policies are updated. Real-time example: an HR chatbot tells users the old remote-work policy. I create a pipeline that loads the latest policy PDFs, splits them into 500-token chunks, embeds them with the default embedding model, and stores them in pgvector. The ChatClient uses QuestionAnswerAdvisor with a filter on the latest policy version. Responses now re ect current rules.

I investigate the retrieval pipeline end to end. I check chunk size and overlap; chunks that are too large dilute relevance, and chunks that are too small lose context. I try different embedding models ne-tuned for the domain. I add query transformation such as query rewriting or multi-query expansion before retrieval. I implement hybrid search combining vector similarity with keyword matching. I add a reranking step using a cross-encoder model that scores retrieved documents against the query. I also verify metadata filters are correct and that the vector store is populated with clean, representative documents. Real-time example: a legal assistant retrieves contracts instead of clauses. I reduce chunk size to 512 tokens with 128 overlap, switch to a domain-specific embedding model, add a keyword boost for clause titles, and use a reranker. Precision improves from 40 percent to 85 percent.

I add a chat memory advisor to the ChatClient. I configure MessageChatMemoryAdvisor with a ChatMemory implementation such as InMemoryChatMemory or a persistent store. I pass a conversation ID with each request so the advisor can retrieve and store messages for that session. For long conversations, I use VectorStoreChatMemoryAdvisor to retrieve semantically relevant past turns instead of sending the entire history. I also set a maximum history length to avoid exceeding the model's context window. Real-time example: a customer support bot forgets the user's order ID after one turn. I add MessageChatMemoryAdvisor to the ChatClient, generate a session ID when the conversation starts, and pass it in the advisors parameter. The bot now references earlier messages and asks clarifying questions only when needed.

I use tool calling to fetch live data. I define a @Tool method such as getOrderStatus(String orderId) that queries the order database or order service. I register the tool with ChatClient.defaultTools. When the user asks about an order, the model calls the tool with the order ID, the application returns the current status, and the model answers in natural language. I combine this with RAG for general policy or product questions. I also add validation to ensure users can only query their own orders, which prevents unauthorized data access. Real-time example: a user asks, Where is my order 12345? The ChatClient recognizes it needs order status, calls getOrderStatus("12345"), which returns {"status":"shipped","eta":"2025-08-10"}, and the model responds: Your order 12345 has been shipped and is expected to arrive on August 10.

I inspect the chat response to see the exact tool name and arguments the model produced. Malformed arguments often happen when the tool description or parameter names are ambiguous. I improve the @Tool method name and add @ToolParameter descriptions so the model understands the expected format. I validate tool inputs and return clear error messages so the model can retry with corrected arguments. I also log every tool invocation and response for debugging. If the issue persists, I switch to a more capable model or use constrained output for critical tools. Real-time example: a weather tool receives dates in different formats. I update the @ToolParameter description to say ISO- 8601 date only, add input validation, and return an error like Invalid date format, expected YYYY-MM-DD when the format is wrong. The model corrects itself on the next call.

I apply the principle of least privilege to tool registration. Only tools that are necessary for the use case are registered with ChatClient. Tools that perform destructive or sensitive operations require authorization checks inside the tool method, verifying the user's identity and permissions. I avoid registering admin-only tools on public-facing chatbots. I also add a SafeGuardAdvisor or custom advisor to review tool calls and block dangerous operations. Tools should be idempotent or reversible when possible, and all invocations should be logged for audit. Real-time example: a support chatbot could accidentally call a deleteUserAccount tool. I remove that tool from the public ChatClient, wrap remaining tools with permission checks that verify the caller is an authorized admin, and log every tool call with user ID and arguments.

I check whether the issue is on the model side or the network side. Streaming from the model returns tokens as they are generated, so latency is expected. Choppy output may be caused by buffering in the web layer or frontend. I ensure the Spring controller returns a Flux and does not collect the entire response before sending. I verify the client consumes the stream correctly with Server-Sent Events or WebFlux. I also check model parameters; some models or configurations buffer more than others. If the backend is buffering, I return the Flux directly and avoid calling block(). Real-time example: a React frontend receives the entire response at once. The Spring controller returns ResponseEntity by calling content(). I change it to return Flux from chatClient.prompt().user(question).stream().content(), and the frontend now shows typing in real time.

I reduce the number of top-K documents retrieved, typically to 3 or 5. I also reduce chunk size so more chunks t within the context window. I add a compression step using a query compressor to remove redundant content. I use a reranker to select only the most relevant chunks before injecting them into the prompt. I can also use a contextual query augmenter that summarizes or filters chunks. If the answer still needs more context, I implement a multi-turn strategy where the model asks follow-up questions to re ne retrieval. Real-time example: a technical documentation assistant retrieves 10 large chunks and over ows the context window. I set topK to 4, reduce chunk size to 300 tokens, and add a reranker. The prompt now ts and the answers remain accurate.

Because Spring AI abstracts the model provider, the main change is dependency and configuration. I replace the OpenAI starter with the Azure OpenAI starter and update properties such as spring.ai.azure.openai.api-key, endpoint, and deployment-name. The ChatClient code, advisors, and tools usually remain unchanged. I verify that model capabilities such as JSON mode or tool calling are supported by the chosen Azure deployment. I also update environment-specific configuration without changing the application logic. This portability is one of the main benefits of using Spring AI. Real-time example: a company moves from OpenAI to Azure OpenAI for enterprise compliance. I change the Maven dependency, update application.yml with the Azure endpoint and deployment name, and rerun tests. The same service now calls Azure without any Java code changes.

I create a class that implements CallAdvisor or StreamAdvisor, depending on whether I need to intercept synchronous or streaming calls. In the around method, I access the ChatClientRequest, log the prompt text and metadata, proceed to the next advisor or model, receive the ChatClientResponse, and log the response content. I set an order value so the logging advisor runs early in the request chain and late in the response chain. I register it with the ChatClient builder as a default advisor. I avoid logging sensitive user data unless required and ensure logs comply with privacy policies. Real-time example: a compliance team requires an audit trail of AI interactions. I implement a LoggingAdvisor that writes prompt and response to SLF4J with conversation ID. I set order to LOWEST_PRECEDENCE so it wraps the entire chain, and I register it as a default advisor. Audit logs now contain request and response for every AI call.

Variability can come from the embedding model, the retrieval algorithm, the LLM, or the prompt. I fix retrieval by setting deterministic parameters such as a fixed top-K and a consistent similarity threshold. I use a reranker to make scoring more stable. I use a deterministic LLM with temperature set to 0 when consistency is more important than creativity. I version the prompt template and the document corpus so the same query produces the same answer against the same data version. I also add citations so users can verify the source. Real-time example: a support bot gives inconsistent refund policy answers. I set topK=5, temperature=0, add a reranker, and pin the embedding model version. I also include citations in the response. The same question now returns nearly identical answers.

I configure a maximum number of tool call iterations on the ToolCallingAdvisor or in application logic. I improve tool descriptions so the model understands when to stop. I add a guard advisor that detects loops and interrupts them. I ensure tools return concise, complete answers rather than partial data that forces repeated calls. For complex work ows, I break the task into explicit steps instead of relying on the model to coordinate everything through tools. Real-time example: an agent calling searchProduct repeatedly with slightly different keywords. I add a loop limit of 5, update the tool description to say call only once and re ne the query internally, and add an advisor that aborts if the same tool is called with the same arguments twice. The loop stops.

I improve the system prompt to explicitly instruct the model to answer only from the provided context and to say it does not know if the answer is not in the context. I make the retrieved context prominent in the prompt, usually by placing it before the user question. I use a clear delimiter such as "Context:" and "Question:" to help the model distinguish sources. I also reduce the temperature so the model is less creative and more grounded. If the model still ignores context, I switch to a model that follows instructions better or ne-tune with examples of grounded answers. Real-time example: a medical assistant answers with generic advice despite retrieved clinical guidelines. I update the system message to "You are a clinical assistant. Answer strictly from the provided guidelines. If the answer is not in the context, say 'I do not have that information.'" and place the context first. The answers now stick to the retrieved documents.

I use streaming to reduce time-to- rst-byte and avoid blocking threads. I configure connection pooling for the model provider HTTP client. I cache similar requests where appropriate. I add rate limiting and backpressure to prevent overwhelming the provider API. For RAG, I precompute embeddings and keep the vector store query fast. I use smaller models for simple tasks and reserve large models for complex reasoning. I also consider running a local model such as Ollama for non-critical traffic to reduce latency and cost. Horizontal scaling of the Spring Boot service behind a load balancer handles concurrent users. Real-time example: a content generation API receives 1000 requests per minute. I enable streaming responses, add a 1000-request-per-minute rate limiter, cache prompts with deterministic parameters, and scale the service to ve replicas. Latency drops and costs remain controlled.

I can use a TranslationQueryTransformer before retrieval if the knowledge base is in one language. If the model itself supports multilingual responses, I detect the language from the user message, add a system instruction to respond in that language, and proceed normally. For RAG, I either translate the query to the document language before retrieval or retrieve documents in the detected language using metadata filters. Spring AI advisors make it easy to add a translation step in the modular RAG pipeline. Real-time example: users ask questions in English, Spanish, and German. I add a language detection step, append "Respond in the same language as the user" to the system prompt, and translate the query to English before vector search because the knowledge base is in English. The model answers in the user's language using retrieved English content.

I implement a CallAdvisor and StreamAdvisor that retrieves the current user identity from the security context and injects it into the prompt or context map. In the advise method, I access the ChatClientRequest, extract the user principal, and either add a system message with user details or update the advisor context with user attributes. The next advisors and the model can use this context. I register the advisor early in the chain so subsequent advisors such as RAG or tool calling can also use the identity. I ensure sensitive user data is handled according to privacy requirements. Real-time example: an enterprise assistant needs to know the user's department to filter documents. I write a UserContextAdvisor that reads the authenticated user from Spring Security, adds a context parameter "department=engineering", and a later RAG advisor uses that parameter to filter vector search results. Responses are personalized and scoped.

I include metadata such as source lename, page number, and URL in the retrieved chunks. In the prompt, I instruct the model to cite the source after each claim using the metadata provided. I use a structured prompt template that includes the context chunks with identifiers such as [1], [2], and asks the model to reference those identifiers. The post-processing step can map identifiers back to full source URLs and display them to the user. Spring AI's document metadata ows through the advisors, so the model has access to the citation fields. Real-time example: a documentation assistant needs to show source links. Each chunk has metadata {"source":"/docs/spring-ai.md","page":42}. The prompt includes these identifiers. The model answers: "Spring AI supports multiple model providers [1]." The frontend maps [1] to the URL and displays it as a clickable citation.

Under load, the issue may be context window over ow due to long prompts, concurrency limits on the model provider, thread exhaustion in the application, or cache inconsistency. I check the prompt size and reduce retrieved context or chat history. I monitor provider rate limits and errors. I verify that the Spring Boot application uses reactive or properly pooled resources and does not block event loops. I also check whether the model temperature or sampling parameters change behavior at scale if requests are batched differently. I add retries with backoff for transient provider errors and circuit breakers for provider outages. Real-time example: during a product launch, an AI summary endpoint returns garbled text. Investigation shows the vector store query times out under load and returns empty results, so the model answers without context. I add a cache for common queries, scale the vector store, and add a fallback that returns a default message instead of calling the model with no context.

I implement an evaluation pipeline that compares generated answers against reference answers or ground truth. Metrics include relevance, faithfulness to retrieved context, answer correctness, and hallucination detection. I use LLM-based evaluators that score responses with structured output. I also collect user feedback with thumbs up or thumbs down and store it for analysis. I run evaluations on a held-out test set before deploying prompt or model changes. Spring AI provides evaluation utilities, and custom advisors can log inputs and outputs for offline analysis. Real-time example: a legal Q&A system needs to verify answer quality. I build a batch evaluator that sends 500 known questions to the chatbot, compares answers to reference answers using an LLM judge, and reports precision, recall, and faithfulness scores. Changes that drop scores below a threshold are blocked from deployment.

I use Ollama or a similar local model server. I add the spring-ai-ollama-spring-boot-starter dependency and configure spring.ai.ollama.base-url and spring.ai.ollama.chat.options.model. Spring AI abstracts the provider, so ChatClient code remains the same. The local model runs on company infrastructure, keeping data on premises. This is useful for regulated data or cost control. The trade-off is that local models may be less capable than large cloud models, so they work best for simpler tasks or as a fallback. I also ensure the hardware has sufficient GPU or CPU resources to run the model efficiently. Real-time example: a healthcare company cannot send patient data to cloud LLMs. I deploy a Llama model with Ollama on an on-premise GPU server, configure Spring AI to point to the local Ollama endpoint, and use the same RAG and tool-calling advisors. Patient data never leaves the data center.

Practice

Follow-up Traps

These are the questions where a confident first answer invites a chain of follow-ups. Interviewers use them to see how deep your understanding actually goes — rehearse the whole chain, not just the opener.

"How does HashMap work internally?"

  1. Opener: You explain buckets, hashCode(), and equals().
  2. Follow-up 1: "What happens when two keys hash to the same bucket?" — Collision handling: a linked list of entries, which Java 8+ converts to a red-black tree once a bucket holds more than 8 entries (treeify threshold), turning O(n) worst case into O(log n).
  3. Follow-up 2: "What happens during resize?" — When size exceeds capacity × load factor (default 0.75), the table doubles and every entry is rehashed into new bucket positions — an O(n) operation, which is why pre-sizing a HashMap when you know the count helps performance.
  4. Follow-up 3: "Is HashMap thread-safe? What breaks if two threads resize concurrently?" — No. Concurrent structural modification (like simultaneous resizes) can corrupt the bucket's linked list into a cycle, causing an infinite loop on get() in older JDKs. Use ConcurrentHashMap instead.

"What does the volatile keyword do?"

  1. Opener: Guarantees visibility of writes across threads by preventing caching in CPU registers/thread-local memory.
  2. Follow-up 1: "Does volatile make a compound operation like count++ thread-safe?" — No. Visibility isn't atomicity; count++ is read-modify-write, and two threads can still interleave those three steps. You'd need AtomicInteger or synchronization.
  3. Follow-up 2: "So when is volatile actually enough on its own?" — For simple flags (e.g., a boolean `running` a worker thread polls to stop), where only one thread ever writes and others only read.
  4. Follow-up 3: "How does volatile relate to the Java Memory Model's happens-before relationship?" — A write to a volatile field happens-before every subsequent read of that field, which also makes all writes that happened before the volatile write visible to the reading thread — this is how double-checked locking singleton patterns rely on volatile.

"Why is String immutable in Java?"

  1. Opener: Enables safe sharing via the string pool, thread-safety by default, and safe use as a HashMap key.
  2. Follow-up 1: "If Strings are immutable, why does `str = str + "x"` appear to modify it?" — It doesn't; it creates a new String object and rebinds the reference `str` to point to it. The original object is unchanged and becomes eligible for garbage collection if nothing else references it.
  3. Follow-up 2: "Why is that expensive in a loop, and what's the fix?" — Each concatenation allocates a new object, making an n-iteration loop O(n²) in the worst case. StringBuilder mutates an internal char buffer instead, making it O(n).
  4. Follow-up 3: "How would you make your own class immutable like String?" — Make fields private and final, provide no setters, don't expose mutable internal state directly (return defensive copies of arrays/collections), and don't allow subclassing (mark the class final) so a subclass can't break the invariant.

"What's the difference between synchronized and ReentrantLock?"

  1. Opener: synchronized is a built-in keyword with automatic lock release; ReentrantLock is an explicit class from java.util.concurrent.locks offering more control.
  2. Follow-up 1: "What can ReentrantLock do that synchronized can't?" — tryLock() with a timeout (avoiding indefinite blocking), interruptible lock acquisition, fairness policies (FIFO ordering of waiting threads), and multiple Condition objects per lock instead of just one implicit monitor.
  3. Follow-up 2: "What's the catch with ReentrantLock?" — You must manually unlock() in a finally block; forgetting to do so causes a permanent deadlock, a mistake synchronized's automatic release makes impossible.
  4. Follow-up 3: "When would you actually reach for ReentrantLock in production code?" — When you need tryLock's non-blocking attempt (e.g., avoiding a deadlock by backing off), or when multiple distinct wait conditions on the same resource make separate Condition objects genuinely useful.

"What's the difference between ArrayList and LinkedList?"

  1. Opener: ArrayList is backed by a resizable array (O(1) random access); LinkedList is a doubly-linked list (O(1) insert/delete at known positions).
  2. Follow-up 1: "In practice, which one should you default to and why?" — ArrayList, almost always. LinkedList's O(1) insertion advantage only applies if you already hold a reference to the node; getting to an arbitrary index is still O(n), and its extra per-node object overhead (two references + object header) usually costs more in real workloads than ArrayList's occasional resize.
  3. Follow-up 2: "What actually happens when an ArrayList resizes?" — A new backing array (typically 1.5x the old capacity) is allocated and the old contents are copied over with System.arraycopy — an O(n) operation, but amortized O(1) per add() across many insertions.

"What is the diamond problem, and how does Java avoid it?"

  1. Opener: Java disallows multiple class inheritance so a class can never inherit the same method from two conflicting parents.
  2. Follow-up 1: "But interfaces can have default methods since Java 8 — doesn't the diamond problem come back?" — Yes, partially. If a class implements two interfaces with the same default method signature, the compiler forces you to explicitly override it and choose (or combine) the implementation — it refuses to guess.
  3. Follow-up 2: "How do you call one specific parent interface's default method inside your override?" — `InterfaceName.super.methodName()` — this is the one case where `super` is qualified with an interface name rather than a class.
Practice

Behavioral Question Bank (STAR)

Prep a real story for each of these prompts using Situation / Task / Action / Result. Edit the fields directly — this page is yours to fill in. Note: since this is a static file, typed content stays only for your current browser session. Copy your finished stories somewhere permanent (Notes app, Google Doc) before you close the tab.

Practice

Mistakes Log

Every time you blank on something in a mock interview, a real interview, or while studying — log it here in one line. This list, re-read the night before an interview, is usually worth more than any question bank. Entries persist only for this browser session, so revisit and re-copy them periodically.

Practice

Day-Before Cram Sheet

Pulls everything rated "shaky" or "weak" from the Q&A bank, plus your STAR stories and mistakes log, into one printable page. Rate questions in the Interview Q&A tab first (each card has a confidence rating at the bottom), then come back here.

Built from your Backend Notes PDF · content preserved, presentation refreshed.
